title: Left Adrenal Hematoma – Factor V Leiden
caption: A 51-year-old patient presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain. Axial unenhanced CT (A) and axial contrast-enhanced CT (B) demonstrate a well-circumscribed, rounded 3.2 × 4.2 cm mass involving the left adrenal gland with attenuation of 51 HU and no significant post-contrast enhancement, suggestive of hematoma. Over the following 3 months the patient developed adrenal insufficiency (fatigue, abdominal pain, weakness). Workup for adrenal bleeding showed a heterozygous factor V Leiden mutation. Axial unenhanced CT (C) and coronal reformatted contrast-enhanced CT (D) at 3-month follow-up showed interval resolution of the hematoma with diffuse left adrenal gland thickening (arrow).

mcqs.q: On the unenhanced CT, the left adrenal mass measures 51 HU and shows no post-contrast enhancement. What does this attenuation/enhancement pattern most strongly suggest?
mcqs.exp: A rounded adrenal mass with high unenhanced attenuation (40–60+ HU) that shows NO enhancement after contrast is the classic signature of a hematoma. Lack of enhancement excludes a solid enhancing tumor, and the high density excludes a lipid-rich adenoma (<10 HU) or myelolipoma (macroscopic fat, negative HU).
mcqs.hint: High density + absolutely no enhancement = blood, not tumor.

mcqs.q: The absence of significant post-contrast enhancement is critical here mainly because it helps exclude which entity?
opts: A solid enhancing neoplasm such as carcinoma or metastasis
mcqs.exp: Enhancement implies vascularized solid tissue. A non-enhancing high-density lesion argues against a solid tumor (carcinoma, metastasis, pheochromocytoma) and supports hematoma. Follow-up showing resolution confirms the benign hemorrhagic nature.
mcqs.hint: Tumors take up contrast; clot does not.

mcqs.q: This patient later developed fatigue, weakness and abdominal pain. In the setting of adrenal hemorrhage, these symptoms indicate which complication?
mcqs.exp: Bilateral or extensive adrenal hemorrhage can destroy cortical tissue and cause primary adrenal insufficiency (Addisonian symptoms: fatigue, weakness, abdominal pain, hypotension). Recognizing this triad after adrenal bleeding is key to avoiding a missed adrenal crisis.
mcqs.hint: Destroyed cortex means it can no longer make its hormones.

mcqs.q: The underlying cause found on workup was a heterozygous factor V Leiden mutation. This predisposes to adrenal hemorrhage through which mechanism?
opts: A hypercoagulable state causing adrenal vein thrombosis and venous infarction
Direct tumor invasion of adrenal vessels
mcqs.exp: Factor V Leiden causes resistance to activated protein C and a hypercoagulable state. Adrenal vein thrombosis leads to venous congestion, hemorrhagic infarction and gland destruction — a recognized non-traumatic cause of adrenal hemorrhage.
mcqs.hint: It is a clotting disorder, not a bleeding one — the vein clots off.

mcqs.q: At 3-month follow-up the hematoma resolved, leaving diffuse adrenal gland thickening. What is the appropriate interpretation of this evolution?
opts: Expected benign evolution of a resolving hematoma
mcqs.exp: Hematomas typically shrink and resolve over weeks to months, sometimes leaving residual gland thickening, calcification or a pseudocyst. Documented interval resolution on follow-up imaging is the definitive confirmation of a benign hemorrhage rather than a neoplasm.
mcqs.hint: Getting smaller over time is reassuring, not worrying.

title: Bilateral Adrenal Burkitt Lymphoma
caption: A 70-year-old patient with a history of Burkitt lymphoma presented with adrenal insufficiency due to lymphomatous involvement of the bilateral adrenal glands. Axial unenhanced CT (A), axial (B) and coronal (C) enhanced CT demonstrate bilateral enhancing adrenal masses (arrows).

mcqs.q: A patient with known Burkitt lymphoma has bilateral enhancing adrenal masses and adrenal insufficiency. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: Bilateral enhancing adrenal masses in a patient with known lymphoma most likely represent secondary adrenal involvement. Adrenal lymphoma (usually non-Hodgkin/aggressive B-cell types such as Burkitt) is frequently bilateral.
mcqs.hint: Known lymphoma + bilateral soft-tissue adrenal masses.

mcqs.q: Bilateral adrenal disease that destroys enough cortex produces which clinical syndrome, as seen in this patient?
mcqs.exp: When both glands are extensively replaced by tumor, cortical hormone production fails, causing primary adrenal insufficiency. Adrenal lymphoma is a classic infiltrative cause of bilateral adrenal enlargement with hypoadrenalism.
mcqs.hint: Bilateral gland destruction → the glands cannot make cortisol.

mcqs.q: Which imaging feature favors lymphoma over a lipid-rich adenoma for these adrenal masses?
opts: Soft-tissue attenuation with homogeneous enhancement and bilaterality
mcqs.exp: Lymphoma appears as homogeneous soft-tissue-density, enhancing tissue and is often bilateral. Adenomas are typically unilateral, low attenuation (<10 HU) from intracytoplasmic lipid, and show signal drop on chemical-shift MRI — none of which apply here.
mcqs.hint: Adenomas are fatty and low-density; lymphoma is solid soft tissue.

mcqs.q: Bilateral adrenal masses have a broad differential. Which of the following is NOT a typical cause of bilateral adrenal enlargement?
mcqs.exp: Bilateral adrenal enlargement is classically caused by metastases, lymphoma, infection (TB, fungal), hemorrhage, and hyperplasia. A simple cortical cyst is a unilateral focal benign lesion, not a cause of bilateral infiltrative enlargement.
mcqs.hint: Which one is a single benign fluid lesion, not an infiltrative process?

mcqs.q: What is the most reliable way to confirm secondary adrenal lymphoma when imaging is suggestive?
opts: Image-guided biopsy / correlation with known lymphoma
mcqs.exp: Tissue diagnosis (biopsy) in the context of known lymphoma confirms adrenal involvement. Hormonal tests (metanephrines, ARR, dexamethasone suppression) evaluate functional tumors, not infiltrative lymphoma.
mcqs.hint: Infiltrative tumor is confirmed with a needle, not a hormone assay.

title: Bilateral Adrenal Metastases – Lung Cancer
caption: A 76-year-old patient with adenocarcinoma of the lung and metastases to the bilateral adrenal glands, kidneys, bones and mesentery presented with adrenal insufficiency. Axial enhanced CT (A) demonstrates bilateral adrenal masses (arrows), with the right adrenal mass indenting the right kidney collecting system. PET/CT (B) demonstrates increased FDG uptake in the bilateral adrenal glands (arrows). Biopsy of the right adrenal gland showed metastatic non–small cell carcinoma.

mcqs.q: In a patient with lung adenocarcinoma, bilateral FDG-avid adrenal masses are most consistent with which diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: The adrenal glands are a common site for metastasis, especially from lung cancer. Bilateral masses that are FDG-avid on PET in a patient with known primary malignancy strongly indicate metastatic disease, confirmed here by biopsy.
mcqs.hint: Known lung primary + bilateral hot masses on PET.

mcqs.q: On FDG-PET/CT, which quality of a lipid-poor adrenal lesion best distinguishes a metastasis from a benign adenoma?
opts: FDG uptake greater than liver background
mcqs.exp: Malignant adrenal lesions typically show FDG uptake exceeding hepatic background, whereas most benign adenomas show uptake at or below liver. FDG avidity greater than liver raises concern for metastasis.
mcqs.hint: Compare lesion brightness to the liver.

mcqs.q: Why is FDG-PET particularly useful in this patient rather than CT washout alone?
opts: It provides whole-body staging and functional characterization of indeterminate lesions
It replaces the need for biopsy in all cases
mcqs.exp: In a cancer patient, PET/CT both characterizes the adrenal lesions functionally and stages the rest of the body (here also kidneys, bones, mesentery). It complements CT washout when adrenal lesions are indeterminate.
mcqs.hint: PET sees the whole body and metabolic activity at once.

title: Lipid-Poor Adenoma – CT Washout
caption: A 53-year-old woman with a 14-year history of hypertension requiring multiple medications and hypokalemia with muscle weakness and fatigue was referred while being treated for breast cancer; primary hyperaldosteronism was suspected. Axial unenhanced CT (A) shows a well-circumscribed 1.5-cm oval right adrenal mass with attenuation of 19 HU. Contrast-enhanced portovenous phase (B) shows attenuation of 106 HU. Delayed 15-minute CT (C) shows washout to 42 HU, yielding an absolute enhancement washout of 74%, characteristic of a lipid-poor adenoma (arrow).

mcqs.q: This right adrenal mass measured 19 HU on unenhanced CT. Why can it NOT be called a lipid-rich adenoma on the unenhanced scan alone?
opts: Because 19 HU is above the ≤10 HU threshold for lipid-rich adenoma
Because it is on the right side
mcqs.exp: A lipid-rich adenoma is diagnosed when unenhanced attenuation is ≤10 HU (specificity ~98%). At 19 HU this lesion is 'lipid-poor' and indeterminate on the unenhanced scan, so a washout study is needed.
mcqs.hint: The magic number for a lipid-rich adenoma is 10 HU or less.

mcqs.q: Using unenhanced 19 HU, portovenous 106 HU, and 15-minute delayed 42 HU, the absolute percentage washout (APW) is 74%. How is APW calculated?
mcqs.exp: APW = (enhanced HU − delayed HU) / (enhanced HU − unenhanced HU) × 100 = (106−42)/(106−19) ×100 ≈ 74%. It requires the unenhanced value; relative washout (RPW) omits it.
mcqs.hint: The denominator uses the unenhanced value; the numerator is the drop from peak.

mcqs.q: An absolute washout of 74% on the 15-minute delayed study indicates what?
mcqs.exp: APW ≥60% (or RPW ≥40%) at 15 minutes indicates rapid contrast washout typical of an adenoma. Adenomas—even lipid-poor ones—wash out contrast quickly, whereas malignancies and pheochromocytomas retain contrast (low washout).
mcqs.hint: Fast washout = benign adenoma; slow retention = worry.

title: Bilateral ACTH-Driven Hyperplasia (Situs Inversus)
caption: A 43-year-old woman with situs inversus totalis and a history of Cushing disease due to a pituitary macroadenoma resected 7 years previously was referred for recurrent hypercortisolism. Axial contrast-enhanced CT (A) and coronal reformatted CT (B) show bilateral diffuse thickening of the adrenal glands (arrows), consistent with bilateral ACTH-driven adrenal hyperplasia.

mcqs.q: Bilateral diffuse (symmetric) adrenal thickening in a patient with recurrent Cushing disease is best explained by which mechanism?
opts: Autonomous cortisol from an adrenal adenoma
mcqs.exp: Excess ACTH (here from recurrent pituitary Cushing disease) diffusely stimulates both adrenal cortices, producing bilateral symmetric gland thickening. This is ACTH-dependent hyperplasia, distinct from a solitary autonomous adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Too much pituitary ACTH stimulates BOTH glands equally.

mcqs.q: In ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome, what is the classic morphology of the adrenal glands on CT?
opts: A single fatty nodule with one atrophic gland
Bilateral, diffuse, relatively symmetric enlargement (may become nodular)
mcqs.exp: ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome causes bilateral diffuse enlargement that over time can appear nodular (multinodular hyperplasia). In contrast, a cortisol-secreting adenoma produces one nodule with atrophy of the contralateral gland.
mcqs.hint: Central (pituitary) drive stimulates both glands together.

mcqs.q: The incidental finding of situs inversus totalis in this case is important chiefly because it:
opts: Reverses the normal left–right position of abdominal organs, which the radiologist must account for
mcqs.exp: Situs inversus totalis mirror-reverses thoracoabdominal organ position. It is an anatomical variant unrelated to the adrenal pathology, but the interpreter must recognize the transposed anatomy to avoid errors in localization.
mcqs.hint: It is about organ position (mirror image), not about the adrenal disease itself.

title: ACTH-Independent Macronodular Hyperplasia (PPNAD/BMAH)
caption: Teaching figure on Cushing syndrome (CS) subtypes. ACTH-dependent CS presents with bilateral diffuse uniform adrenal enlargement that becomes nodular over time (multinodular hyperplasia); large autonomous nodules constitute massive macronodular hyperplasia. ACTH-independent CS can result from an adrenal adenoma, carcinoma, primary pigmented nodular adrenocortical disease (PPNAD) or ACTH-independent macronodular adrenocortical hyperplasia (bilateral macronodular adrenal hyperplasia). Adenomas account for ~60% of adrenal-related CS cases; adrenocortical carcinoma accounts for the remaining ~40%. Axial CT panels (A–C) illustrate bilateral macronodular adrenal enlargement.

mcqs.q: Bilateral macronodular adrenal hyperplasia (BMAH) is a cause of which type of Cushing syndrome?
mcqs.exp: BMAH (ACTH-independent macronodular adrenocortical hyperplasia) autonomously secretes cortisol independent of ACTH; plasma ACTH is suppressed. It is classed with adenoma, carcinoma and PPNAD as an adrenal (ACTH-independent) cause of Cushing syndrome.
mcqs.hint: The adrenals act on their own; the pituitary ACTH is low/suppressed.

mcqs.q: Among adrenal (ACTH-independent) causes of Cushing syndrome, adenomas account for roughly what proportion, with carcinoma making up most of the rest?
mcqs.exp: Per this teaching figure, adenomas cause ~60% of adrenal-related Cushing syndrome and adrenocortical carcinoma ~40%. This underlines that a cortisol-secreting adrenal mass carries a meaningful risk of malignancy.
mcqs.hint: Roughly a 60/40 split favoring adenoma.

mcqs.q: PPNAD (primary pigmented nodular adrenocortical disease) is characteristically associated with which syndrome?
mcqs.exp: PPNAD is a hallmark of Carney complex (PRKAR1A mutations), featuring small pigmented adrenal micronodules with internodular atrophy. It causes ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome, often in young patients.
mcqs.hint: Think 'complex' with myxomas, lentigines and endocrine tumors.

mcqs.q: How does ACTH-dependent hyperplasia typically differ morphologically from ACTH-independent macronodular hyperplasia early on?
opts: ACTH-dependent is diffuse/uniform initially; ACTH-independent BMAH shows large autonomous nodules
mcqs.exp: ACTH-dependent hyperplasia begins as diffuse uniform enlargement (later nodular). BMAH characteristically shows multiple large (macro) nodules distorting both glands, with suppressed ACTH.
mcqs.hint: Diffuse and uniform vs. big lumpy autonomous nodules.

mcqs.q: In a patient with ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome and a unilateral solid adrenal mass, which feature would raise concern for adrenocortical carcinoma rather than adenoma?
opts: Large size (>4 cm), heterogeneity, irregular margins and low washout
mcqs.exp: Adrenocortical carcinoma tends to be large (>4 cm), heterogeneous (necrosis/hemorrhage/calcification), with irregular margins and poor washout. Small, homogeneous, low-attenuation lesions with fast washout favor a benign adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Big, ugly and slow-washout points to cancer.

title: Adrenal Incidentaloma Work-up Algorithm
caption: Algorithm for the incidental adrenal mass >1 cm. Typically benign: lipid-rich adenoma (<10 HU or signal drop on MR), myelolipoma (macroscopic fat), cyst (water density, no enhancement), benign calcification (coarse, curvilinear, septal). Indeterminate 1–4 cm with NCCT >10 HU: if washout or signal drop present, lipid-poor adenoma (if >4 cm probably adenoma, follow up 6–12 months); if no washout/no signal drop, consider pheochromocytoma, adrenocortical carcinoma, metastasis or lipid-poor adenoma (imaging follow-up, biopsy, PET/CT or resection depending on scenario). Indeterminate ≥4 cm: no cancer history → consider resection; cancer history → biopsy or PET/CT.

mcqs.q: On unenhanced CT, what attenuation threshold defines a 'typically benign' lipid-rich adenoma per this algorithm?
mcqs.exp: An unenhanced attenuation <10 HU (or signal drop on chemical-shift MRI) reliably indicates a lipid-rich adenoma. This single measurement can characterize the majority of adrenal incidentalomas as benign.
mcqs.hint: Single digit and one zero.

mcqs.q: An incidentaloma measures NCCT >10 HU but shows adequate contrast washout (or signal drop on MR). What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: A lesion >10 HU that nevertheless demonstrates rapid washout (or out-of-phase signal drop) is characteristic of a lipid-poor adenoma. Washout kinetics rescue the diagnosis when the unenhanced density is indeterminate.
mcqs.hint: High density but washes out fast = still an adenoma, just lipid-poor.

mcqs.q: For an indeterminate lesion >10 HU with NO washout and NO signal drop, which diagnoses must be considered?
opts: Pheochromocytoma, adrenocortical carcinoma, metastasis, or lipid-poor adenoma
mcqs.exp: Absence of washout/signal drop keeps the differential open: pheochromocytoma, ACC, metastasis and some lipid-poor adenomas. Further workup (biochemistry, PET/CT, biopsy, or resection) is guided by the clinical scenario.
mcqs.hint: No washout keeps the 'worrisome' list in play.

mcqs.q: For an indeterminate adrenal mass ≥4 cm in a patient with NO history of cancer, what does the algorithm recommend?
mcqs.exp: A large (≥4 cm) indeterminate mass carries a higher risk of adrenocortical carcinoma. In a patient without known cancer, resection is favored. In a patient WITH cancer history, biopsy or PET/CT is used to determine if it is a metastasis.
mcqs.hint: Big and indeterminate without a known primary — take it out.

mcqs.q: Before any adrenal biopsy or resection of an indeterminate mass, which condition must be biochemically excluded to avoid a life-threatening crisis?
mcqs.exp: Pheochromocytoma must be excluded with plasma/urinary metanephrines before biopsy or surgery, because manipulation can precipitate a catecholamine crisis. This is a mandatory safety step in the incidentaloma workup.
mcqs.hint: Never stick a needle in a catecholamine bomb without checking first.

title: Adrenal Gland Gross Anatomy (Anterior View)
caption: Classic anatomical illustration of the adrenal (suprarenal) glands, anterior view. The right gland is pyramidal and shows a hepatic area, an area in contact with the inferior vena cava, and the suprarenal vein. The left gland is semilunar/crescentic and shows gastric and pancreatic contact areas and its suprarenal vein. Note the short right suprarenal vein draining directly into the IVC and the longer left suprarenal vein draining into the left renal vein.

mcqs.q: The right adrenal (suprarenal) vein characteristically drains directly into which structure?
mcqs.exp: The short right suprarenal vein drains directly into the IVC, whereas the longer left suprarenal vein drains into the left renal vein. This asymmetry makes right adrenal vein cannulation technically harder during adrenal venous sampling.
mcqs.hint: The right one takes the short route to the big vein behind it.

mcqs.q: The left adrenal vein typically drains into which vessel?
mcqs.exp: The left suprarenal vein is longer and drains into the left renal vein (often with the inferior phrenic vein). Knowing this venous anatomy is essential for adrenal venous sampling in primary aldosteronism.
mcqs.hint: Left gland drains via the nearby renal vein, not straight to the IVC.

mcqs.q: Based on shape, how are the right and left adrenal glands classically described?
mcqs.exp: The right adrenal is typically pyramidal (triangular) and the left is crescentic/semilunar. On axial CT the glands show an inverted-Y, V or T configuration with limbs no thicker than the adjacent diaphragmatic crus.
mcqs.hint: One is a triangle/pyramid, the other a crescent moon.

title: Unlabelled Case – Axial T2 MRI, Left Upper Abdomen

descriptive.q: Describe the imaging modality and sequence shown, and state how you can tell.
descriptive.model: This is an axial abdominal MR image with fluid (CSF, gallbladder) appearing bright and a large field of view — features of a T2-weighted sequence. On T2, water/fluid is high signal and this helps characterize cystic versus solid adrenal lesions. Confirm by looking at the CSF in the spinal canal, which is bright on T2.

descriptive.q: Systematically describe the location, borders, size and internal signal of the dominant abnormality, and where the adrenal glands should be sought.
descriptive.model: The adrenal glands lie supero-medial to each kidney, between the crus of the diaphragm and the upper renal pole — the right behind the IVC and posterior to the liver, the left lateral to the aorta and behind the pancreas/stomach. Describe any lesion by side, size, margin (smooth vs irregular), homogeneity and T2 signal (bright = cystic/fluid or pheochromocytoma 'light-bulb'; intermediate = solid).

descriptive.q: Based on T2 signal alone, list the differential considerations and the next best sequences to characterize an adrenal mass.
descriptive.model: A markedly T2-hyperintense adrenal lesion suggests a cyst, or a pheochromocytoma (classically 'light-bulb bright'). An intermediate-signal solid lesion suggests adenoma, metastasis or carcinoma. The key next step is chemical-shift (in-phase/opposed-phase) imaging: signal drop on opposed-phase confirms intracytoplasmic lipid of a lipid-rich adenoma. Post-contrast dynamic imaging and biochemical testing complete the workup.

title: Unlabelled Case – Post-Contrast MRI, Left Adrenal Region

descriptive.q: Describe what you see, including the modality and the location and enhancement pattern of any lesion.
descriptive.model: Axial abdominal MR image. Identify the left adrenal region lateral to the aorta and behind the stomach/pancreas. Describe any nodular lesion there: its size, margins, and whether it enhances (heterogeneous or homogeneous). Note that enhancement indicates vascularized solid tissue, arguing against a simple cyst.

descriptive.q: What role does chemical-shift (in/opposed-phase) MRI play in characterizing this adrenal lesion?
descriptive.model: Chemical-shift MRI exploits the different resonance of fat and water protons. A lipid-rich adenoma loses signal on opposed-phase images relative to in-phase (India-ink/etching artifact at organ boundaries confirms opposed-phase). Absence of signal drop suggests a lipid-poor lesion (adenoma, metastasis, pheochromocytoma, carcinoma), prompting washout CT, biochemistry or further workup.

descriptive.q: Outline the biochemical work-up that should accompany any newly detected adrenal mass before intervention.
descriptive.model: Every incidentaloma needs functional screening: (1) 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test for autonomous cortisol; (2) plasma/urinary metanephrines to exclude pheochromocytoma before any biopsy or surgery; (3) aldosterone-to-renin ratio if hypertensive/hypokalemic. Excluding a pheochromocytoma is essential to avoid a catecholamine crisis during intervention.

title: Unlabelled Case – Contrast CT, Right Adrenal Low-Density Lesion

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed lesion: which gland, its shape, margins and attenuation relative to surrounding tissue.
descriptive.model: The arrow indicates a well-circumscribed, rounded low-attenuation lesion in the right adrenal gland (behind the IVC, posterior to the liver). Describe it as smoothly marginated and hypodense relative to solid organs — features that favor a benign process such as a cyst or lipid-rich adenoma. Note that this is a contrast-enhanced image, so a true unenhanced HU cannot be quoted.

descriptive.q: Why is an unenhanced CT (or washout study) needed here rather than relying on this contrast-enhanced image alone?
descriptive.model: Attenuation thresholds for a lipid-rich adenoma (≤10 HU) are validated only on UNENHANCED CT. On a contrast-enhanced scan you cannot apply the 10-HU rule. If the lesion is indeterminate, obtain a dedicated adrenal protocol: unenhanced, portovenous (60–70 s) and 15-minute delayed images to calculate absolute/relative washout.

descriptive.q: List features that would reassure you the lesion is a simple cyst versus features that would prompt further work-up.
descriptive.model: Reassuring (cyst): water attenuation (near 0 HU), thin/imperceptible wall, no enhancement, no soft-tissue nodularity. Concerning: thick or nodular enhancing wall, internal septations with enhancement, high attenuation, or interval growth — these warrant washout CT/MRI, biochemical testing, or resection.

title: Unlabelled Case – Unenhanced CT with ROI, Left Adrenal Nodule (0.7 HU)

descriptive.q: A region-of-interest (ROI) measurement over the left adrenal nodule reads 0.7 HU. Interpret this value.
descriptive.model: An unenhanced attenuation of ~0.7 HU is well below the 10-HU threshold, diagnostic of a lipid-rich adenoma (abundant intracytoplasmic lipid). A value this low is essentially conclusive — specificity approaches 98% at ≤10 HU — and no further characterization is usually required for the benign nature.

descriptive.q: Explain how the ROI should be placed to obtain a reliable adrenal attenuation measurement.
descriptive.model: Place the ROI over at least two-thirds of the lesion, on the unenhanced series, avoiding calcification, macroscopic fat, the margin, and adjacent structures. Record the mean HU. A large representative ROI reduces sampling error; a very small ROI (as here, ~0.8 cm²) can be susceptible to partial-volume effects, so correlate with the lesion size.

descriptive.q: Given a benign lipid-rich adenoma on imaging, what determines whether it still requires clinical follow-up or intervention?
descriptive.model: Even a definite adenoma requires biochemical evaluation for autonomous hormone secretion (cortisol via dexamethasone suppression; aldosterone if hypertensive/hypokalemic; metanephrines to exclude pheochromocytoma). A non-functioning, imaging-benign adenoma generally needs no size follow-up per recent guidelines, whereas a functioning or enlarging lesion may warrant surgery.

title: Unlabelled Case – Unenhanced CT with ROI, Right Adrenal Nodule (18 HU)

descriptive.q: The ROI over the right adrenal nodule measures ~18 HU on unenhanced CT. Interpret and give the next step.
descriptive.model: At ~18 HU the lesion is above the 10-HU cutoff, so it is indeterminate ('lipid-poor') and cannot be called a benign lipid-rich adenoma on the unenhanced scan alone. The next step is a washout study (unenhanced, portovenous, 15-minute delayed) — a lipid-poor adenoma will show high washout (APW ≥60% / RPW ≥40%).

descriptive.q: Which lesions typically fall in the 10–30 HU 'indeterminate' unenhanced range, and how are they separated?
descriptive.model: Lipid-poor adenomas, pheochromocytomas, metastases and some adrenocortical carcinomas can all sit in this range. They are separated by washout kinetics (adenomas wash out fast), morphology (size, heterogeneity, margins), biochemistry (metanephrines, cortisol), and if needed PET/CT or biopsy after excluding pheochromocytoma.

descriptive.q: Describe the adrenal CT washout protocol and the diagnostic thresholds.
descriptive.model: Acquire unenhanced, contrast-enhanced portovenous (~60–70 s) and 15-minute delayed images. Absolute percentage washout (APW) = (enhanced − delayed)/(enhanced − unenhanced) ×100; relative percentage washout (RPW) = (enhanced − delayed)/enhanced ×100. APW ≥60% or RPW ≥40% indicates an adenoma; lower values are suspicious and warrant further evaluation.

title: Unlabelled Case – Unenhanced CT with ROI, Right Adrenal Lesion (65 HU)

descriptive.q: The ROI reads ~65 HU on this unenhanced image. What does a high unenhanced attenuation like this suggest?
descriptive.model: High unenhanced attenuation (>40–50 HU) argues strongly against a benign lipid-rich adenoma and raises the possibility of hemorrhage (hematoma), calcification, a lipid-poor solid tumor, or, if the scan is actually enhanced, a hypervascular lesion. Correlate with clinical history and compare to prior imaging; hemorrhage should not enhance and should evolve/resolve over time.

descriptive.q: How would you distinguish adrenal hemorrhage from a solid tumor when the unenhanced density is high?
descriptive.model: Hemorrhage shows high attenuation but NO enhancement after contrast and typically decreases in size/density on follow-up. A solid tumor enhances and may grow. Therefore, obtain post-contrast imaging (to assess enhancement) and short-interval follow-up if hemorrhage is suspected.

descriptive.q: What clinical scenarios predispose to non-traumatic adrenal hemorrhage?
descriptive.model: Anticoagulation, coagulopathy or hypercoagulable states (e.g., factor V Leiden, antiphospholipid syndrome), sepsis/stress (Waterhouse–Friderichsen), underlying tumors (pheochromocytoma, metastasis) that bleed, and neonatal stress. Bilateral hemorrhage can precipitate adrenal insufficiency.

title: Unlabelled Case – Unenhanced CT with ROI, Right Adrenal Nodule (23.5 HU)

descriptive.q: The small right adrenal nodule measures ~23.5 HU unenhanced. Classify this lesion and justify.
descriptive.model: At ~23.5 HU it is above 10 HU and therefore indeterminate/lipid-poor on the unenhanced scan; it cannot be confirmed as a benign lipid-rich adenoma by density alone. Most such small nodules prove to be lipid-poor adenomas, but confirmation requires washout CT or chemical-shift MRI.

descriptive.q: Given the very small size (sub-centimetre ROI), what pitfalls affect HU measurement and interpretation?
descriptive.model: Small lesions suffer partial-volume averaging with surrounding fat and normal gland, which can spuriously raise or lower the measured HU. Use the thinnest slices, a representative ROI, and correlate across the lesion. Very small (<1 cm) nodules may be hard to fully characterize and are often followed or assessed with MRI.

descriptive.q: What is the current approach to a small, homogeneous, indeterminate but likely-benign adrenal nodule that is non-functioning?
descriptive.model: If biochemically non-functioning and with benign morphology, many guidelines (e.g., ESE 2016) allow no further imaging follow-up, especially if ≤10 HU. For indeterminate density, options include chemical-shift MRI or washout CT; if still indeterminate and small, interval imaging or MDT discussion is reasonable rather than immediate resection.

title: Unlabelled Case – T1 In-Phase MRI, Left Adrenal Nodule

descriptive.q: Identify the sequence and describe the arrowed left adrenal nodule's signal on this image.
descriptive.model: This is a T1-weighted in-phase gradient-echo image (fat and water signals additive). Describe the arrowed left adrenal nodule's size, margins and signal intensity relative to liver/spleen. The in-phase image is the baseline against which the opposed-phase image is compared for signal drop.

descriptive.q: Explain the physics of chemical-shift imaging and what 'signal drop on opposed-phase' means.
descriptive.model: Fat and water protons precess at slightly different frequencies. At the opposed-phase echo time their signals cancel within a voxel containing both, causing signal loss. A lipid-rich adenoma, which contains intracytoplasmic lipid (fat and water in the same voxel), therefore loses signal on opposed-phase relative to in-phase — the diagnostic feature.

descriptive.q: How is the degree of signal drop quantified, and what threshold suggests a lipid-rich adenoma?
descriptive.model: Compare lesion signal on in-phase (SI-in) and opposed-phase (SI-opp), often normalized to spleen. A visible or >16–20% drop in adrenal-to-spleen signal ratio on opposed-phase indicates intracytoplasmic lipid and a benign adenoma. No drop suggests a lipid-poor lesion needing further evaluation.

title: Unlabelled Case – T2 MRI, Left Adrenal Nodule

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed left adrenal lesion's T2 signal and shape, and what that signal implies.
descriptive.model: On T2, a rounded well-defined lesion is seen at the left adrenal. Very high (fluid-like) T2 signal suggests a cyst or the classic 'light-bulb bright' pheochromocytoma; intermediate signal suggests a solid adenoma or metastasis. Describe margins (smooth favors benign) and homogeneity.

descriptive.q: If this lesion were markedly T2-hyperintense, what two very different diagnoses must be considered and how are they separated?
descriptive.model: A markedly T2-bright adrenal lesion may be a simple cyst or a pheochromocytoma. They are separated by enhancement (cyst does not enhance; pheochromocytoma enhances avidly), by wall/complexity, and crucially by biochemistry — elevated plasma/urinary metanephrines confirm pheochromocytoma. Never biopsy before excluding pheochromocytoma.

descriptive.q: What complementary sequences and tests confirm the diagnosis suggested by T2 signal?
descriptive.model: Add in/opposed-phase (lipid), dynamic post-gadolinium (enhancement/washout), and DWI. Combine with biochemical screening (metanephrines, cortisol, aldosterone). Functional imaging such as MIBG or Ga-68 DOTATATE can confirm a pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma when suspected.

title: Unlabelled Case – CT, Small Left Para-Adrenal Nodule

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed structure and confirm whether it is adrenal in origin.
descriptive.model: The arrow points to a small nodule in the left suprarenal/para-aortic region. Confirm adrenal origin by tracing continuity with the adrenal limbs and its position above the kidney, lateral to the aorta, behind the pancreas/stomach. A retrocrural or para-aortic lymph node or a vessel can mimic an adrenal nodule and must be excluded on multiplanar images.

descriptive.q: What normal adrenal dimensions help you decide if a limb is abnormally thickened?
descriptive.model: Normal adrenal limbs are generally ≤ the thickness of the adjacent diaphragmatic crus (roughly ≤5–6 mm limb width); the whole gland body is up to ~1 cm. A discrete nodule or limb thicker than the crus is abnormal and should be characterized (HU, washout, chemical shift).

descriptive.q: Outline how you would characterize a sub-centimetre indeterminate adrenal nodule.
descriptive.model: Measure unenhanced HU (≤10 = adenoma). If indeterminate, use chemical-shift MRI or washout CT. Screen biochemically. For a small, non-functioning, morphologically benign nodule, follow-up imaging or MDT review is preferable to immediate biopsy; exclude pheochromocytoma before any intervention.

title: Unlabelled Case – CT, Left Adrenal Nodule

descriptive.q: Systematically describe the arrowed left adrenal nodule (side, size, density, margins, homogeneity).
descriptive.model: Describe the arrowed left adrenal nodule by side (left, lateral to aorta), approximate size, attenuation relative to muscle/soft tissue, margin (smooth vs irregular) and homogeneity. A small, smooth, homogeneous low-density nodule favors an adenoma; irregular, heterogeneous or large favors malignancy.

descriptive.q: State the single most useful CT measurement for characterizing this lesion and its threshold.
descriptive.model: Unenhanced attenuation. A value ≤10 HU is diagnostic of a lipid-rich adenoma with high specificity. If the image is contrast-enhanced or the value is >10 HU, proceed to washout CT or chemical-shift MRI.

descriptive.q: When is functional (nuclear) imaging indicated in adrenal characterization?
descriptive.model: FDG-PET/CT helps distinguish benign from malignant indeterminate lesions and stages cancer patients (malignant lesions are FDG-avid > liver). MIBG or Ga-68 DOTATATE targets pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma. Functional imaging is reserved for indeterminate lesions, suspected malignancy, or catecholamine-secreting tumors after biochemical suspicion.

title: Unlabelled Case – T2 Fat-Sat MRI, Left Adrenal Bright Nodule

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed lesion on this fat-saturated T2 image and the significance of fat suppression.
descriptive.model: The arrow shows a rounded, T2-hyperintense left adrenal nodule on a fat-saturated sequence. Fat saturation nulls macroscopic fat, so persistent high signal indicates fluid/water content rather than fat — helping distinguish a cyst or a T2-bright pheochromocytoma from a fat-containing myelolipoma (which would lose signal with fat sat).

descriptive.q: How does fat-saturated imaging help separate a myelolipoma from a fluid-containing lesion?
descriptive.model: Macroscopic fat (as in a myelolipoma) is bright on T2/T1 without fat sat but drops out (becomes dark) on fat-suppressed sequences. A cyst or pheochromocytoma stays bright on fat-suppressed T2 because their signal is from fluid, not fat. India-ink artifact and opposed-phase drop indicate microscopic (intracytoplasmic) lipid instead.

descriptive.q: For a markedly T2-bright adrenal nodule, what biochemical test is mandatory before any biopsy or surgery, and why?
descriptive.model: Plasma or 24-hour urinary metanephrines to exclude a pheochromocytoma, which can be strikingly T2-bright. Biopsy or surgical manipulation of an unrecognized pheochromocytoma can precipitate a life-threatening hypertensive/catecholamine crisis, so it must be excluded first.

title: Unlabelled Case – Contrast CT, Suprarenal Region

descriptive.q: Describe the normal adrenal anatomy visible on this axial contrast CT and how to locate each gland.
descriptive.model: On axial CT, find the right adrenal posterior to the IVC and behind the right lobe of the liver, and the left adrenal antero-lateral to the aorta, behind the pancreas and stomach, above the left kidney. Normal glands have an inverted-Y, V or T shape with thin limbs (no thicker than the adjacent diaphragmatic crus).

descriptive.q: What criteria distinguish a normal adrenal gland from a pathologically enlarged or nodular one?
descriptive.model: Normal: thin, smooth limbs ≤ crus thickness, no focal nodule, uniform soft-tissue density. Abnormal: focal nodule, limb thickening beyond crus, mass effect, heterogeneity, abnormal enhancement, or bilateral diffuse enlargement (suggesting hyperplasia/infiltration).

descriptive.q: List common structures that can mimic an adrenal lesion on CT and how to avoid the error.
descriptive.model: Gastric diverticulum, splenic lobulation/accessory spleen, tortuous vessels, pancreatic tail lesions, exophytic renal/upper-pole lesions and para-aortic nodes can mimic adrenal masses. Use multiplanar reformats, oral/IV contrast and continuity assessment to confirm the lesion arises from the adrenal gland.

title: Unlabelled Case – T1 MRI, Left Adrenal Lesion

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed left adrenal lesion's T1 signal and what high or low T1 signal implies.
descriptive.model: Describe the lesion's T1 signal relative to liver/spleen. Intrinsic T1 hyperintensity suggests fat, subacute blood (methemoglobin), or proteinaceous fluid; low T1 suggests simple fluid or solid tumor. Correlate with in/opposed-phase and fat-saturated images to determine whether the T1 signal is from fat or hemorrhage.

descriptive.q: How do in-phase and opposed-phase images differ, and which lesion characteristically loses signal on opposed-phase?
descriptive.model: In-phase: fat and water signals add. Opposed-phase: they cancel in mixed voxels, causing signal loss where microscopic (intracytoplasmic) lipid coexists with water. A lipid-rich adenoma characteristically drops in signal on opposed-phase — the hallmark of benignity.

descriptive.q: What is the added value of dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI in adrenal masses?
descriptive.model: Dynamic gadolinium imaging assesses enhancement and washout: adenomas enhance and wash out quickly; pheochromocytomas enhance avidly and may show cystic/necrotic change; malignancies show heterogeneous, persistent enhancement. It complements chemical shift when the lesion is lipid-poor.

title: Unlabelled Case – Post-Contrast MRI, Left Adrenal Lesion

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed left adrenal lesion and its enhancement on this post-gadolinium image.
descriptive.model: Describe the left adrenal lesion's size, margins and enhancement pattern (homogeneous vs heterogeneous, avid vs mild). Avid or heterogeneous enhancement suggests a vascular/solid tumor (pheochromocytoma, carcinoma, metastasis), whereas an adenoma shows moderate enhancement with rapid washout and a cyst shows none.

descriptive.q: What enhancement/washout behaviour distinguishes an adenoma from a malignant or catecholamine-secreting tumor on MRI?
descriptive.model: Adenomas enhance moderately and wash out quickly. Malignant lesions (metastasis, ACC) enhance heterogeneously and retain contrast (delayed washout). Pheochromocytomas enhance avidly, are markedly T2-bright, and may have cystic/hemorrhagic areas. Washout kinetics and T2 signal together help separate them.

descriptive.q: Summarize a practical MRI protocol for characterizing an indeterminate adrenal mass.
descriptive.model: T1 in/opposed-phase (microscopic lipid), T2 (fluid/pheochromocytoma), fat-saturated sequences (macroscopic fat), diffusion-weighted imaging, and dynamic post-gadolinium T1 (enhancement/washout). Interpret alongside biochemical screening; add functional imaging (PET, MIBG, DOTATATE) as indicated.

title: Unlabelled Case – CT, Bilateral Adrenal Masses

descriptive.q: Describe the distribution of the arrowed abnormalities and why bilaterality changes the differential.
descriptive.model: The arrows indicate bilateral adrenal masses. Bilateral involvement shifts the differential toward metastases, lymphoma, infection (TB, fungal), bilateral hemorrhage, hyperplasia, and bilateral pheochromocytomas (as in hereditary syndromes). Unilateral solitary lesions more often represent adenoma or a solitary tumor.

descriptive.q: List the key differential diagnoses for bilateral adrenal masses and a distinguishing feature of each.
descriptive.model: Metastases (known primary, FDG-avid, soft-tissue density); lymphoma (homogeneous soft tissue, encasing without displacing vessels); infection/TB (may calcify, can cause insufficiency); hemorrhage (high density, no enhancement, resolves); hyperplasia (diffuse thickening, endocrine syndrome); bilateral pheochromocytomas (T2-bright, ↑metanephrines, MEN2/VHL/NF1).

descriptive.q: What clinical/biochemical evaluation is essential when bilateral adrenal enlargement is found?
descriptive.model: Assess adrenal function (morning cortisol/ACTH, Synacthen test) for insufficiency given the risk of bilateral cortical destruction; screen for pheochromocytoma (metanephrines) and cortisol excess; take an oncologic and infectious history. Biopsy may be needed for suspected metastasis/lymphoma/infection AFTER excluding pheochromocytoma.

title: Unlabelled Case – CT, Complex Left Adrenal Mass

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed left-sided mass, emphasising size, heterogeneity and margins.
descriptive.model: The arrows indicate a large, heterogeneous left suprarenal mass. Note irregular margins and mixed internal attenuation (possible necrosis/hemorrhage/calcification). Large size (>4 cm), heterogeneity and irregular borders are features that raise concern for a malignant lesion such as adrenocortical carcinoma.

descriptive.q: What CT features favour adrenocortical carcinoma over a benign adenoma?
descriptive.model: ACC tends to be large (>4–6 cm), heterogeneous with necrosis/hemorrhage/calcification, irregularly marginated, with poor contrast washout, possible venous (IVC/renal vein) invasion and metastases. Adenomas are small, homogeneous, low-attenuation (≤10 HU or high washout) and smoothly marginated.

descriptive.q: How does functional status and staging influence management of a suspected primary adrenal malignancy?
descriptive.model: Assess for cortisol/androgen/aldosterone excess and exclude pheochromocytoma. Stage with contrast CT/MRI (local invasion, nodes, IVC tumor thrombus) and often FDG-PET. A suspected ACC is generally managed with complete surgical resection by an experienced center rather than percutaneous biopsy, which risks tumor seeding.

title: Unlabelled Case – CT, Right Adrenal Curvilinear Rim Calcification

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed right adrenal lesion, paying attention to the calcification pattern.
descriptive.model: The arrow shows a rounded right adrenal lesion with thin, curvilinear (eggshell) rim calcification. Curvilinear/peripheral calcification with low-density (fluid) centre suggests a benign adrenal cyst or pseudocyst (often post-hemorrhagic). Coarse or nodular calcification within a soft-tissue mass would be more concerning.

descriptive.q: What is the differential for a calcified adrenal lesion, and which patterns are reassuring?
descriptive.model: Differential: adrenal cyst/pseudocyst (thin curvilinear rim), old hemorrhage, prior infection/TB, myelolipoma (may calcify), and calcified neoplasm (ACC, pheochromocytoma). Thin curvilinear/septal calcification is reassuring; thick, coarse or nodular calcification within an enhancing soft-tissue mass is suspicious for malignancy.

descriptive.q: How would you confirm the benign cystic nature of this lesion?
descriptive.model: Assess internal attenuation (water density), lack of enhancement of the contents, thin non-nodular wall, and stability over time. MRI shows fluid signal (bright T2, low T1) without enhancing soft-tissue components. If features are atypical (thick/nodular wall, enhancement), consider resection.

title: Unlabelled Case – CT, Bilateral Adrenal Enlargement

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed bilateral adrenal abnormality and its symmetry.
descriptive.model: The arrows indicate bilateral adrenal enlargement. Note whether it is diffuse and relatively symmetric (favoring hyperplasia or infiltration) or nodular/mass-like (favoring bilateral tumors/metastases). Symmetric diffuse thickening in a patient with hypercortisolism suggests ACTH-driven hyperplasia.

descriptive.q: How do you differentiate bilateral hyperplasia from bilateral metastatic disease on imaging and clinically?
descriptive.model: Hyperplasia: bilateral diffuse (often symmetric) thickening, may become nodular, associated with an endocrine syndrome (Cushing) and appropriate ACTH levels. Metastases: bilateral discrete soft-tissue masses, known primary, FDG-avid, may cause adrenal insufficiency. Biochemistry and oncologic history are decisive; biopsy if needed.

descriptive.q: Why must adrenal function be assessed in bilateral adrenal disease?
descriptive.model: Extensive bilateral involvement (metastasis, hemorrhage, infiltration, infection) can destroy enough cortex to cause primary adrenal insufficiency, a potentially fatal but treatable condition. Conversely, bilateral hyperplasia may cause hormone excess. Testing cortisol/ACTH and, when relevant, metanephrines and aldosterone guides management.

title: Unlabelled Case – Contrast CT, Adrenal and Retroperitoneal Findings

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed findings (note the different-coloured arrows) and their anatomic locations.
descriptive.model: The white and black arrows point to separate findings — an adrenal lesion and an adjacent retroperitoneal/para-aortic abnormality (e.g., a lymph node or second lesion). Describe each by location, size and enhancement, and assess whether the adrenal lesion and the nodal finding suggest a unifying diagnosis such as metastatic disease or lymphoma.

descriptive.q: When an adrenal mass is accompanied by retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, how does that affect your differential?
descriptive.model: Concurrent adrenal mass and lymphadenopathy raise concern for malignancy — metastatic disease (with a known/occult primary), lymphoma (which can involve both adrenals and nodes, classically encasing vessels), or adrenocortical carcinoma with nodal spread. Correlate with primary tumor history, FDG-PET, and tissue sampling.

descriptive.q: What imaging and tissue steps establish the diagnosis in this scenario?
descriptive.model: Contrast CT/MRI to map extent and vascular involvement, FDG-PET/CT for staging and lesion metabolism, biochemical screening (including metanephrines before any biopsy), and image-guided biopsy of the safest target (often a node) to obtain histology (e.g., lymphoma vs metastasis).

title: Unlabelled Case – CT, Bilateral Renal/Adrenal Region Lesions

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed bilateral lesions and clarify whether they are adrenal or renal in origin.
descriptive.model: The arrows indicate bilateral upper-abdominal lesions near the kidneys/adrenals. Determine origin by anatomy: adrenal lesions sit supero-medial to the kidney and separate from renal parenchyma, whereas exophytic renal masses arise from and claw into the cortex. Multiplanar imaging and the plane of cleavage clarify the organ of origin.

descriptive.q: What is the differential for bilateral, symmetric upper-abdominal masses in this location?
descriptive.model: If adrenal: bilateral metastases, lymphoma, pheochromocytomas (hereditary), hemorrhage, hyperplasia, infection. If renal: bilateral RCC (e.g., VHL), angiomyolipomas, cysts, lymphoma. Several hereditary syndromes (VHL, MEN2) can cause synchronous adrenal and renal lesions, so a syndromic evaluation may be warranted.

descriptive.q: How would you proceed to characterize and manage bilateral indeterminate lesions?
descriptive.model: Characterize each lesion (unenhanced HU, washout, chemical shift, enhancement), screen biochemically (metanephrines, cortisol, aldosterone), assess for a hereditary syndrome and family history, and use PET/biopsy where malignancy is suspected — always excluding pheochromocytoma before intervention. Manage in a multidisciplinary setting.

title: Unlabelled Case – CT, Right Adrenal Low-Density Mass

descriptive.q: Describe the arrowed right adrenal mass, focusing on attenuation and whether macroscopic fat is present.
descriptive.model: The arrow shows a well-defined right adrenal mass of low attenuation. Look specifically for macroscopic fat (negative HU, around −30 to −100), which would indicate a myelolipoma, versus water density (near 0 HU) for a cyst, versus soft-tissue density for a solid lesion. The presence of macroscopic fat is essentially diagnostic of a benign myelolipoma.

descriptive.q: How does the presence of macroscopic fat change the diagnosis and management?
descriptive.model: Macroscopic fat within an adrenal mass indicates a myelolipoma (or rarely a lipomatous component of another lesion) — a benign, typically non-functioning tumor. Recognizing fat avoids unnecessary workup; large or symptomatic/hemorrhage-prone myelolipomas may be resected, but most are simply followed.

descriptive.q: Contrast the CT appearance of a myelolipoma, a lipid-rich adenoma, and a simple cyst.
descriptive.model: Myelolipoma: contains MACROSCOPIC fat (negative HU) mixed with myeloid soft tissue. Lipid-rich adenoma: MICROSCOPIC (intracytoplasmic) lipid giving low HU (≤10) but not frankly fatty, with signal drop on opposed-phase MRI. Simple cyst: water attenuation (~0 HU), thin wall, no enhancement.

title: Adrenal Gland Anatomy & Vasculature
caption: Anatomy of the adrenal glands and surrounding structures. The illustration shows the anatomical relationships of the adrenal glands to nearby organs and vasculature: the right adrenal gland and its vein, the inferior vena cava, abdominal aorta and left kidney. The arterial blood supply is provided by the superior, middle and inferior suprarenal arteries, and the glands are drained by the left and right suprarenal veins.

mcqs.q: The adrenal glands receive arterial blood from three sources. Which set correctly names them?
opts: Superior, middle and inferior suprarenal arteries
Coeliac, superior mesenteric and inferior mesenteric arteries
mcqs.exp: Each adrenal is supplied by the superior suprarenal artery (from the inferior phrenic artery), the middle suprarenal artery (from the aorta), and the inferior suprarenal artery (from the renal artery). This rich multi-source supply contrasts with the single main draining vein.
mcqs.hint: Three arteries in, named by position: top, middle, bottom.

mcqs.q: The superior suprarenal artery is typically a branch of which vessel?
mcqs.exp: The superior suprarenal artery arises from the inferior phrenic artery; the middle arises directly from the aorta; the inferior arises from the renal artery. This is a classic anatomy exam point.
mcqs.hint: It comes down from the diaphragmatic (phrenic) supply above.

mcqs.q: The right suprarenal vein has a characteristically short, direct course draining into which structure?
mcqs.exp: The right suprarenal vein is short and drains directly into the IVC, making it technically challenging to cannulate during adrenal venous sampling; the left suprarenal vein drains into the left renal vein.
mcqs.hint: Right side drains straight into the big vein it sits behind.

mcqs.q: The left suprarenal vein usually drains into which vein, often joining another vessel?
opts: Left renal vein (commonly with the inferior phrenic vein)
mcqs.exp: The left suprarenal vein is longer and drains into the left renal vein, frequently sharing a common trunk with the left inferior phrenic vein. This anatomy is exploited during adrenal venous sampling for primary aldosteronism.
mcqs.hint: Left gland uses the renal vein below it.

mcqs.q: Why is the venous drainage anatomy of the adrenal glands clinically important?
opts: It is the basis of adrenal venous sampling (AVS) for lateralizing hormone excess
It explains lymphatic spread of tumors
mcqs.exp: Adrenal venous sampling catheterizes each adrenal vein to lateralize autonomous hormone secretion (especially aldosterone). The short right adrenal vein draining into the IVC makes right-sided sampling the harder, more failure-prone step.
mcqs.hint: Think about how endocrinologists prove WHICH gland is the culprit.

title: Discoid Adrenal Gland – Renal Agenesis
caption: Discoid adrenal gland in a patient with Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome. CT in a 20-year-old female presenting with amenorrhea shows a bicornuate uterus with a cystic structure and the cervix on the left side of the pelvic floor (white dotted circle), along with agenesis of the left kidney. The left adrenal gland is flattened and elongated, displaying the characteristic discoid (pancake/lying-down) shape (A, B: black arrow), commonly associated with unilateral renal agenesis.

mcqs.q: In unilateral renal agenesis, the ipsilateral adrenal gland characteristically assumes which shape?
mcqs.exp: When a kidney fails to develop, the adrenal on that side loses the mould normally provided by the upper renal pole and adopts a flat, elongated 'discoid' or 'lying-down' shape. The gland is present (adrenal development is independent of the kidney), just differently shaped.
mcqs.hint: Without a kidney to sit on, the gland lies flat like a pancake.

mcqs.q: The presence of a discoid adrenal gland should prompt the radiologist to look for what?
mcqs.exp: A flat, discoid adrenal is a useful clue to ipsilateral renal agenesis or a severely ectopic/hypoplastic kidney. Recognizing it prevents mistaking absence of a kidney for surgical removal, and prompts a search for associated anomalies.
mcqs.hint: The abnormal shape points you to a missing/absent neighbour.

mcqs.q: Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome classically combines uterine/vaginal anomalies with which renal finding?
mcqs.exp: Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich (OHVIRA) syndrome features uterus didelphys/obstructed hemivagina with ipsilateral renal agenesis. The associated discoid adrenal reflects the absent ipsilateral kidney, tying the müllerian and renal (mesonephric) developmental link together.
mcqs.hint: It is the syndrome of obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal anomaly.

title: Ectopic Adrenocortical Adenoma
caption: Ectopic adrenocortical adenoma. A 60-year-old male had a mass near the right renal hilum found on health screening. Unenhanced CT shows low attenuation of 8 HU (A); contrast-enhanced images reveal a washout tendency (B, C). On T2 the mass shows slightly low signal (D). On chemical-shift imaging the lesion is high signal on in-phase (E) and loses signal on opposed-phase (F), indicating intracytoplasmic fat. Surgically resected and confirmed as an ectopic adrenocortical adenoma.

mcqs.q: The lesion measured 8 HU on unenhanced CT. What does this indicate?
mcqs.exp: An unenhanced attenuation of 8 HU (≤10 HU) reflects abundant intracytoplasmic lipid and is diagnostic of a lipid-rich adenoma, whatever its location. Density below 10 HU is the single most specific CT sign of a benign adenoma.
mcqs.hint: 8 HU is under the key threshold of 10.

mcqs.q: The lesion loses signal on opposed-phase relative to in-phase chemical-shift imaging. What does this confirm?
mcqs.exp: Signal drop on opposed-phase images indicates microscopic intracytoplasmic lipid — the MRI hallmark of an adrenal adenoma. This corroborates the low CT attenuation. Macroscopic fat instead drops out on fat-saturated sequences.
mcqs.hint: Opposed-phase signal loss = fat and water together in the cells.

mcqs.q: This adenoma was located near the right renal hilum rather than in the adrenal gland. What does 'ectopic adrenocortical' tissue reflect?
opts: Adrenal rest tissue along the embryologic path of gonadal/adrenal descent
mcqs.exp: Ectopic (accessory) adrenocortical tissue arises from adrenal rests that migrate along the path of gonadal descent (near kidney, along the gonadal vessels, in the retroperitoneum, or even the scrotum). Such rests can form adenomas outside the normal gland.
mcqs.hint: Embryonic 'rests' of adrenal tissue can be left along the way down.

mcqs.q: Despite the unusual location, which imaging features allowed a confident benign diagnosis here?
opts: Low unenhanced HU, contrast washout, and opposed-phase signal drop
mcqs.exp: The combination of ≤10 HU on unenhanced CT, washout tendency on delayed CT, and opposed-phase signal loss on MRI all point to a benign lipid-rich adenoma. Concordant benign features across modalities give a confident diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: All three benign signatures were present together.

mcqs.q: Which structure is a well-recognized site for ectopic/accessory adrenocortical rests?
opts: Along the gonadal vessels and near the kidney
mcqs.exp: Accessory adrenocortical tissue is most often found near the celiac axis, along the gonadal vessels, adjacent to the kidney, in the broad ligament, and in the testis/spermatic cord — reflecting the shared embryologic origin and descent with the gonads.
mcqs.hint: Follow the route the gonads travel during development.

title: ¹³¹I-Adosterol Adrenocortical Scintigraphy
caption: Posterior-to-anterior views of ¹³¹I-adosterol (NP-59) scintigraphy with correlative imaging. (A,B) Subclinical Cushing syndrome, 50-year-old female: moderate uptake in the right adrenal adenoma with mild contralateral suppression; CT shows a hypoattenuating right adrenal mass. (C,D) Cushing syndrome, 60-year-old female: marked uptake in the adenoma with obvious contralateral suppression; chemical-shift subtraction highlights fat content. (E,F) ACTH-dependent Cushing disease, 42-year-old female: bilateral adrenal uptake. (G,H) Bilateral micronodular disease, 67-year-old female: symmetrical high uptake bilaterally despite CT suggesting a unilateral adenoma. (I,J) Bilateral macronodular disease, 71-year-old male: high bilateral uptake with multiple nodules on CT.

mcqs.q: ¹³¹I-adosterol (NP-59) is a radiolabeled analog of which substance, allowing it to image the adrenal cortex?
mcqs.exp: NP-59 (¹³¹I-6β-iodomethyl-norcholesterol) is a cholesterol analog taken up by the adrenal cortex, which uses cholesterol as the substrate for steroidogenesis. It therefore images cortical (steroid-producing) function, not the medulla.
mcqs.hint: The cortex makes steroids from this lipid building block.

mcqs.q: In a unilateral cortisol-secreting adenoma, the classic NP-59 scintigraphic pattern is:
opts: Increased uptake on the adenoma side with suppression of the contralateral gland
Uptake only in the contralateral gland
mcqs.exp: An autonomous cortisol-secreting adenoma concentrates NP-59 while suppressing ACTH, which shuts down the contralateral normal gland — giving unilateral uptake with contralateral suppression. This functional lateralization is the strength of NP-59.
mcqs.hint: The autonomous nodule lights up; the suppressed partner goes dark.

mcqs.q: Bilateral symmetric NP-59 uptake, as in panels E–J, is characteristic of which conditions?
opts: ACTH-dependent Cushing disease and bilateral micro-/macronodular hyperplasia
mcqs.exp: Bilateral symmetric cortical uptake indicates a bilateral process — ACTH-dependent (pituitary) Cushing disease or bilateral nodular hyperplasia — rather than a single autonomous adenoma. NP-59 can reveal bilateral functional disease even when CT suggests one nodule.
mcqs.hint: Both glands glowing means the problem is on both sides or ACTH-driven.

mcqs.q: In panels G–H, CT suggested a unilateral adenoma but scintigraphy showed bilateral uptake, changing management. What does this illustrate?
opts: Functional imaging can reveal bilateral disease that anatomic imaging misclassifies as unilateral
CT is the gold standard for function
mcqs.exp: Functional NP-59 scintigraphy characterizes hormone activity, which can differ from anatomic appearance. Here it revealed bilateral micronodular disease, preventing an inappropriate unilateral adrenalectomy — showing the complementary value of functional imaging.
mcqs.hint: Anatomy and function don't always agree; function guided surgery here.

mcqs.q: Compared with ¹³¹I-adosterol (cortical imaging), which tracer would you use to image the adrenal MEDULLA / a pheochromocytoma?
mcqs.exp: MIBG (a noradrenaline analog) and somatostatin-receptor agents (Ga-68 DOTATATE) target chromaffin/medullary tissue and pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma. NP-59 images the cortex; the medulla needs catecholamine- or somatostatin-based tracers.
mcqs.hint: Medulla = catecholamines = a noradrenaline-analog tracer.

title: Testicular Adrenal Rest Tumors in CAH
caption: Testicular adrenal rest tumors in congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Unenhanced CT in an 18-year-old male shows multinodular masses in both adrenal glands (arrows) and soft-tissue masses in both testes (arrowhead), typical of testicular adrenal rest tumors associated with congenital adrenal hyperplasia.

mcqs.q: Bilateral adrenal enlargement with bilateral testicular masses in a young male with congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is explained by which mechanism?
opts: Chronic ACTH stimulation causing adrenal hyperplasia and growth of testicular adrenal rest tumors
mcqs.exp: In poorly controlled CAH, cortisol deficiency drives chronically elevated ACTH, which stimulates both the adrenal glands (hyperplasia) and ectopic adrenal rest tissue within the testes, producing testicular adrenal rest tumors (TARTs).
mcqs.hint: Low cortisol → high ACTH → it stimulates adrenal tissue wherever it sits, including the testes.

mcqs.q: Testicular adrenal rest tumors are important to recognize because they:
opts: Are highly malignant and require orchidectomy
Are benign but can impair fertility and mimic testicular tumors
mcqs.exp: TARTs are benign hyperplastic adrenal-rest tissue but can compress seminiferous tubules and cause infertility. They can mimic malignant testicular tumors on ultrasound; recognizing the CAH context (bilateral, near mediastinum testis) avoids unnecessary orchidectomy.
mcqs.hint: Benign, but they threaten fertility and mimic cancer.

mcqs.q: What is the mainstay of management that can shrink testicular adrenal rest tumors in CAH?
opts: Optimizing glucocorticoid therapy to suppress ACTH
mcqs.exp: Because TARTs are ACTH-driven, intensifying/optimizing glucocorticoid replacement suppresses ACTH and can reduce tumor size, preserving testicular function. This mirrors how better CAH control also reduces adrenal hyperplasia.
mcqs.hint: Turn down the ACTH drive with adequate steroid replacement.

title: Aldosterone-Producing Adenoma (Conn)
caption: CT and MRI of an aldosterone-producing adenoma. A 73-year-old male with hypertension and nocturia; aldosterone–renin ratio >200 on captopril challenge. Unenhanced CT shows a low-attenuation area (3 HU) anteriorly in the right adrenal mass indicating fat content. The posterior portion increases from 20 HU (unenhanced) to 70 HU on portal venous phase, a relative enhancement ratio of 250% (exceeding the 210% cutoff), consistent with a cortical adenoma. Chemical-shift subtraction suggests lipid content. Adrenal venous sampling confirmed an aldosterone-producing adenoma; the patient underwent surgical resection.

mcqs.q: An elevated aldosterone-to-renin ratio (here >200) is the screening test for which condition suggested by this adenoma?
mcqs.exp: A high aldosterone-to-renin ratio indicates primary aldosteronism — autonomous aldosterone with suppressed renin. Confirmatory testing (e.g., saline/captopril suppression) and often adrenal venous sampling then localize the source before surgery.
mcqs.hint: High aldosterone with low renin = the 'Conn' screen.

mcqs.q: The anterior part of the lesion measured 3 HU on unenhanced CT. What does this signify?
opts: Lipid (fat) content typical of a cortical adenoma
mcqs.exp: A 3-HU region reflects lipid-rich cortical tissue — supporting a benign aldosterone-producing adenoma. Aldosteronomas are often small and lipid-rich, giving low unenhanced attenuation.
mcqs.hint: Single-digit HU means fat-rich, benign adenoma.

mcqs.q: Even with a clear unilateral adenoma on CT, adrenal venous sampling (AVS) was performed. Why is AVS often essential before adrenalectomy in primary aldosteronism?
opts: To confirm the lesion is the actual source and lateralize aldosterone secretion (CT can be misleading)
mcqs.exp: A visible adrenal nodule may be a non-functioning incidentaloma while the contralateral gland is the true aldosterone source (or disease may be bilateral). AVS lateralizes secretion and prevents removing the wrong or an unnecessary gland, especially in patients >35–40 years.
mcqs.hint: Seeing a nodule doesn't prove it's the culprit — sampling does.

mcqs.q: The posterior portion rose from 20 HU to 70 HU on portal venous phase (relative enhancement ratio 250%, above the 210% cutoff). This enhancement behaviour is used to support which diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: Robust early enhancement with an appropriate enhancement ratio (and later washout) is characteristic of a vascular cortical adenoma. Combined with the low-attenuation lipid area, the features favor a benign aldosterone-producing adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Adenomas take up contrast avidly and then wash it out.

mcqs.q: What is the definitive treatment for a lateralized, unilateral aldosterone-producing adenoma?
mcqs.exp: For unilateral, AVS-lateralized primary aldosteronism, laparoscopic unilateral adrenalectomy can cure or greatly improve hypertension and hypokalemia. Medical therapy with a mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist is reserved for bilateral disease or non-surgical candidates.
mcqs.hint: One culprit gland that is lateralized → take that one gland out.

title: Adrenocortical Carcinoma (10 cm, FDG-Avid)
caption: Imaging findings of adrenocortical carcinoma. Unenhanced CT (A) shows a 10-cm right adrenal mass with heterogeneous low density. Enhanced CT (B, C) shows fine tumor vessels and heterogeneous gradual enhancement. FDG-PET/CT (D) shows intense accumulation with a maximum standardized uptake value (SUVmax) of 19.27, indicating high metabolic activity consistent with adrenocortical carcinoma.

mcqs.q: Which combination of features in this 10-cm mass most strongly suggests adrenocortical carcinoma rather than adenoma?
opts: Large size, heterogeneity, and intense FDG uptake
mcqs.exp: ACC is typically large (>4–6 cm), heterogeneous (necrosis, hemorrhage, calcification), and markedly FDG-avid (high SUVmax). Small, homogeneous, low-attenuation lesions with fast washout instead favor a benign adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Big, ugly and metabolically hot = cancer.

mcqs.q: An SUVmax of 19.27 on FDG-PET indicates what about this lesion?
opts: High glucose metabolism, concerning for malignancy
mcqs.exp: A high SUVmax reflects high glucose metabolism typical of malignancy. Adrenal lesions with uptake clearly above liver background are suspicious for carcinoma or metastasis, whereas most adenomas show low uptake.
mcqs.hint: High SUV = hungry, fast-dividing malignant tissue.

mcqs.q: Adrenocortical carcinomas are frequently functional. Which hormonal syndrome is most commonly produced?
opts: Cortisol excess (Cushing), often with androgen excess (virilization)
mcqs.exp: Functional ACCs most often secrete cortisol (causing Cushing syndrome) and/or androgens (causing virilization), sometimes with mixed steroid excess. A rapidly progressive Cushing/virilizing picture with a large adrenal mass is a red flag for ACC.
mcqs.hint: Think rapidly developing Cushing with virilization.

mcqs.q: Which imaging feature would specifically indicate advanced/aggressive ACC on cross-sectional imaging?
opts: Invasion of the IVC/renal vein (tumor thrombus) or adjacent organs
mcqs.exp: Local invasion, IVC/renal vein tumor thrombus, regional lymphadenopathy and distant metastases indicate aggressive ACC and are critical for staging and surgical planning. Adenomas do not invade vessels.
mcqs.hint: Look for the tumor growing into the big veins.

mcqs.q: What is the recommended approach to a large adrenal mass suspicious for ACC, and why is percutaneous biopsy usually avoided?
opts: Percutaneous biopsy first in all cases
Complete en-bloc surgical resection at an experienced center; biopsy risks tumor seeding and rarely changes management
mcqs.exp: Suspected ACC (after excluding pheochromocytoma) is managed by complete surgical resection with clear margins at a specialist center. Percutaneous biopsy risks capsular rupture and tumor tract seeding and seldom alters the plan for a resectable primary.
mcqs.hint: Cut it out whole; don't poke it and risk spreading it.

title: Pheochromocytoma in Von Hippel–Lindau
caption: Pheochromocytoma related to von Hippel–Lindau disease. Enhanced CT (A) shows a homogeneously enhancing right adrenal mass (black arrow) consistent with pheochromocytoma, and a mass in the tail of the pancreas (arrowhead) suggestive of a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor. Gadolinium-enhanced T1 MRI (B) shows a cystic tumor with an intensely enhancing mural nodule in the left cerebellum, consistent with a hemangioblastoma. Findings suggest von Hippel–Lindau disease with pheochromocytoma, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor and cerebellar hemangioblastoma.

mcqs.q: The combination of pheochromocytoma, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor and cerebellar hemangioblastoma indicates which hereditary syndrome?
mcqs.exp: This triad is classic for von Hippel–Lindau (VHL) disease (VHL tumor-suppressor gene). VHL features include CNS/retinal hemangioblastomas, clear-cell renal cell carcinoma, pheochromocytomas, pancreatic NETs/cysts and endolymphatic sac tumors.
mcqs.hint: Hemangioblastoma + pheo + pancreatic NET = a specific 'VHL' constellation.

mcqs.q: Pheochromocytomas arising in VHL are more likely than sporadic ones to be:
opts: Bilateral/multifocal and to present at a younger age
mcqs.exp: Hereditary pheochromocytomas (VHL, MEN2, NF1, SDHx) are more frequently bilateral, multifocal and diagnosed younger than sporadic tumors. This warrants germline testing and lifelong surveillance, and influences the choice of cortical-sparing surgery.
mcqs.hint: Genetic cases tend to be on both sides and in younger patients.

mcqs.q: On MRI, pheochromocytomas are classically described as having what T2 appearance?
mcqs.exp: Pheochromocytomas are often strikingly T2-hyperintense ('light-bulb bright'), though this is variable and less reliable when hemorrhage/necrosis is present. Avid enhancement and elevated metanephrines support the diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: The classic teaching phrase is 'light-bulb bright' on T2.

mcqs.q: Before any surgery or biopsy of a suspected pheochromocytoma, which preparation is essential?
opts: Adequate alpha-adrenergic blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine) with volume expansion
mcqs.exp: Pheochromocytomas must be biochemically confirmed (metanephrines) and the patient pre-treated with alpha-blockade (and volume repletion) before beta-blockade or surgery, to prevent a catastrophic hypertensive crisis from unopposed alpha stimulation during manipulation.
mcqs.hint: Alpha-block BEFORE beta-block; never touch it unprepared.

mcqs.q: Which functional imaging agents are most useful for localizing pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma, especially in hereditary disease?
mcqs.exp: MIBG (catecholamine analog) and Ga-68 DOTATATE PET (somatostatin-receptor imaging) localize pheochromocytomas/paragangliomas and detect multifocal or metastatic disease, which is particularly important in hereditary syndromes like VHL and SDHx mutations.
mcqs.hint: Catecholamine-analog or somatostatin-receptor tracers, not cortical NP-59.

title: Pheochromocytoma in Neurofibromatosis Type 1
caption: Pheochromocytoma associated with neurofibromatosis type 1. FDG-PET/CT maximum-intensity projection shows increased FDG uptake in the left adrenal gland (arrow); the fusion image confirms intense uptake in the left adrenal pheochromocytoma. Contrast-enhanced CT reveals a hypervascular cystic left adrenal mass and a right-sided subcutaneous chest-wall nodule (arrowheads) consistent with neurofibromatosis. Coronal T2 shows the characteristic 'light-bulb' sign of the left adrenal pheochromocytoma.

mcqs.q: A cutaneous/subcutaneous nodule plus an adrenal pheochromocytoma should raise suspicion for which hereditary syndrome?
mcqs.exp: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1, neurofibromin gene) features neurofibromas, café-au-lait macules, Lisch nodules and an increased risk of pheochromocytoma. The chest-wall neurofibroma alongside an adrenal pheochromocytoma fits NF1.
mcqs.hint: Skin neurofibromas + pheo point to the 'NF' syndrome.

mcqs.q: The left adrenal mass is markedly bright on T2 ('light-bulb sign'). This classically suggests:
mcqs.exp: The 'light-bulb bright' T2 appearance is a classic (though variable) feature of pheochromocytoma, reflecting its vascular, sometimes cystic composition. It supports the diagnosis alongside avid enhancement and biochemical confirmation.
mcqs.hint: Very bright T2 = the classic pheo teaching sign.

mcqs.q: On CT, this pheochromocytoma is described as hypervascular and cystic. How do pheochromocytomas typically behave on contrast enhancement and washout?
mcqs.exp: Pheochromocytomas usually enhance avidly and often retain contrast (low washout), and may have cystic/necrotic or hemorrhagic areas. This means they can mimic malignancy on washout criteria — biochemistry is decisive.
mcqs.hint: Very vascular, but does not wash out like an adenoma.

mcqs.q: Which biochemical tests best confirm a pheochromocytoma before intervention?
opts: Plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines
mcqs.exp: Plasma free metanephrines and urinary fractionated metanephrines are the most sensitive tests for pheochromocytoma. Confirmation is mandatory before biopsy or surgery to allow alpha-blockade and avoid a catecholamine crisis.
mcqs.hint: Measure the catecholamine breakdown products — metanephrines.

mcqs.q: Because this patient has NF1, what additional consideration applies to the pheochromocytoma work-up?
opts: Consider germline testing and screen for bilateral/multifocal disease and syndromic tumors
mcqs.exp: Syndromic pheochromocytomas (NF1, VHL, MEN2, SDHx) are more often bilateral/multifocal and recur; genetic counseling, germline testing and lifelong biochemical/imaging surveillance are warranted.
mcqs.hint: Hereditary context means think both sides, genes and surveillance.

title: Pheochromocytoma in MEN2A
caption: Pheochromocytoma in multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2A. ¹²³I-MIBG scintigraphy shows significant radiotracer accumulation in the right thyroid (arrowhead) and left adrenal gland (arrow), consistent with medullary thyroid cancer and pheochromocytoma respectively. Enhanced neck CT shows a low-density area with coarse calcifications at the upper pole of the right thyroid lobe; total thyroidectomy confirmed medullary thyroid cancer. Unenhanced abdominal CT shows a left adrenal nodule, pathologically confirmed as pheochromocytoma.

mcqs.q: The combination of medullary thyroid carcinoma and pheochromocytoma is characteristic of which syndrome?
mcqs.exp: MEN2 (RET proto-oncogene) combines medullary thyroid carcinoma with pheochromocytoma; MEN2A adds primary hyperparathyroidism, and MEN2B adds mucosal neuromas and a marfanoid habitus. MEN1 instead involves parathyroid, pituitary and pancreatic tumors (no pheochromocytoma).
mcqs.hint: Medullary thyroid cancer + pheo = the 'MEN2' pairing.

mcqs.q: In a patient with MEN2, which tumor should generally be excluded/treated BEFORE thyroid surgery, and why?
mcqs.exp: A coexisting pheochromocytoma must be excluded and, if present, resected (or the patient alpha-blocked) before any other surgery. Anesthesia/surgery in an unprepared pheochromocytoma patient can trigger a fatal hypertensive crisis.
mcqs.hint: Deal with the catecholamine tumor first to make anesthesia safe.

mcqs.q: ¹²³I-MIBG accumulates in these tumors because MIBG is an analog of which molecule?
mcqs.exp: MIBG is a noradrenaline (guanethidine) analog taken up by catecholamine-storing cells via the noradrenaline transporter. It localizes pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma and neural-crest tumors; medullary thyroid cancer (also neural-crest derived) can show uptake.
mcqs.hint: It mimics the catecholamine the tumor makes and stores.

mcqs.q: MEN2 is caused by a germline mutation in which gene?
mcqs.exp: MEN2 results from activating germline mutations in the RET proto-oncogene. Identifying RET mutations enables genetic screening of relatives and risk-stratified prophylactic thyroidectomy for medullary thyroid carcinoma.
mcqs.hint: An activating oncogene, not a tumor-suppressor — 'RET'.

mcqs.q: For hereditary pheochromocytoma such as in MEN2, which surgical strategy is often preferred when feasible?
opts: Cortical-sparing (subtotal) adrenalectomy to preserve adrenal function
mcqs.exp: Because hereditary pheochromocytomas are often bilateral/recurrent, cortical-sparing adrenalectomy is favored when oncologically safe, to avoid lifelong steroid dependence and Addisonian risk from bilateral total adrenalectomy.
mcqs.hint: Save some cortex to avoid permanent adrenal insufficiency.

title: Pheochromocytoma in SDHB (PGL4) Syndrome
caption: Pheochromocytoma in PGL4 syndrome (SDHB mutation). A 50-year-old man with confirmed succinate dehydrogenase subunit B (SDHB) mutation. ¹²³I-MIBG scintigraphy shows uptake in the retroperitoneal tumor but mostly minimal uptake. FDG-PET/CT shows intense FDG uptake, more prominent than MIBG — the typical discrepancy of SDHB tumors. Contrast-enhanced MRI shows hypervascularity in both a right renal mass (clear-cell carcinoma) and a right adrenal mass (pheochromocytoma). Associated conditions in PGL4 include pituitary adenoma, GIST and renal-cell carcinoma.

mcqs.q: In SDHB-related (PGL4) paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma, which functional imaging pattern is typical?
opts: High MIBG uptake, low FDG uptake
Low/minimal MIBG uptake but intense FDG (¹⁸F-FDG-PET) uptake
mcqs.exp: SDHx (especially SDHB) tumors are often poorly MIBG-avid but strongly FDG-avid, reflecting a pseudohypoxic, glycolytic (Warburg) phenotype. FDG-PET (and Ga-68 DOTATATE) therefore outperforms MIBG for staging SDHB disease.
mcqs.hint: SDHB tumors are 'MIBG-cold' but 'FDG-hot'.

mcqs.q: SDHB mutations are particularly important because the associated pheochromocytomas/paragangliomas have a higher risk of being:
