mcqs.exp: SDHB carries the highest risk among the succinate-dehydrogenase genes for malignant/metastatic paraganglioma and extra-adrenal disease. This drives intensive surveillance and influences treatment planning.
mcqs.hint: Of the SDH genes, 'B' is the one most linked to metastasis.

mcqs.q: This patient also had a right renal clear-cell carcinoma alongside the pheochromocytoma. Which hereditary paraganglioma-associated feature does this reflect?
opts: SDH-related tumor predisposition (paraganglioma syndromes can include RCC, GIST, pituitary adenoma)
mcqs.exp: SDH-deficiency syndromes predispose not only to paragangliomas/pheochromocytomas but also to renal cell carcinoma, GIST (Carney–Stratakis/Carney triad) and pituitary adenomas — a recognizable tumor constellation prompting germline testing.
mcqs.hint: SDH syndromes bundle paraganglioma with RCC and GIST.

mcqs.q: Given the FDG-avid, MIBG-poor phenotype, which PET tracer is often most sensitive for SDHx paraganglioma localization?
mcqs.exp: Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT has excellent sensitivity for SDHx-related paragangliomas (which strongly express somatostatin receptors) and, with FDG-PET, is preferred over MIBG for staging these tumors.
mcqs.hint: Somatostatin-receptor PET excels for these tumors.

mcqs.q: What does the identification of a germline SDHB mutation mandate for the patient and family?
opts: Genetic counseling, cascade testing of relatives and lifelong surveillance for metachronous tumors
mcqs.exp: A germline SDHB mutation requires genetic counseling, testing of at-risk relatives, and lifelong biochemical and whole-body imaging surveillance given the risk of multifocal, metachronous and malignant paragangliomas.
mcqs.hint: Germline mutation = test the family and watch for life.

title: ¹³¹I-MIBG Therapy for Malignant Pheochromocytoma
caption: Pre- and post-¹²³I-MIBG therapy in malignant pheochromocytoma. A 60-year-old man with malignant pheochromocytoma. Pre-treatment ¹²³I-MIBG scintigraphy (anterior and posterior views) shows multiple areas of radiotracer accumulation indicating widespread metastatic disease. The ¹³¹I-MIBG post-therapy scan shows increased accumulation in the lesions. Scans 5 months after treatment show significant reduction in radiotracer uptake, suggesting a positive response; urinary norepinephrine decreased from 1620 µg/day to 477 µg/day.

mcqs.q: Multiple sites of MIBG accumulation throughout the body in a pheochromocytoma patient indicate what?
mcqs.exp: Multifocal MIBG-avid lesions beyond the adrenal indicate metastatic malignant pheochromocytoma. Malignancy in pheochromocytoma is defined by metastasis to sites where chromaffin tissue is not normally present (e.g., bone, liver, lymph nodes).
mcqs.hint: Many hot spots around the body = spread = malignant.

mcqs.q: Why can ¹³¹I-MIBG be used therapeutically (not just diagnostically) in MIBG-avid tumors?
opts: It delivers targeted beta-radiation to tumor cells that concentrate the tracer
mcqs.exp: ¹³¹I emits beta particles; when carried by MIBG into catecholamine-avid tumor cells, it delivers targeted internal radiotherapy. This 'theranostic' approach treats MIBG-avid metastatic pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma and neuroblastoma.
mcqs.hint: ¹³¹I is a beta-emitter — the tracer carries radiation into the tumor.

mcqs.q: In this case, treatment response was judged by reduced MIBG uptake AND a fall in urinary norepinephrine (1620→477 µg/day). Why combine imaging with biochemistry?
opts: Functional/biochemical markers together give a more reliable measure of tumor burden and response than either alone
mcqs.exp: Catecholamine/metanephrine levels reflect functional tumor burden and symptoms, while MIBG imaging shows anatomic extent and avidity. Concordant improvement in both provides robust evidence of treatment response.
mcqs.hint: Match the pictures with the hormone numbers to confirm response.

mcqs.q: A prerequisite for offering ¹³¹I-MIBG therapy is that the tumor must:
opts: Demonstrate MIBG avidity on a diagnostic scan
mcqs.exp: Only MIBG-avid tumors will concentrate therapeutic ¹³¹I-MIBG. A diagnostic MIBG scan confirms avidity and selects candidates; MIBG-poor (e.g., some SDHB) tumors are treated with other options such as DOTATATE-based PRRT or systemic therapy.
mcqs.hint: The diagnostic scan must light up before the therapy can work.

mcqs.q: Before administering catecholamine-targeted therapy or handling metastatic pheochromocytoma, what supportive measure is important?
opts: Adequate alpha-adrenergic blockade and blood-pressure control
mcqs.exp: Adequate alpha-blockade and blood-pressure/volume optimization protect against catecholamine surges during therapy or procedures. Unopposed beta-blockade is dangerous before alpha-blockade in these patients.
mcqs.hint: Alpha-block first to prevent a catecholamine surge.

title: Neuroblastoma (Stage IV, MYCN Non-amplified)
caption: Imaging findings of neuroblastoma. A 5-year-old male with Stage IV neuroblastoma, MYCN non-amplified. ¹²³I-MIBG scintigraphy shows radiotracer accumulation at the periphery of the left retroperitoneal mass with multiple bone metastases (calvarium, vertebrae, left ilium, right sciatic bone, bilateral femurs) and liver metastasis. Unenhanced and enhanced CT show a large left retroperitoneal mass with calcification; enhanced CT shows peripheral enhancement with extensive internal necrosis, characteristic of advanced neuroblastoma.

mcqs.q: A large calcified retroperitoneal/adrenal mass in a young child crossing the midline is most suggestive of which tumor?
mcqs.exp: Neuroblastoma is the most common extracranial solid tumor of childhood, arising from sympathetic neural-crest tissue (often adrenal medulla). It characteristically calcifies, is heterogeneous with necrosis, and encases/displaces vessels, often crossing the midline.
mcqs.hint: Young child + calcified adrenal mass crossing midline.

mcqs.q: Neuroblastoma takes up MIBG because, like pheochromocytoma, it arises from which tissue lineage?
mcqs.exp: Neuroblastoma is a neural-crest (sympathoadrenal) tumor and concentrates MIBG via the noradrenaline transporter. MIBG scintigraphy is central to staging and response assessment, and MIBG-avid disease can be treated with ¹³¹I-MIBG.
mcqs.hint: Same catecholamine-handling lineage as the adrenal medulla.

mcqs.q: Which molecular marker in neuroblastoma is a key adverse prognostic factor (its absence here is favorable)?
mcqs.exp: MYCN amplification is a major adverse prognostic marker in neuroblastoma, associated with aggressive behavior. This tumor is MYCN non-amplified, a relatively more favorable feature, though the disease is still Stage IV.
mcqs.hint: An amplified 'MYC' family oncogene marks worse prognosis.

mcqs.q: The pattern of bone metastases (calvarium, vertebrae, ilium, femurs) plus liver involvement indicates what stage of neuroblastoma?
mcqs.exp: Widespread skeletal and hepatic metastases define metastatic (Stage IV/M) neuroblastoma. MIBG scintigraphy is excellent for detecting this multifocal marrow/bone disease and directing therapy.
mcqs.hint: Bones all over the body plus liver = advanced/metastatic.

mcqs.q: Which CT features in this mass point to an aggressive tumor rather than a benign adrenal lesion?
opts: Coarse calcification, heterogeneous enhancement and extensive internal necrosis
mcqs.exp: Coarse/stippled calcification, heterogeneous enhancement, necrosis, large size and vascular encasement are hallmarks of neuroblastoma. Benign adrenal lesions are small, homogeneous and low-attenuation or contain fat.
mcqs.hint: Calcified, necrotic and heterogeneous = aggressive.

title: Ganglioneuroma
caption: Imaging findings of ganglioneuroma. A 31-year-old female with an incidentaloma. Unenhanced CT shows a well-circumscribed mass in the left adrenal gland with fine speckled calcifications. T2-weighted MRI shows high signal intensity of the mass. Fusion SPECT/CT shows mild ¹²³I-MIBG uptake in the lesion (occasionally seen in ganglioneuroma, though negative scans are more typical). FDG-PET/CT shows no significant FDG uptake, consistent with ganglioneuroma. Pathology confirmed the diagnosis.

mcqs.q: Ganglioneuroma is best characterized as which type of tumor?
opts: A benign, fully differentiated tumor of the sympathetic ganglia
mcqs.exp: Ganglioneuroma is the benign, fully differentiated end of the neuroblastic tumor spectrum (neuroblastoma → ganglioneuroblastoma → ganglioneuroma). It arises from sympathetic ganglion cells and is typically indolent.
mcqs.hint: The mature, benign cousin of neuroblastoma.

mcqs.q: Which imaging feature is characteristic (though nonspecific) of ganglioneuroma on CT?
opts: Fine speckled/punctate calcification within a well-circumscribed mass
mcqs.exp: Ganglioneuromas are often well-defined and may contain fine, discrete punctate calcifications (versus the coarse calcification of neuroblastoma). They tend to show progressive delayed enhancement and can insinuate around vessels without occluding them.
mcqs.hint: Fine speckled calcs in a well-defined mass.

mcqs.q: The lack of significant FDG uptake in this tumor supports which conclusion?
mcqs.exp: Ganglioneuromas usually show low or no FDG uptake, in keeping with their benign, slow-growing nature. Marked FDG avidity would instead raise concern for a malignant neuroblastic tumor or other malignancy.
mcqs.hint: Metabolically quiet = benign.

mcqs.q: Ganglioneuromas can occasionally show mild MIBG uptake. Why?
opts: They are of sympathetic neural-crest origin (some catecholamine-handling capacity)
mcqs.exp: Being sympathetic neural-crest tumors, ganglioneuromas may occasionally accumulate MIBG, although negative scans are more typical. This shared lineage with neuroblastoma and pheochromocytoma explains the variable uptake.
mcqs.hint: Same neural-crest family, so some tracer avidity is possible.

mcqs.q: How is a ganglioneuroma typically diagnosed and managed?
opts: Often surgical resection for definitive histology, given imaging overlap with other tumors
mcqs.exp: Because imaging features overlap with other adrenal/retroperitoneal tumors, ganglioneuromas are frequently resected for definitive diagnosis and cure; they are benign with an excellent prognosis after complete excision. Pheochromocytoma must be excluded biochemically beforehand.
mcqs.hint: Imaging can't fully prove it, so it's usually taken out.

title: Adrenal Myelolipoma with Retroperitoneal Hemorrhage
caption: Retroperitoneal hemorrhage from an adrenal myelolipoma. A 38-year-old male presented with right-upper-quadrant pain. Unenhanced CT shows a right adrenal mass containing macroscopic fat, suspected to be an adrenal myelolipoma (or cortical adenoma with myelolipomatous degeneration); the tumor had grown 8 mm/year. Enhanced CT shows contrast extravasation within the mass, confirming active bleeding. Right-renal angiography shows active extravasation from the right inferior adrenal artery, successfully treated with glue embolization.

mcqs.q: The presence of macroscopic fat within this adrenal mass is essentially diagnostic of which lesion?
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic fat (negative HU, around −30 to −100) within an adrenal mass is essentially pathognomonic of a myelolipoma — a benign tumor of mature fat and hematopoietic (myeloid) tissue. This distinguishes it from a lipid-rich adenoma, which has only microscopic intracytoplasmic lipid.
mcqs.hint: Frank/gross fat, not just low HU, points to one benign tumor.

mcqs.q: What is the most important clinical complication of large adrenal myelolipomas, as demonstrated here?
mcqs.exp: Myelolipomas are non-functioning and benign, but large lesions can undergo spontaneous hemorrhage or rupture, causing pain and potentially life-threatening retroperitoneal bleeding — the indication for treatment here.
mcqs.hint: Big fatty adrenal tumors can bleed.

mcqs.q: On the enhanced CT, contrast extravasation within the mass indicates what?
mcqs.exp: A focus of contrast pooling/extravasation on the enhanced scan indicates active bleeding. This is a marker of ongoing hemorrhage and often prompts urgent intervention such as angiographic embolization.
mcqs.hint: Contrast leaking out = active bleed.

mcqs.q: The active bleeding was treated by embolization of which vessel?
mcqs.exp: Angiography localized extravasation to the right inferior suprarenal (adrenal) artery, which was embolized with glue. Trans-arterial embolization can control adrenal hemorrhage and, in some cases, avoid or delay surgery.
mcqs.hint: Bleeding came from one of the three adrenal arteries — the inferior one.

mcqs.q: For an asymptomatic small adrenal myelolipoma, what is the usual management, and what changes that?
opts: Observation; resection considered if large (often >4–6 cm), symptomatic, growing, or hemorrhagic
mcqs.exp: Most myelolipomas are incidental, benign and simply observed. Surgery is reserved for large, rapidly growing, symptomatic or hemorrhage-prone lesions (as here, with 8 mm/year growth and active bleeding).
mcqs.hint: Small and quiet = watch; big, growing or bleeding = remove.

title: Primary Adrenal Lymphoma
caption: Imaging features of adrenal lymphoma. A 63-year-old man with weight loss, pathologically confirmed primary adrenal lymphoma. FDG-PET maximum-intensity projection shows intense uptake in bilateral adrenal masses; fusion PET/CT highlights the FDG uptake. Contrast-enhanced CT reveals bilateral bulky adrenal masses with heterogeneous enhancement. Diffusion-weighted MRI shows high signal (restricted diffusion). Adrenocortical scintigraphy is negative for abnormal uptake, commonly seen in adrenal lymphoma.

mcqs.q: Bilateral bulky FDG-avid adrenal masses with weight loss and restricted diffusion are most consistent with which diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: Primary (or secondary) adrenal lymphoma typically presents as bilateral bulky, homogeneous-to-heterogeneous soft-tissue masses that are intensely FDG-avid and show restricted diffusion. B-symptoms such as weight loss support the diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: Bilateral bulky FDG-hot masses + B symptoms.

mcqs.q: Adrenocortical (NP-59) scintigraphy is negative in adrenal lymphoma. Why is that expected?
opts: Lymphoma is not steroid-producing cortical tissue, so it does not take up the cholesterol-analog tracer
mcqs.exp: NP-59 is a cholesterol analog taken up by functioning adrenal cortex. Lymphoma replaces/infiltrates the gland with lymphoid tissue that does not concentrate NP-59, so cortical scintigraphy is negative — a helpful corroborating clue.
mcqs.hint: No cortical steroid cells = no cholesterol-analog uptake.

mcqs.q: Restricted diffusion (high DWI signal, low ADC) in these adrenal masses reflects what?
mcqs.exp: Densely packed, highly cellular tumors like lymphoma restrict water diffusion, giving high DWI signal and low ADC. This supports a hypercellular malignant process over a benign cystic or fatty lesion.
mcqs.hint: Tightly packed cells trap water molecules.

mcqs.q: What is a recognized clinical consequence of bilateral adrenal lymphomatous involvement?
mcqs.exp: Extensive bilateral adrenal replacement can cause primary adrenal insufficiency. Recognizing and treating hypoadrenalism is important, and steroid replacement may be needed alongside lymphoma-directed chemotherapy.
mcqs.hint: Both glands destroyed → they can't make cortisol.

mcqs.q: How is the diagnosis of adrenal lymphoma most reliably established?
opts: Image-guided biopsy for histology (after excluding pheochromocytoma)
mcqs.exp: Because imaging is suggestive but not specific, tissue biopsy establishes the diagnosis and subtype of adrenal lymphoma, guiding chemotherapy. Pheochromocytoma should be biochemically excluded before any biopsy.
mcqs.hint: Infiltrative tumor needs a needle for diagnosis — but screen first.

title: Adrenal Metastasis with Hemorrhage (Angiosarcoma)
caption: Imaging of adrenal metastasis in a patient with angiosarcoma of the maxillary sinus. MRI of the maxillary sinus shows a mass with heterogeneous enhancement. Unenhanced CT shows a high-density area dorsally in the left adrenal mass, suggesting retroperitoneal hemorrhage. Enhanced CT shows irregular tumor enhancement in the left adrenal mass, consistent with metastasis. Adrenal metastasis can occasionally be complicated by hemorrhage.

mcqs.q: In a patient with a known primary malignancy, an irregularly enhancing adrenal mass most likely represents:
mcqs.exp: The adrenal glands are a frequent site of metastasis. In a patient with a known primary, an irregularly enhancing, indeterminate adrenal mass is presumed metastatic until proven otherwise, especially when it does not meet benign adenoma criteria.
mcqs.hint: Known cancer + irregular enhancing adrenal mass.

mcqs.q: The high-density area within the left adrenal mass on unenhanced CT here indicates what complication?
mcqs.exp: A spontaneously high-attenuation region on unenhanced CT within a tumor indicates hemorrhage. Vascular metastases (e.g., from angiosarcoma, melanoma, RCC) are prone to bleed, complicating the appearance.
mcqs.hint: High density without contrast, in a vascular tumor = bleed.

mcqs.q: Which primary tumors are especially prone to hemorrhagic adrenal metastases?
opts: Hypervascular tumors such as angiosarcoma, renal cell carcinoma and melanoma
mcqs.exp: Hypervascular malignancies — angiosarcoma (as here), RCC, melanoma, choriocarcinoma and thyroid cancer — tend to produce vascular metastases that can hemorrhage. Recognizing hemorrhage helps explain atypical adrenal mass appearances.
mcqs.hint: The 'bleeder' primaries are the very vascular ones.

mcqs.q: How would you confirm that an indeterminate, possibly hemorrhagic adrenal mass in a cancer patient is a metastasis?
opts: FDG-PET and/or image-guided biopsy after excluding pheochromocytoma
mcqs.exp: FDG-PET can demonstrate malignant metabolic activity, and biopsy provides tissue confirmation when management depends on it. Pheochromocytoma must be excluded biochemically before biopsy.
mcqs.hint: Metabolic imaging then a needle, once pheo is ruled out.

mcqs.q: Why can hemorrhage make washout CT criteria unreliable in this setting?
opts: Hemorrhage raises baseline attenuation and obscures enhancement/washout measurement
mcqs.exp: Blood products raise unenhanced attenuation and create heterogeneity, confounding ROI-based washout calculations. In hemorrhagic or very heterogeneous masses, washout criteria may be invalid, so other tools (PET, biopsy, follow-up) are used.
mcqs.hint: Blood muddies the density numbers the washout formula relies on.

title: Adrenal Infarction in Myelodysplastic Syndrome
caption: Adrenal infarction in a patient with myelodysplastic syndrome. A male in his 50s presented with acute onset of abdominal pain. Unenhanced CT shows swelling of the bilateral adrenal glands with increased surrounding fat density (periadrenal stranding), consistent with adrenal infarction. CT also shows elevated density in the pelvis and bilateral iliac bones, probably related to atypical cells from the myelodysplastic syndrome.

mcqs.q: Bilateral adrenal swelling with periadrenal fat stranding and acute pain, without a discrete mass, suggests which diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: Adrenal infarction presents as gland enlargement/swelling with surrounding inflammatory fat stranding and acute pain, rather than a focal mass. It can be bilateral and is associated with hypercoagulable and hematologic conditions.
mcqs.hint: Swollen glands + fat stranding + acute pain, no true mass.

mcqs.q: Myelodysplastic syndrome predisposes to adrenal infarction largely through which mechanism?
mcqs.exp: Hematologic disorders and hypercoagulable states can cause adrenal vascular thrombosis and infarction. The adrenal's limited venous drainage makes it vulnerable to congestion/infarction when thrombosis occurs.
mcqs.hint: A clotting tendency blocks the gland's drainage.

mcqs.q: What is the most feared functional consequence of bilateral adrenal infarction?
mcqs.exp: Bilateral adrenal infarction/hemorrhage can precipitate acute adrenal insufficiency — a medical emergency. Prompt recognition and stress-dose glucocorticoids can be life-saving.
mcqs.hint: Both glands knocked out acutely → adrenal crisis.

mcqs.q: How does adrenal infarction typically differ from an adrenal tumor on contrast-enhanced CT?
opts: Infarcted tissue shows avid homogeneous enhancement
Infarcted gland shows little/no enhancement with preserved gland shape and periadrenal stranding, not a discrete enhancing mass
mcqs.exp: Infarcted adrenal tissue enhances poorly and retains the gland's contour with surrounding stranding, whereas a tumor forms a discrete enhancing mass. Correlation with acute presentation and a prothrombotic condition supports infarction.
mcqs.hint: Non-enhancing swollen gland vs. an enhancing lump.

mcqs.q: What clinical/laboratory work-up is appropriate when adrenal infarction is diagnosed?
opts: Assess adrenal function and evaluate for an underlying hypercoagulable/hematologic disorder
mcqs.exp: Evaluate adrenal function (cortisol/ACTH, Synacthen) to detect insufficiency, and investigate the prothrombotic cause (hematologic disease, antiphospholipid syndrome, thrombophilia). Management is supportive, treating the underlying condition and any insufficiency.
mcqs.hint: Check the glands' function and hunt for the clotting cause.

title: Adrenal Tuberculosis
caption: Adrenal tuberculosis. A 74-year-old female presented with an incidental adrenal mass. Enhanced CT shows bilateral adrenal masses with low attenuation. T2-weighted MRI shows low signal intensity in the lesions. FDG-PET shows high uptake in the bilateral adrenal masses. Pathological analysis confirmed caseating necrosis, leading to the diagnosis of adrenal tuberculosis.

mcqs.q: Bilateral adrenal masses that are FDG-avid with caseating necrosis on pathology point to which diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: Adrenal tuberculosis causes bilateral adrenal enlargement/masses, can be FDG-avid (mimicking malignancy), and shows caseating necrosis histologically. In endemic areas it is an important infectious cause of bilateral adrenal disease and Addison's disease.
mcqs.hint: Bilateral + caseating necrosis = an infective granulomatous cause.

mcqs.q: Adrenal tuberculosis is a classic infectious cause of which endocrine disorder, especially with chronic bilateral involvement?
mcqs.exp: Historically, TB was a leading cause of Addison's disease. Chronic bilateral adrenal destruction impairs cortisol/aldosterone production; the glands may enlarge early and calcify/atrophy later.
mcqs.hint: Worldwide, a classic infectious cause of Addison's.

mcqs.q: Why is FDG-PET potentially misleading in adrenal tuberculosis?
opts: Active granulomatous/infectious inflammation is FDG-avid and can mimic malignancy
mcqs.exp: Active infection/inflammation (including TB) is metabolically active and FDG-avid, so PET cannot reliably separate infection from malignancy. Histology/microbiology (caseating necrosis, acid-fast bacilli) is required for diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: Infection is metabolically hot too — PET can fool you.

mcqs.q: What chronic imaging feature is characteristic of long-standing (healed) adrenal tuberculosis?
opts: Adrenal calcification (often bilateral) with gland atrophy
mcqs.exp: Chronic/healed adrenal TB classically leads to bilateral adrenal calcification and atrophy. Early active disease shows enlargement with central low-attenuation necrosis; calcification develops over time.
mcqs.hint: Old, burnt-out TB leaves calcified, shrunken glands.

mcqs.q: How is the diagnosis of adrenal tuberculosis established when imaging is nonspecific?
opts: Biopsy/microbiology (caseating necrosis, AFB/culture) plus clinical correlation, after excluding pheochromocytoma
mcqs.exp: Because imaging overlaps with malignancy, tissue sampling demonstrating caseating granulomas and mycobacteria (with cultures/PCR) confirms TB. Assess adrenal function and treat with anti-tuberculous therapy; exclude pheochromocytoma before biopsy.
mcqs.hint: Nonspecific pictures need a needle and a microbiologist.

title: Pheochromocytoma – CT and Gross Specimen
caption: A right 6-cm pheochromocytoma seen on CT (arrow) and after removal (gross specimen). Pheochromocytomas are vascular and may appear variable, with solid, cystic, calcified and necrotic components.

mcqs.q: Which statement best reflects the typical imaging appearance of a pheochromocytoma?
opts: Vascular and heterogeneous, often with solid, cystic, calcified and/or necrotic components
mcqs.exp: Pheochromocytomas are classically vascular (avidly enhancing) and heterogeneous, frequently containing cystic/necrotic areas, hemorrhage and occasionally calcification. This variability is why they are called one of the 'great mimics'.
mcqs.hint: Vascular and mixed-looking, not simple and uniform.

mcqs.q: A 6-cm vascular, heterogeneous adrenal mass should prompt which essential biochemical test before any biopsy or surgery?
mcqs.exp: Any indeterminate adrenal mass — especially a vascular, heterogeneous one — requires exclusion of pheochromocytoma with metanephrines before intervention, to prevent a catecholamine crisis during manipulation.
mcqs.hint: Rule out the catecholamine tumor first, always.

mcqs.q: What is the treatment of choice for a resectable pheochromocytoma after appropriate preparation?
mcqs.exp: Surgical removal (usually laparoscopic adrenalectomy) is curative for localized pheochromocytoma, performed only after adequate preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade and volume expansion to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis.
mcqs.hint: Prepare with alpha-blockade, then take it out.

title: Adrenocortical Carcinoma – CT and Specimen
caption: A left adrenocortical cancer on an adrenal-protocol (arterial phase) contrast-enhanced CT and the corresponding gross specimen after removal. Adrenal cancers often display a heterogeneous (variable) appearance due to necrosis, calcifications and bleeding into the tumor.

mcqs.q: Which appearance is typical of an adrenocortical carcinoma on CT?
opts: Large and heterogeneous with necrosis, calcification and hemorrhage
mcqs.exp: Adrenocortical carcinomas are usually large and heterogeneous, reflecting internal necrosis, hemorrhage and calcification. This contrasts sharply with the small, homogeneous, low-attenuation appearance of a benign adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Big and messy inside, not small and uniform.

mcqs.q: Why is an 'adrenal-protocol' multiphase CT useful when characterizing such a mass?
opts: It provides unenhanced, enhanced and delayed phases to assess attenuation, enhancement and washout
mcqs.exp: An adrenal protocol (unenhanced, portovenous, 15-minute delayed) allows measurement of baseline HU and washout, helping separate benign adenomas (fast washout) from malignant/indeterminate lesions (poor washout), and depicts tumor vascularity and invasion.
mcqs.hint: Multiple phases let you measure density and washout.

mcqs.q: In suspected adrenocortical carcinoma, what management principle applies to obtaining a diagnosis?
opts: Complete surgical resection is preferred; biopsy is generally avoided due to seeding risk (and pheochromocytoma must be excluded)
mcqs.exp: A resectable suspected ACC is removed en bloc at an experienced center rather than biopsied, because percutaneous biopsy risks capsular rupture and tumor seeding and rarely changes management. Pheochromocytoma must first be excluded biochemically.
mcqs.hint: Take it out whole; don't risk seeding by poking it.

title: Pheochromocytoma – MRI and Pathology
caption: MRI demonstrating a left pheochromocytoma (arrow) with the corresponding pathology. Pheochromocytomas often enhance and light up brightly on MRI, an appearance so characteristic that it can strongly suggest the diagnosis.

mcqs.q: Pheochromocytomas classically 'light up brightly' on which MRI sequence?
mcqs.exp: The classic (though variable) MRI feature of pheochromocytoma is marked T2 hyperintensity ('light-bulb bright'), reflecting its vascular and sometimes cystic nature. Avid enhancement and elevated metanephrines support the diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: Very bright on the fluid-sensitive sequence.

mcqs.q: A lipid-rich adenoma would behave OPPOSITELY to a pheochromocytoma on which sequence?
opts: Opposed-phase chemical shift (adenoma loses signal; pheo usually does not)
mcqs.exp: An adenoma is usually not markedly T2-bright and, crucially, loses signal on opposed-phase imaging due to intracytoplasmic lipid, whereas a pheochromocytoma is typically T2-bright and does not drop out on opposed-phase. Both features help distinguish them.
mcqs.hint: Adenoma: not light-bulb bright, and drops on opposed-phase.

mcqs.q: Despite a characteristic MRI appearance, what is required before surgical removal of a suspected pheochromocytoma?
mcqs.exp: Imaging suggests but does not replace biochemical confirmation. Elevated plasma/urinary metanephrines confirm the diagnosis, and adequate alpha-adrenergic blockade with volume expansion is essential before surgery to prevent a hypertensive crisis.
mcqs.hint: Confirm with hormones and prepare with alpha-blockade.

title: Adrenocortical Carcinoma – MRI Features
caption: MRI showing an adrenal cancer (arrow) arising from the right adrenal gland. Note the heterogeneity and irregular borders.

mcqs.q: Which MRI features shown here favor a malignant adrenal mass?
mcqs.exp: Heterogeneity (from necrosis/hemorrhage) and irregular, infiltrative margins are features of malignancy. Benign adenomas are small, homogeneous, smoothly marginated, and lose signal on opposed-phase from lipid.
mcqs.hint: Irregular and heterogeneous points to cancer.

mcqs.q: Which additional MRI/CT finding would strongly indicate an aggressive right adrenocortical carcinoma?
opts: Invasion of the inferior vena cava with tumor thrombus
mcqs.exp: Right-sided ACC can invade the adjacent IVC, producing tumor thrombus — a key staging and surgical-planning finding. Vascular invasion is a hallmark of malignancy not seen with benign adenomas.
mcqs.hint: Look for the tumor growing into the big vein next door.

mcqs.q: On T2-weighted imaging, how do most adrenocortical carcinomas typically appear relative to a benign adenoma?
opts: Higher/heterogeneous T2 signal (intermediate-to-high), unlike the low-intermediate signal of adenomas
mcqs.exp: Malignant adrenal masses tend to show higher, heterogeneous T2 signal, whereas adenomas are usually low-to-intermediate on T2. T2 signal is one of several nonspecific features integrated with size, margins, washout and chemical shift.
mcqs.hint: Cancers are brighter/more heterogeneous on T2 than adenomas.

title: MIBG Scan – Right Pheochromocytoma
caption: An MIBG scan demonstrating positive uptake in the right adrenal gland (arrow), proving to be a pheochromocytoma.

mcqs.q: Focal MIBG uptake in the adrenal gland supports which diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: MIBG (a noradrenaline analog) is concentrated by catecholamine-storing chromaffin cells, so focal adrenal uptake indicates a pheochromocytoma. It is also useful for detecting extra-adrenal paragangliomas and metastatic disease.
mcqs.hint: Catecholamine-analog tracer lighting up the adrenal medulla tumor.

mcqs.q: What is the main advantage of MIBG scintigraphy over anatomic imaging in pheochromocytoma?
opts: Functional/whole-body localization of multifocal or metastatic catecholamine-secreting disease
mcqs.exp: MIBG provides functional, whole-body assessment, detecting additional or metastatic lesions that anatomic imaging may miss, and identifies MIBG-avid tumors suitable for ¹³¹I-MIBG therapy.
mcqs.hint: Function and whole-body coverage, not just anatomy.

mcqs.q: Certain medications can cause false-negative MIBG scans by blocking uptake. Which class is a classic example?
opts: Tricyclic antidepressants and certain antihypertensives (e.g., labetalol)
mcqs.exp: Drugs that interfere with the noradrenaline transporter or vesicular storage — tricyclic antidepressants, labetalol, reserpine, sympathomimetics — can reduce MIBG uptake and cause false negatives, and should be withheld before scanning.
mcqs.hint: Drugs affecting catecholamine handling can block the tracer.

title: Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT – Metastatic Pheochromocytoma
caption: A Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT for malignant pheochromocytoma demonstrating multiple metastatic lesions in bone and lymph nodes (arrows).

mcqs.q: Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT images pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma by targeting what?
opts: Somatostatin receptors expressed by neuroendocrine tumor cells
mcqs.exp: DOTATATE binds somatostatin receptors (especially subtype 2) that are highly expressed on many neuroendocrine tumors, including pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma. Ga-68 DOTATATE PET has excellent sensitivity for detecting metastatic and multifocal disease.
mcqs.hint: It's a somatostatin-receptor tracer.

mcqs.q: Multiple DOTATATE-avid lesions in bone and lymph nodes in a pheochromocytoma patient indicate what?
mcqs.exp: Uptake at sites where chromaffin tissue is not normally present (bone, lymph nodes, liver) defines metastatic malignant pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma. DOTATATE PET is often superior to MIBG for staging, especially in SDHx-related disease.
mcqs.hint: Tracer-avid bone and nodes = spread = malignant.

mcqs.q: A key therapeutic advantage of somatostatin-receptor-positive disease is eligibility for which treatment?
opts: Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (e.g., Lu-177 DOTATATE)
mcqs.exp: Somatostatin-receptor-positive neuroendocrine tumors, including metastatic pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma, may be treated with peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (Lu-177 DOTATATE), a targeted 'theranostic' option guided by the DOTATATE PET.
mcqs.hint: The same receptor lets you deliver targeted radiotherapy (PRRT).

title: Lipid-Rich Adenoma – Washout Worked Example
caption: Lipid-rich adenoma. (A) Unenhanced CT shows a left adrenal mass measuring −6 HU. (B) Contrast-enhanced CT at 60 s shows enhancement to 50 HU. (C) On delayed CT at 15 minutes the mass measures 15 HU. These measurements provide an absolute contrast enhancement washout of 63% and a relative washout of 70%, proving a lipid-rich adenoma.

mcqs.q: The unenhanced attenuation of −6 HU already establishes which diagnosis, even before washout is calculated?
mcqs.exp: An unenhanced value of −6 HU (well below 10 HU) is diagnostic of a lipid-rich adenoma due to abundant intracytoplasmic lipid. In practice, a value ≤10 HU is sufficient and washout is not even required.
mcqs.hint: Negative HU is comfortably under the 10-HU rule.

mcqs.q: Using unenhanced −6 HU, 60-s 50 HU, and 15-min delayed 15 HU, the absolute percentage washout is 63%. Which formula gives this?
mcqs.exp: APW = (enhanced − delayed)/(enhanced − unenhanced) × 100 = (50 − 15)/(50 − (−6)) × 100 ≈ 63%. It requires the unenhanced value in the denominator.
mcqs.hint: Numerator is the drop from peak; denominator subtracts the unenhanced value.

mcqs.q: The relative percentage washout (RPW) here is 70%. How is RPW calculated (no unenhanced value needed)?
mcqs.exp: RPW = (enhanced − delayed)/enhanced × 100 = (50 − 15)/50 × 100 = 70%. RPW is used when no unenhanced scan is available; the threshold for an adenoma is ≥40%.
mcqs.hint: Divide the drop by the enhanced value alone.

mcqs.q: APW of 63% and RPW of 70% both exceed their thresholds. What are those adenoma thresholds?
mcqs.exp: An absolute washout ≥60% and/or a relative washout ≥40% at 15 minutes indicate an adenoma. Both are exceeded here, confirming a benign adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Sixty and forty.

mcqs.q: Why do adenomas wash out contrast rapidly compared with malignancies?
opts: They have capillaries that both enhance and de-enhance quickly, unlike the disorganized vasculature of malignancy which retains contrast
mcqs.exp: Adenomas have a capillary network that enhances promptly and releases contrast rapidly (fast washout). Malignant lesions and pheochromocytomas have disorganized/leaky vasculature that retains contrast, giving lower washout.
mcqs.hint: Orderly vessels fill and empty fast; chaotic tumor vessels hold on to contrast.

title: Lipid-Poor Adenoma – Washout Worked Example
caption: Lipid-poor adenoma. (A) Unenhanced CT shows a right adrenal mass measuring 15 HU. (B) Contrast-enhanced CT at 60 s shows enhancement to 75 HU. (C) On delayed CT at 15 minutes the mass measures 25 HU. These measurements provide an absolute contrast enhancement washout of 83% and a relative washout of 67%, proving a lipid-poor adenoma.

mcqs.q: This mass measures 15 HU on unenhanced CT. Why can it NOT be called a lipid-rich adenoma at this stage?
opts: Because 15 HU exceeds the ≤10 HU threshold, making it lipid-poor/indeterminate
Because it is on the right
mcqs.exp: At 15 HU the lesion is above the 10-HU cutoff, so it is 'lipid-poor' and indeterminate on the unenhanced scan. A washout study is then needed to characterize it.
mcqs.hint: 15 is above the 10-HU line.

mcqs.q: With unenhanced 15 HU, 60-s 75 HU and 15-min 25 HU, the absolute washout is 83%. This value indicates:
mcqs.exp: APW = (75 − 25)/(75 − 15) × 100 ≈ 83%, well above the 60% threshold, so despite the higher unenhanced density this is a benign lipid-poor adenoma. Washout rescues the diagnosis when HU is indeterminate.
mcqs.hint: High washout means adenoma even without the low HU.

mcqs.q: The relative washout here is 67%. Which threshold does this exceed to support an adenoma?
mcqs.exp: RPW = (75 − 25)/75 × 100 ≈ 67%, above the ≥40% threshold, again supporting a benign adenoma. RPW is useful when no unenhanced scan is available.
mcqs.hint: The relative-washout cutoff is 40%.

mcqs.q: What is the practical value of the washout technique demonstrated by this case?
opts: It characterizes indeterminate (>10 HU) lesions as adenomas without biopsy
mcqs.exp: Washout CT allows confident, non-invasive diagnosis of many indeterminate (lipid-poor) adrenal masses as benign adenomas, avoiding biopsy or resection. It is a cornerstone of the incidentaloma work-up.
mcqs.hint: It turns an 'indeterminate' lesion into a confident benign call.

mcqs.q: Which lesions can occasionally mimic an adenoma by showing relatively high washout, and thus require caution?
mcqs.exp: Although washout is reliable, some hypervascular metastases (e.g., RCC, HCC) and pheochromocytomas can show relatively high washout, so washout results must be interpreted with clinical context, size and morphology.
mcqs.hint: Very vascular tumors can occasionally fake fast washout.

title: Benign vs Malignant Adrenal Mass – Imaging Criteria
caption: Non-specific anatomical imaging findings that help differentiate benign from malignant adrenal masses. Benign mass: size <4 cm, round/smooth margins, homogeneous, high lipid content (except lipid-poor adenomas), slow growth, CT density <10 HU (lipid-rich) or >10 HU (lipid-poor), early enhancement with early washout, absolute washout >60%, relative washout >40%, low T2 signal, adreno-splenic ratio <70%, signal-intensity index (chemical-shift signal loss) >5%. Malignant tumor (primary ACC or metastasis): size >4 cm, thick/irregular margins, heterogeneous, low lipid content, rapid growth, density >10 HU, variable enhancement with slow washout, absolute washout <60%, relative washout <40%, high T2 signal, adreno-splenic ratio >70%, signal-intensity index <5%.

mcqs.q: According to these criteria, which size threshold raises concern for malignancy in an adrenal mass?
mcqs.exp: A size >4 cm is a classic threshold that increases the likelihood of malignancy (ACC or metastasis), whereas most benign adenomas are <4 cm. Size is combined with other features, since some large lesions are still benign (e.g., myelolipoma).
mcqs.hint: The classic worrisome cut-off is four centimetres.

mcqs.q: Which absolute percentage washout (APW) value favors a benign adenoma versus malignancy?
mcqs.exp: An absolute washout >60% (and relative washout >40%) at 15 minutes favors a benign adenoma; lower washout suggests a malignant or indeterminate lesion needing further evaluation.
mcqs.hint: Above 60% is reassuring; below is worrying.

mcqs.q: On T2-weighted MRI, which signal pattern is generally associated with malignant adrenal masses?
mcqs.exp: Malignant lesions tend to show high (and heterogeneous) T2 signal, whereas benign adenomas are usually low-to-intermediate on T2. This is one nonspecific criterion combined with washout, chemical shift, size and margins.
mcqs.hint: Brighter T2 leans malignant.

mcqs.q: The chemical-shift 'signal-intensity index' (SII) measures signal loss on opposed-phase imaging. Which value indicates a benign lipid-rich adenoma?
mcqs.exp: A signal-intensity index >5% (i.e., significant opposed-phase signal loss) indicates intracytoplasmic lipid and a benign adenoma. Malignant/lipid-poor lesions show little signal drop (SII <5%).
mcqs.hint: More opposed-phase signal drop = more fat = benign.

mcqs.q: The adreno-splenic ratio (ASR) compares adrenal-to-spleen signal on opposed-phase. Which ASR favors a benign adenoma?
opts: ASR <70% (adrenal loses more signal than spleen)
mcqs.exp: An adreno-splenic ratio <70% indicates the adrenal lesion loses relatively more signal than the spleen on opposed-phase imaging, reflecting intracytoplasmic lipid and favoring a benign adenoma; ASR >70% suggests a lipid-poor/malignant lesion.
mcqs.hint: Lower ratio means the adrenal dropped out more than the spleen — that's fat.

title: Adrenal Myelolipoma – Macroscopic Fat
caption: Myelolipoma. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrating a large right adrenal mass containing a large amount of low-attenuation tissue of fatty density (arrow). This low attenuation is characteristic of myelolipomas; the attenuation of myelolipomas is usually between fat and water, and in this example the attenuation value was −15 HU.

mcqs.q: A −15 HU (frankly fatty) component within an adrenal mass is characteristic of which lesion?
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic fat (negative HU) within an adrenal mass is essentially diagnostic of a myelolipoma — a benign tumor of mature fat and hematopoietic tissue. Virtually no other adrenal lesion contains macroscopic fat.
mcqs.hint: Negative HU means real fat — think myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: How does the fat of a myelolipoma differ from the 'lipid' that makes an adenoma low-density?
opts: Myelolipoma has macroscopic fat; adenoma has microscopic intracytoplasmic lipid (not frank fat)
mcqs.exp: Myelolipomas contain macroscopic fat (like subcutaneous fat, negative HU, suppresses on fat-sat MRI). Adenomas contain microscopic intracytoplasmic lipid, giving low HU (≤10) and opposed-phase signal drop but not frank fat density.
mcqs.hint: Gross fat vs. fat inside the cells.

mcqs.q: What is the usual management of an incidentally found, asymptomatic adrenal myelolipoma?
opts: Observation, with surgery reserved for large, symptomatic, growing or hemorrhagic lesions
mcqs.exp: Myelolipomas are benign and non-functioning; most are simply observed. Surgery is considered for large (often >4–6 cm), symptomatic, rapidly growing, or hemorrhage-prone lesions.
mcqs.hint: Benign and quiet — watch unless big or bleeding.

title: Adenoma on MR Chemical-Shift Imaging
caption: Adenoma on MR chemical-shift imaging. Axial T1 in-phase image of a left adrenal mass (arrow). The axial T1 out-of-phase image demonstrates visible loss of signal intensity within the mass. This visible signal loss is characteristic of an adenoma.

mcqs.q: Visible signal loss on the out-of-phase (opposed-phase) image compared with in-phase indicates:
opts: Intracytoplasmic (microscopic) lipid, characteristic of an adenoma
mcqs.exp: Signal drop on opposed-phase imaging reflects coexisting fat and water within voxels (intracytoplasmic lipid), the hallmark of a benign adrenal adenoma. This is the MRI equivalent of a low unenhanced CT attenuation.
mcqs.hint: Opposed-phase dropout = lipid within the cells = adenoma.

mcqs.q: Which patients particularly benefit from chemical-shift MRI rather than CT washout for adrenal characterization?
opts: Those needing to avoid iodinated contrast/radiation, or with indeterminate lipid-poor lesions on CT
mcqs.exp: Chemical-shift MRI avoids ionizing radiation and iodinated contrast and can characterize lesions that are mildly lipid-poor on CT. It is especially useful in younger patients or those with contrast contraindications.
mcqs.hint: No radiation, no iodinated contrast — good for some patients.

mcqs.q: A lipid-POOR adenoma may show little or no opposed-phase signal drop. What is the next best step then?
opts: CT washout study (lipid-poor adenomas still show high washout)
mcqs.exp: Some adenomas contain little lipid and do not drop out on chemical shift. A CT washout study can still confirm them as adenomas because they wash out contrast rapidly (APW ≥60% / RPW ≥40%).
mcqs.hint: If it won't drop on MRI, check its washout on CT.

title: Left Adrenal Carcinoma with Calcification
caption: Left adrenal carcinoma. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrating the characteristic large heterogeneous mass with areas of calcification (arrows), suggesting the diagnosis of a carcinoma.

mcqs.q: Which combination of features shown here is most suggestive of adrenocortical carcinoma?
opts: Large, heterogeneous mass with internal calcification
mcqs.exp: A large, heterogeneous adrenal mass with internal calcification is suspicious for adrenocortical carcinoma. Calcification occurs in roughly a third of ACCs and, combined with size and heterogeneity, raises malignant concern.
mcqs.hint: Large, heterogeneous and calcified points to cancer.

mcqs.q: Approximately how often do adrenocortical carcinomas contain calcification?
mcqs.exp: Calcification is seen in roughly 30% of ACCs. While not specific, its presence in a large heterogeneous mass supports malignancy. Note that calcification can also occur in benign lesions (old hemorrhage, cysts, granulomas).
mcqs.hint: Roughly a third of these cancers calcify.

mcqs.q: What staging feature should be specifically sought on CT/MRI in a suspected left adrenocortical carcinoma?
opts: Local invasion, venous (renal vein/IVC) tumor thrombus, nodal and distant metastases
mcqs.exp: Assessment for local organ invasion, venous tumor thrombus, lymphadenopathy and distant metastases is essential for staging and surgical planning of ACC. These aggressive features distinguish carcinoma from benign lesions.
mcqs.hint: Look for invasion, vein thrombus and spread.

title: Pheochromocytoma – No Chemical-Shift Drop
caption: Adrenal pheochromocytoma on MR chemical-shift imaging. Axial T1 in-phase image of a left heterogeneous adrenal mass (arrow). The axial T1 out-of-phase image demonstrates NO loss of signal intensity within or in the periphery of the mass. The adrenal mass is hence an indeterminate (non-adenoma) mass on chemical-shift imaging — a surgically confirmed pheochromocytoma.

mcqs.q: The absence of signal drop on opposed-phase imaging tells you the lesion:
mcqs.exp: No opposed-phase signal loss means little intracytoplasmic lipid, so a benign lipid-rich adenoma is excluded. The lesion is indeterminate and includes lipid-poor adenoma, pheochromocytoma, metastasis or carcinoma in the differential.
mcqs.hint: No dropout = not a lipid-rich adenoma.

mcqs.q: For a heterogeneous, lipid-poor adrenal mass that does not drop out on chemical shift, which test is essential before any biopsy or surgery?
mcqs.exp: Because pheochromocytoma is in the differential for a lipid-poor mass, metanephrines must be checked before intervention to avoid a catecholamine crisis — as this surgically confirmed pheochromocytoma illustrates.
mcqs.hint: Rule out the catecholamine tumor before touching it.

mcqs.q: This case highlights an important limitation of chemical-shift MRI. What is it?
opts: It only identifies lipid-containing (adenomatous) lesions; lipid-poor benign and malignant lesions look alike (indeterminate)
mcqs.exp: Chemical-shift MRI confirms lipid-rich adenomas but cannot reliably distinguish among lipid-poor lesions (lipid-poor adenoma, pheochromocytoma, metastasis, carcinoma). Such indeterminate lesions need washout CT, biochemistry, PET or biopsy.
mcqs.hint: It's great for fatty adenomas, blind among the lipid-poor lesions.

title: Adrenal Metastasis – Breast Cancer
caption: Adrenal metastases. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrating a large right-sided adrenal metastasis (arrowhead) and multiple liver metastases (arrows) in a patient with disseminated breast cancer.

mcqs.q: In a patient with disseminated cancer, a right adrenal mass alongside multiple liver lesions most likely represents:
mcqs.exp: The adrenal glands are among the most common sites of hematogenous metastasis. In a patient with widespread disease (here breast cancer with liver metastases), a new indeterminate adrenal mass is presumed metastatic until proven otherwise.
mcqs.hint: Widespread cancer + new adrenal mass = likely a met.

mcqs.q: Which primary cancers most commonly metastasize to the adrenal glands?
opts: Lung, breast, melanoma, renal and gastrointestinal cancers
mcqs.exp: Common primaries metastasizing to the adrenal include lung, breast, melanoma, renal cell and GI cancers. Knowing the primary helps interpret an indeterminate adrenal lesion in an oncology patient.
mcqs.hint: Lung and breast top the list, plus melanoma and renal.

mcqs.q: When an adrenal lesion is the only site that would change management in a cancer patient, what is the best step if imaging is indeterminate?
opts: Characterize with washout CT/MRI or FDG-PET, and biopsy if still indeterminate (after excluding pheochromocytoma)
mcqs.exp: If distinguishing a solitary metastasis from a benign adenoma alters staging/treatment, characterize with washout CT/chemical-shift MRI or FDG-PET, and biopsy if still uncertain — always after excluding pheochromocytoma biochemically.
mcqs.hint: When it matters, characterize thoroughly, then biopsy if needed.

title: Pheochromocytoma – MIBG-Negative, FDG-Positive
caption: Pheochromocytoma. Whole-body ¹²³I-MIBG SPECT (anterior and posterior views) shows no abnormal radionuclide uptake. Reprojected FDG-PET of the same patient shows high heterogeneous uptake in a large right adrenal tumor (arrow).

mcqs.q: This pheochromocytoma showed no MIBG uptake but intense FDG uptake. What does a negative MIBG scan NOT do?
mcqs.exp: A negative MIBG scan does not exclude pheochromocytoma — some tumors (especially certain hereditary/aggressive ones) are MIBG-poor. FDG-PET can detect these MIBG-negative tumors, so functional imaging is complementary.
mcqs.hint: MIBG-negative does not mean 'no pheo'.

mcqs.q: For an MIBG-negative but strongly FDG-avid pheochromocytoma, which underlying genetics is classically implicated?
mcqs.exp: SDHx-related (especially SDHB) pheochromocytomas/paragangliomas are typically MIBG-poor but FDG-avid due to a pseudohypoxic glycolytic phenotype. This pattern should prompt germline testing.
mcqs.hint: The 'FDG-hot, MIBG-cold' pattern flags an SDH gene.

mcqs.q: What is the practical imaging lesson from this MIBG-negative, FDG-positive case?
opts: Combining functional tracers (MIBG, FDG, DOTATATE) improves detection because tumors vary in tracer avidity
mcqs.exp: Because pheochromocytomas/paragangliomas vary in tracer uptake, using complementary functional imaging (MIBG, FDG-PET, Ga-68 DOTATATE) improves sensitivity, particularly for metastatic or hereditary disease.
mcqs.hint: Different tumors light up with different tracers — use more than one.

title: Pheochromocytoma – MIBG and ¹⁸F-Fluorodopamine PET
caption: Pheochromocytoma. Whole-body ¹²³I-MIBG SPECT (anterior and posterior views) shows radionuclide uptake in the right adrenal (arrow). Reprojected ¹⁸F-fluorodopamine (FDA) PET of the same patient shows high homogeneous uptake in the large right adrenal tumor (arrow).

mcqs.q: ¹⁸F-fluorodopamine (FDA) PET localizes pheochromocytoma because it is a substrate for which transport system?
opts: The catecholamine (noradrenaline) uptake and storage system
mcqs.exp: ¹⁸F-fluorodopamine is a catecholamine analog taken up and stored by chromaffin cells via the noradrenaline transporter/vesicular system, giving specific, high-contrast localization of pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma.
mcqs.hint: It's a catecholamine (dopamine) analog, like MIBG but PET.

mcqs.q: What is an advantage of PET-based catecholamine tracers (e.g., ¹⁸F-FDA, ¹⁸F-FDOPA) over ¹²³I-MIBG SPECT?
opts: Higher spatial resolution and sensitivity, with faster imaging
mcqs.exp: PET tracers offer higher spatial resolution and sensitivity than MIBG SPECT and allow same-day imaging, improving detection of small or metastatic lesions.
mcqs.hint: PET generally beats SPECT on resolution and speed.

mcqs.q: In this case both MIBG and FDA localized the tumor. What general principle does concordant functional imaging provide?
opts: Concordant uptake increases diagnostic confidence and helps select theranostic therapy
mcqs.exp: Concordant uptake across catecholamine-specific tracers strengthens the diagnosis and, importantly, MIBG avidity identifies candidates for ¹³¹I-MIBG therapy, linking diagnosis to treatment.
mcqs.hint: Agreement boosts confidence and can guide targeted therapy.

title: Adenoma MRI Signal & Signal-Intensity Index
caption: Adenomas characteristically reveal homogeneous signal intensity on all MRI pulse sequences, with relative isointensity to liver on T2-weighted images. The amount of intracellular lipid corresponds to the amount of signal intensity lost on chemical-shift MRI. A diagnosis of adenoma can be made if the signal-intensity index is above 20%. Myelolipoma is an uncommon benign tumor composed of fat and hematopoietic tissue in varying proportions; the presence of macroscopic fat is critical to the diagnosis because virtually no other adrenal lesion contains fat.

mcqs.q: According to this text, a diagnosis of adrenal adenoma on chemical-shift MRI can be made when the signal-intensity index is:
mcqs.exp: A signal-intensity index (SII) above 20% (marked opposed-phase signal loss) indicates significant intracytoplasmic lipid and supports a benign adenoma. Different sources cite thresholds around 16.5–20%; higher drop = more confidence in adenoma.
mcqs.hint: The stated cutoff here is twenty percent.

mcqs.q: On T2-weighted MRI, adenomas are characteristically described as:
mcqs.exp: Adenomas are typically homogeneous and relatively isointense to liver on T2, unlike the markedly bright pheochromocytoma or heterogeneous, higher-signal malignancies.
mcqs.hint: Quiet and liver-like on T2, not bright.

mcqs.q: Why is the presence of macroscopic fat so useful for diagnosing a myelolipoma?
opts: Because fat is common in many adrenal tumors
Because virtually no other adrenal lesion contains macroscopic fat, making it nearly specific
Because fat suppresses on all sequences
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic fat is essentially unique to myelolipoma among adrenal masses, so identifying frank fat (negative HU, fat-sat dropout) is nearly diagnostic. This lets radiologists confidently avoid further work-up for these benign lesions.
mcqs.hint: Fat in the adrenal almost always means one specific benign tumor.

title: Adrenocortical Carcinoma – MRI Signal Characteristics
caption: Adrenal carcinoma has heterogeneous low signal on T1-weighted MRI and heterogeneous high signal on T2-weighted images. Some masses may lose focal signal intensity on chemical-shift imaging (due to small foci of intracytoplasmic lipid within the tumor).

mcqs.q: On MRI, adrenocortical carcinoma is typically described as:
opts: Heterogeneous low signal on T1 and heterogeneous high signal on T2
mcqs.exp: ACCs are heterogeneous (necrosis, hemorrhage) with low-to-intermediate T1 and high T2 signal, in contrast to the homogeneous, liver-isointense appearance of benign adenomas.
mcqs.hint: Heterogeneous, dark-ish on T1 and bright on T2.

mcqs.q: Why can some adrenocortical carcinomas show FOCAL signal loss on chemical-shift imaging, and why is that a pitfall?
opts: They can contain small foci of intracytoplasmic lipid, so partial signal drop does not guarantee a benign adenoma
mcqs.exp: Because ACCs may harbor scattered lipid-rich cells, focal opposed-phase signal loss can occur and mimic an adenoma. Signal drop should be interpreted alongside size, heterogeneity, margins and washout — not in isolation.
mcqs.hint: A little dropout can be a trap in a big, ugly mass.

mcqs.q: Which overall feature set best separates ACC from a benign adenoma on MRI?
opts: Large size, heterogeneity, irregular margins, high T2 signal and possible vascular invasion
mcqs.exp: The gestalt of a large, heterogeneous, irregularly marginated, T2-bright mass — potentially invading vessels — indicates ACC. Any incidental focal lipid should not override these malignant features.
mcqs.hint: Judge the whole picture, not one lipid focus.

title: Adrenal Lymphoma & Metastases – Imaging Principles
caption: CT and MRI findings of adrenal lymphoma are nonspecific and definitive diagnosis requires histology. CT shows soft-tissue masses that enlarge or replace the gland; contrast enhancement resembles retroperitoneal adenopathy; masses show low T1 and heterogeneous high T2 signal. Adrenal metastases arise from primaries that spread — commonly lung, breast and renal cell carcinomas, and melanoma — and are highly variable in size and appearance.

mcqs.q: Why does adrenal lymphoma require biopsy for definitive diagnosis?
opts: Its CT/MRI features are nonspecific and overlap with other soft-tissue masses
mcqs.exp: Adrenal lymphoma's imaging (soft-tissue masses replacing the gland, enhancement like nodal disease, low T1/high T2) is nonspecific and overlaps with metastasis and other tumors, so histology is needed to confirm and subtype it.
mcqs.hint: Nonspecific pictures → tissue needed.

mcqs.q: Which primaries most commonly cause adrenal metastases?
opts: Lung, breast, renal cell carcinoma and melanoma
mcqs.exp: Lung, breast and renal cell carcinomas and melanoma are the classic primaries that metastasize to the adrenal glands. This informs the differential when a cancer patient has an indeterminate adrenal mass.
mcqs.hint: Lung, breast, renal, melanoma.

mcqs.q: A useful distinguishing feature is that adrenal metastases (with a notable exception) do NOT lose signal on chemical-shift MRI. Which metastasis is the classic exception?
opts: Clear-cell renal carcinoma metastasis (can contain lipid)
mcqs.exp: Most metastases are lipid-poor and do not drop out on opposed-phase imaging. Clear-cell renal cell carcinoma metastases are a classic exception because they can contain intracytoplasmic lipid and mimic an adenoma with signal drop.
mcqs.hint: The fatty-cell kidney cancer can fake an adenoma.

title: Incidentaloma Diagnostic Strategy (Unenhanced HU)
caption: Diagnostic strategy for an adrenal incidentaloma on unenhanced CT. If <−20 HU: myelolipoma. If >−10 and <10 HU: no intralesional enhancement = adrenal cyst; lesion enhancement = lipid-rich adenoma. If >10 HU: assess washout — enhancement washout >60% (or relative washout >40%) = lipid-poor adenoma; if not, further diagnostic work-up is necessary. Metastatic disease is variable on MRI (usually low T1, high T2) and does not lose signal on chemical-shift imaging, with the rare exception of clear-cell renal carcinoma metastases. Metastases cannot be excluded in grossly normal/hyperplastic-appearing glands.

mcqs.q: Per this strategy, an unenhanced adrenal lesion measuring less than −20 HU is:
mcqs.exp: Attenuation below −20 HU indicates macroscopic fat, essentially diagnostic of a myelolipoma. No other adrenal lesion reliably contains gross fat.
mcqs.hint: Very negative HU = frank fat = one benign tumor.

mcqs.q: For a lesion between −10 and +10 HU, what distinguishes a cyst from a lipid-rich adenoma?
opts: Intralesional enhancement (adenoma enhances; cyst does not)
mcqs.exp: In the low-HU range, a lesion with NO intralesional enhancement is a cyst (water density, no vascular tissue), whereas one that enhances is a lipid-rich adenoma. Enhancement indicates viable vascularized tissue.
mcqs.hint: Cyst = no enhancement; adenoma = enhances.

mcqs.q: For a lesion >10 HU on unenhanced CT, what washout result confirms a lipid-poor adenoma?
mcqs.exp: For lesions above 10 HU, an absolute washout >60% (or relative >40%) at 15 minutes indicates a lipid-poor adenoma. If washout criteria are not met, further work-up (PET, biopsy, follow-up, resection) is necessary.
mcqs.hint: Sixty absolute / forty relative confirms adenoma.

mcqs.q: The strategy warns that metastases 'cannot be ruled out from glands that appear grossly normal or hyperplastic.' What is the clinical implication?
opts: Micrometastatic involvement can exist without a discrete mass, so clinical context and follow-up matter
mcqs.exp: Metastatic deposits may not form a discrete measurable mass, so a normal-appearing gland does not exclude micrometastasis. In high-risk oncology patients, clinical judgment, PET and follow-up are important.
mcqs.hint: No visible lump doesn't guarantee no disease.

mcqs.q: Most adrenal metastases do NOT drop signal on chemical-shift MRI. Which is the rare exception that can?
mcqs.exp: Clear-cell RCC metastases can contain intracytoplasmic lipid and therefore lose signal on opposed-phase imaging, mimicking an adenoma — a well-known pitfall to remember in RCC patients.
mcqs.hint: The lipid-rich kidney cancer is the exception.

title: Pheochromocytoma – CT/MRI Characteristics
caption: Pheochromocytoma is seen on CT as a soft-tissue adrenal lesion, usually larger than 3 cm, with a central area of hemorrhagic necrosis. Exuberant and persistent enhancement is observed after IV contrast, typical of a hypervascular tumor. Calcifications, generally thin and peripheral, are present in about 10% of cases.

mcqs.q: Which enhancement behaviour is typical of a pheochromocytoma on contrast CT?
opts: Only rim enhancement of a fatty mass
mcqs.exp: Pheochromocytomas are hypervascular and show avid, persistent enhancement, often with central hemorrhagic necrosis in larger lesions. Persistent enhancement means washout is usually low, unlike an adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Very vascular, lights up strongly and holds contrast.

mcqs.q: Approximately what proportion of pheochromocytomas show calcification, and what pattern is typical?
mcqs.exp: Roughly 10% of pheochromocytomas calcify, usually with thin peripheral calcification. Calcification is therefore a supportive but nonspecific finding.
mcqs.hint: About one in ten, and it's a thin peripheral rim.

mcqs.q: A soft-tissue adrenal lesion >3 cm with central necrosis and avid enhancement should prompt which mandatory step before biopsy/surgery?
opts: Biochemical exclusion of pheochromocytoma (metanephrines) and alpha-blockade if positive
mcqs.exp: Such a lesion may be a pheochromocytoma; metanephrines must be measured before any intervention, and alpha-adrenergic blockade with volume expansion instituted if positive, to prevent a catecholamine crisis.
mcqs.hint: Test metanephrines and alpha-block before touching it.

title: Left Adrenal Nodule – 45 HU on Non-Contrast CT
caption: A well-defined homogeneous nodular mass lesion is seen in the left adrenal gland with an attenuation of approximately 45 HU on the non-contrast scan.

mcqs.q: A well-defined homogeneous left adrenal nodule measures ~45 HU on unenhanced CT. How should this be classified?
opts: Indeterminate (lipid-poor) — cannot be called a benign adenoma on density alone
mcqs.exp: At ~45 HU the lesion is well above the 10-HU threshold, so it is indeterminate/lipid-poor and cannot be confirmed as a benign lipid-rich adenoma on the unenhanced scan alone. Further characterization (washout CT, chemical shift, biochemistry) is required.
mcqs.hint: 45 HU is far above 10 — not a straightforward adenoma.

mcqs.q: What is the most appropriate next imaging step for this indeterminate 45-HU nodule?
mcqs.exp: A dedicated washout CT (unenhanced, portovenous, 15-minute delayed) — or chemical-shift MRI — characterizes the lesion. High washout (APW ≥60%/RPW ≥40%) would indicate a lipid-poor adenoma; poor washout warrants further work-up.
mcqs.hint: Measure how fast it washes out contrast.

mcqs.q: Alongside imaging, what should always be done for a newly discovered adrenal nodule?
opts: Biochemical screening for hormone excess and exclusion of pheochromocytoma
Repeat CT in 5 years without tests
mcqs.exp: Every adrenal incidentaloma needs functional assessment: overnight dexamethasone suppression (cortisol), metanephrines (pheochromocytoma), and aldosterone-to-renin ratio if hypertensive/hypokalemic. This guides management regardless of imaging appearance.
mcqs.hint: Always test what the nodule might be secreting.

title: Large Left Adrenal Mass with Fat and Calcification
caption: A large heterogeneous mass likely arising from the left adrenal gland measuring 78 × 58 mm contains fat and calcification. There is a bulky left ovary. No suspicious lesion was detected in the liver, spleen, kidneys, pancreas or right adrenal; no significant lymphadenopathy; no free fluid; lower lung sections unremarkable; no suspicious bony lesion.

mcqs.q: A large adrenal mass containing macroscopic fat and calcification is most consistent with which benign diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic fat within an adrenal mass strongly favors a myelolipoma, which can also calcify. The presence of gross fat is near-diagnostic because virtually no other adrenal lesion contains macroscopic fat.
mcqs.hint: Gross fat (± calcification) in the adrenal = myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: Given its size (78 × 58 mm), which complication of this fatty adrenal mass is the main concern?
mcqs.exp: Large myelolipomas (often >4–6 cm) carry a risk of spontaneous hemorrhage or rupture. This size-related bleeding risk, or symptoms/growth, is what prompts consideration of resection.
mcqs.hint: The bigger the fatty mass, the higher the bleed risk.

mcqs.q: The report notes a bulky left ovary but no metastases or nodes. Why is this 'staging-style' negative survey still valuable for a benign fatty adrenal mass?
opts: It confirms an isolated benign process and documents an incidental finding needing separate follow-up
mcqs.exp: A clear survey supports an isolated benign lesion (no metastatic disease) and flags an unrelated incidental finding (bulky ovary) for appropriate separate evaluation. Comprehensive reporting avoids missing concurrent pathology.
mcqs.hint: A clean survey supports benignity and catches incidental findings.

title: Adrenocortical Adenoma – T1 In-Phase MRI
caption: Adrenocortical adenoma (ACA) in a 53-year-old woman with diabetes and hypertension. Axial T1-weighted IN-PHASE MR image shows a hypointense left adrenal mass (arrowhead) with mild heterogeneity. (On the corresponding out-of-phase image there is marked signal loss confirming increased intravoxel fat-to-water ratio; the gross specimen showed cystic change and hemorrhage, and histology showed lipid-rich cells with rich vascular supply.)

mcqs.q: On the T1 IN-PHASE image, this adenoma appears mildly heterogeneous and hypointense. What is the role of the in-phase image in chemical-shift MRI?
opts: It is the baseline (fat+water additive) against which the opposed-phase image is compared for signal drop
mcqs.exp: The in-phase image (fat and water signals add) serves as the reference. Comparing it with the opposed-phase image reveals the signal drop that indicates intracytoplasmic lipid — the hallmark of an adenoma.
mcqs.hint: In-phase is the 'before' picture for measuring dropout.

mcqs.q: This adenoma occurred in a 53-year-old with diabetes and hypertension. Why is biochemical screening still important in an imaging-benign adenoma?
opts: Adenomas can autonomously secrete cortisol/aldosterone, contributing to hypertension, diabetes and metabolic disease
mcqs.exp: Even a benign-appearing adenoma may show autonomous cortisol secretion (mild autonomous cortisol secretion/subclinical Cushing) or aldosterone excess, which can drive hypertension, dysglycemia and cardiovascular risk. Screening (dexamethasone suppression, ARR) is therefore essential.
mcqs.hint: A benign look doesn't rule out silent hormone secretion.

mcqs.q: The gross specimen of this adenoma showed cystic change and hemorrhage. What does this teach about adenoma appearances?
opts: Adenomas can show mild heterogeneity (cystic/hemorrhagic change) yet still be benign
mcqs.exp: While adenomas are usually homogeneous, degenerative cystic change or hemorrhage can produce mild heterogeneity. Interpreting heterogeneity requires the whole picture (size, lipid content, washout), not heterogeneity alone.
mcqs.hint: Some internal change is compatible with a benign adenoma.

title: Adrenocortical Adenoma – T1 Out-of-Phase MRI
caption: Adrenocortical adenoma (ACA) in a 53-year-old woman with diabetes and hypertension. Axial T1-weighted OUT-OF-PHASE MR image shows a marked decrease in signal intensity (arrowhead) in the posterior aspect of the mass, confirming an increased intravoxel fat-to-water ratio (intracytoplasmic lipid). Histology confirmed a lipid-rich adenoma.

mcqs.q: The marked signal DROP on this out-of-phase image (versus in-phase) confirms which finding?
opts: Increased intravoxel (intracytoplasmic) lipid — diagnostic of a lipid-rich adenoma
mcqs.exp: Signal loss on opposed-phase relative to in-phase indicates coexisting fat and water within voxels (intracytoplasmic lipid), the defining MRI feature of a benign lipid-rich adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Opposed-phase dropout = lipid inside the cells = adenoma.

mcqs.q: Which cause of intracytoplasmic lipid drop would this NOT be — i.e., what appearance would indicate macroscopic fat instead?
opts: Signal loss on FAT-SATURATED sequences (indicating gross fat, e.g., myelolipoma)
mcqs.exp: Intracytoplasmic (microscopic) lipid drops on opposed-phase imaging (adenoma). Macroscopic fat instead nulls on fat-saturated sequences (myelolipoma). Distinguishing the two determines the diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: Microscopic lipid → opposed-phase; gross fat → fat-sat dropout.

mcqs.q: How does this MRI signal-drop finding correlate with the unenhanced CT appearance of the same adenoma?
opts: It corresponds to low unenhanced attenuation (≤10 HU) from the same intracytoplasmic lipid
It corresponds to macroscopic fat (<−20 HU)
mcqs.exp: The intracytoplasmic lipid that causes opposed-phase signal loss on MRI is the same lipid that lowers unenhanced CT attenuation to ≤10 HU. CT and MRI thus provide concordant evidence of a lipid-rich adenoma.
mcqs.hint: The MRI dropout and the low CT HU come from the same fat.

title: Unilateral Nodular Cortical Hyperplasia – In-Phase MRI
caption: Adrenal cortical hyperplasia (ACH) in a 33-year-old man with multidrug-resistant arterial hypertension and hypokalemia due to unilateral nodular cortical hyperplasia. Preoperative axial T1-weighted IN-PHASE MR image shows a small, homogeneous, intermediate-signal left adrenal mass (arrowhead). (The out-of-phase image showed a significant signal drop, and histology confirmed unilateral nodular cortical hyperplasia.)

mcqs.q: A young man with resistant hypertension and hypokalemia and a left adrenal lesion most likely has which functional disorder?
mcqs.exp: Resistant hypertension with hypokalemia in a young patient is the classic presentation of primary aldosteronism. Both an aldosterone-producing adenoma and unilateral nodular cortical hyperplasia can cause it, and adrenal venous sampling helps lateralize the source.
mcqs.hint: Resistant hypertension + low potassium in a young adult.

mcqs.q: This lesion was nodular cortical hyperplasia, not an adenoma, yet showed intracytoplasmic lipid. What does this illustrate?
opts: There is imaging overlap between nodular cortical hyperplasia and adenoma (both can contain lipid)
Hyperplasia is always bilateral and diffuse only
mcqs.exp: Nodular cortical hyperplasia can contain lipid-rich cells and mimic an adenoma on chemical-shift MRI (signal drop). This overlap means imaging alone may not distinguish them; functional testing and AVS guide management.
mcqs.hint: Hyperplastic nodules can look just like adenomas.

mcqs.q: Why is adrenal venous sampling (AVS) particularly relevant in this case?
opts: To lateralize aldosterone secretion, since a visible nodule may not be the true or only source
mcqs.exp: In primary aldosteronism, especially in patients >35–40 years or with nodular hyperplasia, a visible nodule may not be the culprit and disease may be bilateral. AVS lateralizes secretion and determines whether unilateral adrenalectomy will help.
mcqs.hint: Prove which side is actually oversecreting before surgery.

title: Unilateral Nodular Cortical Hyperplasia – Out-of-Phase MRI
caption: Adrenal cortical hyperplasia (ACH) in a 33-year-old man. Preoperative axial T1-weighted OUT-OF-PHASE MR image shows a significant drop in signal intensity (arrowhead), consistent with intracytoplasmic fat, underscoring the radiologic overlap that can occur in the spectrum of adrenal cortical nodular hyperplasia and adenoma. Photomicrograph confirmed rare unilateral nodular cortical hyperplasia.

mcqs.q: The out-of-phase signal drop here indicates intracytoplasmic lipid. Why does this NOT prove the lesion is an adenoma?
opts: Because nodular cortical hyperplasia can also contain lipid and drop signal, overlapping with adenoma
mcqs.exp: Chemical-shift signal drop reflects intracytoplasmic lipid, which is present in both lipid-rich adenomas AND lipid-containing cortical hyperplasia. Thus signal drop confirms benign cortical (lipid-rich) tissue but does not by itself distinguish adenoma from hyperplasia.
mcqs.hint: Both benign cortical entities can have the fat that drops out.

mcqs.q: Given the overlap, what determines management in this patient with primary aldosteronism?
opts: Biochemical confirmation plus adrenal venous sampling to lateralize, then targeted surgery vs. medical therapy
mcqs.exp: Because imaging cannot separate a functioning adenoma from functioning hyperplasia, biochemical confirmation and AVS lateralization guide the choice between unilateral adrenalectomy and mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist therapy.
mcqs.hint: Function and sampling, not the pictures, drive the plan.

mcqs.q: What is the fundamental physical principle that produces the opposed-phase signal loss seen here?
opts: Cancellation of fat and water proton signals within the same voxel at the opposed-phase echo time
mcqs.exp: At the opposed-phase echo time, fat and water protons in the same voxel are out of phase and their signals cancel, causing signal loss where microscopic lipid coexists with water — the basis of chemical-shift imaging.
mcqs.hint: Fat and water signals cancel in mixed voxels.

title: Macronodular Hyperplasia – ACTH-Independent Cushing
caption: ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome due to macronodular hyperplasia with marked adrenal enlargement (MHMAE) in a 46-year-old woman. Preoperative axial contrast-enhanced CT shows bilateral hypo- to isoattenuating macronodules (arrowheads) throughout both adrenals, with marked abdominal subcutaneous fat deposition (stars) due to Cushing syndrome. Sectioned bilateral adrenalectomy specimens showed multiple yellow-to-tan macronodules replacing the cortex; the adrenals weighed >100 g combined.

mcqs.q: Bilateral macronodular adrenal enlargement causing Cushing syndrome with SUPPRESSED ACTH is classified as:
mcqs.exp: Bilateral macronodular adrenal hyperplasia (BMAH/MHMAE) autonomously secretes cortisol independent of ACTH, so plasma ACTH is suppressed. It is an adrenal (ACTH-independent) cause of Cushing syndrome, alongside adenoma, carcinoma and PPNAD.
mcqs.hint: The adrenals act autonomously; ACTH is low.

mcqs.q: The combined adrenal weight exceeded 100 g. What is the normal combined weight of the adrenal glands, for comparison?
mcqs.exp: Normal adrenals weigh only ~4–6 g each (roughly 8–12 g combined). A combined weight >100 g reflects the massive nodular enlargement characteristic of macronodular hyperplasia.
mcqs.hint: Normal glands are tiny — under a dozen grams together.

mcqs.q: The marked abdominal subcutaneous fat deposition (stars) is a manifestation of what?
opts: Cortisol excess (Cushing syndrome) causing central fat redistribution
mcqs.exp: Chronic cortisol excess causes central/truncal obesity with increased subcutaneous and visceral fat, a moon face, and a dorsocervical fat pad. The imaging fat deposition mirrors the clinical Cushingoid phenotype.
mcqs.hint: Cortisol excess redistributes fat centrally.

mcqs.q: How does macronodular hyperplasia differ morphologically from ACTH-dependent (pituitary) Cushing hyperplasia?
opts: Macronodular hyperplasia shows large bilateral nodules that grossly enlarge/replace the cortex, whereas ACTH-dependent hyperplasia is diffuse (later nodular)
mcqs.exp: BMAH/MHMAE shows large, multiple macronodules distorting both glands with suppressed ACTH. ACTH-dependent hyperplasia begins diffuse and symmetric (becoming nodular over time) with inappropriately normal/high ACTH.
mcqs.hint: Big autonomous nodules vs. diffuse ACTH-driven enlargement.

mcqs.q: What is the typical treatment for symptomatic ACTH-independent macronodular hyperplasia causing overt Cushing syndrome?
opts: Bilateral (or unilateral in selected cases) adrenalectomy, with steroid replacement as needed
mcqs.exp: Because the disease is adrenal and bilateral, treatment is surgical adrenalectomy (bilateral, or unilateral of the larger gland in selected cases) with appropriate glucocorticoid (± mineralocorticoid) replacement afterward. Pituitary-directed therapy is inappropriate as ACTH is suppressed.
mcqs.hint: The problem is in the adrenals, so remove adrenal tissue.

title: Bilateral Adrenal Hemorrhage – Critically Ill Patient
caption: Adrenal hemorrhage in a 62-year-old man hospitalized for severe brain infarction with upper gastrointestinal bleeding during his hospital course. Axial post-contrast CT shows bilateral homogeneously hyperattenuating adrenal enlargement (arrowheads), consistent with hemorrhage. Postmortem gross specimen shows marked fresh hemorrhage throughout the gland, centered in the medulla.

mcqs.q: Bilateral homogeneously hyperattenuating adrenal enlargement in a critically ill, stressed patient indicates what?
mcqs.exp: Bilateral high-attenuation (hyperdense) adrenal enlargement in a stressed/critically ill patient indicates adrenal hemorrhage. Severe illness, coagulopathy, sepsis and stress are recognized precipitants.
mcqs.hint: Bilateral dense swollen glands in a sick patient = bleeding.

mcqs.q: The gross specimen showed hemorrhage centered in the medulla. Why does adrenal hemorrhage often begin centrally?
opts: The cortex has no blood supply
The rich arterial supply drains into a limited medullary venous plexus, creating a vascular 'dam' prone to congestion and hemorrhage
mcqs.exp: The adrenal has abundant arterial inflow but drains through a limited venous system converging in the medulla, forming a relative vascular bottleneck. Under stress or with venous thrombosis, congestion and hemorrhage characteristically start in the medulla.
mcqs.hint: Lots of arteries in, few veins out — pressure builds in the middle.

mcqs.q: What life-threatening complication must be anticipated with bilateral adrenal hemorrhage?
mcqs.exp: Bilateral adrenal hemorrhage can abruptly destroy cortical function, causing acute adrenal insufficiency. In a deteriorating critically ill patient, recognizing this and giving stress-dose glucocorticoids can be life-saving.
mcqs.hint: Both glands bleeding out → sudden cortisol failure.

title: Organizing Adrenal Hematoma – Ultrasound
caption: Adrenal hemorrhage in a 72-year-old woman with hypertension and a known stable 2.3-cm right adrenal mass (most consistent with an adenoma).
