Imaging 10 years later showed interval enlargement to 8 cm; workup was negative for stress, trauma, anticoagulation, infection or urine metanephrines. Post-resection pathology confirmed an organizing hematoma; given its size it was not conclusive whether the bleeding arose from the adenoma or another cause. Sonographic image shows a well-circumscribed suprarenal mass with heterogeneous echotexture and peripherally hyperechoic areas (arrowheads).

mcqs.q: A previously stable 2.3-cm adrenal adenoma that enlarges to 8 cm over 10 years, proven to be an organizing hematoma, illustrates which important point?
opts: An adenoma can bleed, and hemorrhage into an adrenal mass can cause marked interval enlargement
mcqs.exp: Adrenal adenomas (and other masses) can hemorrhage, producing striking interval growth that mimics malignancy. Here the enlarging mass was an organizing hematoma, underscoring hemorrhage as a benign cause of adrenal mass enlargement.
mcqs.hint: Sudden growth can be a bleed, not cancer.

mcqs.q: On ultrasound, this organizing hematoma appears as a well-circumscribed suprarenal mass with heterogeneous echotexture. Why is ultrasound generally limited for adrenal characterization?
opts: It cannot show any adrenal mass
It cannot measure attenuation/washout or reliably characterize tissue, so CT/MRI are preferred
It is more accurate than CT
mcqs.exp: Ultrasound can detect adrenal masses but cannot measure HU, washout or chemical-shift lipid, and the retroperitoneal location is often hard to image. CT and MRI are the mainstays of characterization.
mcqs.hint: US shows it but can't measure density or washout.

mcqs.q: The workup here (negative for stress, trauma, anticoagulation, infection, metanephrines) reflects which principle when adrenal hemorrhage is found?
opts: Investigate potential causes of adrenal hemorrhage and exclude an underlying tumor (including pheochromocytoma)
mcqs.exp: When adrenal hemorrhage is identified, causes such as stress, trauma, anticoagulation, coagulopathy, infection and underlying tumors (including pheochromocytoma, which can bleed) should be sought. Metanephrines were appropriately checked to exclude a hemorrhagic pheochromocytoma.
mcqs.hint: Hunt for a cause and rule out a bleeding tumor.

title: Organizing Adrenal Hematoma – Post-Contrast CT
caption: Adrenal hemorrhage / organizing hematoma in a 72-year-old woman (same case). Axial post-contrast CT shows heterogeneously decreased attenuation centrally with peripherally enhancing areas (arrowheads).

mcqs.q: On post-contrast CT, this organizing hematoma shows central non-enhancing low attenuation with peripheral enhancement. How does this help distinguish it from a solid tumor?
opts: The non-enhancing center indicates clot/necrosis, though peripheral enhancement can occur in an organizing hematoma and requires follow-up
mcqs.exp: A non-enhancing central component favors clot/necrosis rather than viable tumor. However, an organizing hematoma can develop peripheral enhancement (granulation/capsule), which overlaps with tumor, so short-interval follow-up (or resection if enlarging) is used to confirm resolution.
mcqs.hint: Clot centers don't enhance, but a rim can — follow it.

mcqs.q: What imaging behaviour over time would confirm a benign hematoma rather than a neoplasm?
opts: Interval decrease in size and attenuation on follow-up
New enhancement of the entire lesion
mcqs.exp: Hematomas typically shrink and decrease in attenuation over weeks to months, sometimes leaving a pseudocyst or calcification. Documented regression is the key confirmation of a benign hemorrhagic process.
mcqs.hint: Getting smaller confirms benign; growing worries you.

mcqs.q: Why can washout CT criteria be unreliable for a hemorrhagic adrenal mass like this?
opts: Because blood lowers attenuation to fat density
Because heterogeneous blood products distort attenuation/enhancement measurements, invalidating ROI-based washout
Because washout is unaffected by hemorrhage
mcqs.exp: Blood products create heterogeneous high/variable attenuation that confounds the ROI measurements the washout formula depends on. In hemorrhagic or very heterogeneous masses, washout is unreliable and follow-up, MRI or resection is used instead.
mcqs.hint: Blood muddies the density numbers the formula needs.

title: Organizing Adrenal Hematoma – Fat-Sat T1 MRI
caption: Adrenal hemorrhage / organizing hematoma in a 72-year-old woman (same case). Axial precontrast fat-saturated T1-weighted MR image shows areas of increased signal intensity correlating with recent hemorrhage (arrowheads).

mcqs.q: Intrinsic HIGH signal on the precontrast fat-saturated T1 image within this mass indicates what?
mcqs.exp: High T1 signal that persists on FAT-SATURATED images indicates subacute blood products (methemoglobin), not fat (fat would be suppressed). This confirms recent hemorrhage within the mass.
mcqs.hint: Bright on fat-sat T1 = blood, not fat.

mcqs.q: How does fat saturation help prove the T1-bright material is blood rather than fat?
opts: Fat suppresses (darkens) with fat sat, whereas methemoglobin/hemorrhage stays bright
mcqs.exp: Fat-saturation nulls macroscopic fat, so any residual T1 hyperintensity must be from something other than fat — here methemoglobin from hemorrhage. This distinguishes a hemorrhagic lesion from a fatty one (e.g., myelolipoma).
mcqs.hint: Fat goes dark with fat-sat; blood stays bright.

mcqs.q: MRI is often useful for characterizing the AGE of adrenal hemorrhage because blood products evolve. Which sequence pattern reflects this evolution?
opts: Changing T1/T2 signal as oxyhemoglobin → deoxyhemoglobin → methemoglobin → hemosiderin over time
mcqs.exp: Hemorrhage evolves predictably (deoxyhemoglobin, then intra- and extracellular methemoglobin, then hemosiderin), producing characteristic T1/T2 signal changes over time. MRI can therefore estimate hemorrhage age and confirm a resolving hematoma.
mcqs.hint: Blood changes signal as it ages on MRI.

title: Organizing Adrenal Hematoma – Fat-Sat T2 MRI
caption: Adrenal hemorrhage / organizing hematoma in a 72-year-old woman (same case). Axial fat-saturated T2-weighted MR image shows heterogeneous signal centrally, consisting of areas of hyperintensity (correlating with intermediate-period hemorrhage) and linear hypointensity (likely representing hemosiderin/organizing components) with a hypointense rim.

mcqs.q: The heterogeneous T2 signal with a hypointense rim in this mass is characteristic of what?
opts: An organizing hematoma (mixed blood products with a hemosiderin rim)
mcqs.exp: Mixed T2 signal with a peripheral hypointense (hemosiderin) rim reflects blood products of differing ages within an organizing hematoma. The dark rim from hemosiderin is a helpful clue to prior hemorrhage.
mcqs.hint: Mixed signal + dark rim = evolving blood.

mcqs.q: A peripheral hypointense (dark) rim on T2 in an adrenal mass most likely represents:
mcqs.exp: A T2-hypointense rim commonly represents hemosiderin deposition around an organizing hematoma (a fibrous/hemosiderin-laden capsule). It signals a prior bleed rather than a solid enhancing tumor rim.
mcqs.hint: Dark T2 rim = iron (hemosiderin) from old blood.

mcqs.q: Considering all four modalities in this case (US, CT, fat-sat T1 and T2 MRI), what is the overall lesson for indeterminate enlarging adrenal masses?
opts: Multimodality imaging (and follow-up/resection) may be needed to distinguish an organizing hematoma from a neoplasm
mcqs.exp: Because an organizing hematoma can mimic a tumor and even develop peripheral enhancement, combining modalities, documenting blood products, following over time, or resecting when uncertain is required to reach a confident benign diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: When in doubt, combine modalities, follow, or resect.

title: Organizing Adrenal Hematoma – Post-Contrast Fat-Sat MRI
caption: Adrenal hemorrhage / organizing hematoma in a 72-year-old woman (same case). Axial post-contrast fat-saturated T1-weighted MR image shows peripheral and septal enhancement (arrowheads) around the non-enhancing hemorrhagic center — a feature of an organizing hematoma that can overlap with a neoplasm.

mcqs.q: Peripheral/septal enhancement around a non-enhancing hemorrhagic center in an organizing hematoma is important because it:
opts: Overlaps with the appearance of a neoplasm, so cannot alone confirm benignity
mcqs.exp: Organizing hematomas develop granulation tissue and a fibrous capsule that enhance peripherally, mimicking a cystic/necrotic tumor. Enhancement therefore cannot by itself distinguish benign hematoma from neoplasm; follow-up or resection resolves the uncertainty.
mcqs.hint: A rim can enhance in both a healing clot and a tumor.

mcqs.q: The central component remains non-enhancing on this post-contrast image. What does that indicate?
opts: Avascular clot/necrosis (no viable enhancing tissue centrally)
mcqs.exp: Absence of central enhancement indicates non-viable clot or necrosis rather than solid perfused tumor. A predominantly non-enhancing lesion favors hematoma, but the enhancing periphery keeps a neoplasm in the differential until follow-up confirms regression.
mcqs.hint: No central uptake = no living tumor tissue there.

mcqs.q: Given the overlapping features, what management is appropriate for this enlarging, partially enhancing adrenal mass?
opts: Short-interval follow-up imaging to confirm regression, or resection if it persists/enlarges or remains indeterminate
mcqs.exp: Because an organizing hematoma can mimic a neoplasm, short-interval follow-up to document regression is appropriate; persistent, enlarging or indeterminate lesions are resected. This case was ultimately resected and proved to be an organizing hematoma.
mcqs.hint: Follow to see it shrink, or take it out if it doesn't.

title: Large Adrenal Pseudocyst with Wall Calcification
caption: Adrenal cyst in a 67-year-old man with a large left retroperitoneal pseudocyst and subsequent adrenal cortical insufficiency after removal. Coronal post-contrast CT shows a predominantly hypoattenuating well-circumscribed mass with dystrophic wall calcification (arrowhead), displacing the left kidney inferiorly. The surgical specimen showed red-tan friable tissue consistent with internal hemorrhage; the cyst wall ranged from 2 to 30 mm in thickness, correlating with an underlying functional adrenal cortical adenoma with subclinical Cushing syndrome.

mcqs.q: A large, predominantly hypoattenuating well-circumscribed adrenal mass with dystrophic wall calcification is most consistent with:
mcqs.exp: A well-circumscribed, predominantly low-attenuation (fluid) mass with dystrophic (often curvilinear) wall calcification is characteristic of an adrenal cyst/pseudocyst, frequently post-hemorrhagic. Mass effect displacing the kidney reflects large size, not necessarily malignancy.
mcqs.hint: Fluid mass + wall calcification = cyst/pseudocyst.

mcqs.q: The cyst wall varied from 2 to 30 mm and harbored a functional adenoma. What does focal wall THICKENING/nodularity in an adrenal cyst warrant?
opts: Concern for an underlying tumor or malignancy — closer evaluation/resection
mcqs.exp: Thin, uniform walls favor a simple benign cyst; focal thick or nodular enhancing wall raises concern for an underlying neoplasm (here a functional adenoma). Such atypical cysts may need resection and are correlated with biochemistry.
mcqs.hint: Thick/nodular walls are the worrisome ones.

mcqs.q: The patient developed adrenal cortical insufficiency AFTER removal. Why, and what does this teach about preoperative assessment?
opts: Surgery is unrelated to adrenal function
The functional adenoma had caused subclinical Cushing (suppressing the contralateral gland); removing it can unmask insufficiency, so perioperative steroid cover and screening are needed
mcqs.exp: Autonomous cortisol (subclinical Cushing) suppresses ACTH and the contralateral adrenal. Removing the source can precipitate transient adrenal insufficiency. Preoperative screening (dexamethasone suppression) identifies this so perioperative glucocorticoid cover can be given.
mcqs.hint: A silently cortisol-secreting lesion suppresses the other gland — cover steroids around surgery.

title: Adrenal Myelolipoma – Incidental Macroscopic Fat
caption: Adrenal myelolipoma in a 37-year-old man. Axial post-contrast CT shows an incidental left adrenal mass containing macroscopic fat (arrowhead), highly consistent with a myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: An incidental adrenal mass containing macroscopic fat on CT is highly consistent with which benign diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic fat (negative HU) within an adrenal mass is highly specific for a myelolipoma, a benign tumor of fat and hematopoietic tissue. Recognizing the fat allows a confident benign diagnosis without further work-up in most cases.
mcqs.hint: Gross fat in the adrenal = myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: For a small, asymptomatic myelolipoma discovered incidentally, what is the usual recommendation?
mcqs.exp: Small, asymptomatic myelolipomas are managed conservatively because they are benign and non-functioning. Surgery is reserved for large (often >4–6 cm), symptomatic, growing or hemorrhage-prone lesions.
mcqs.hint: Benign and quiet — just watch it.

mcqs.q: Even though a myelolipoma is non-functioning, why is a brief hormonal screen of any adrenal incidentaloma still reasonable?
opts: To exclude coexisting autonomous hormone secretion and pheochromocytoma before assuming a purely incidental lesion
mcqs.exp: Standard incidentaloma work-up includes hormonal screening (cortisol autonomy, metanephrines, and aldosterone if indicated). While a clear myelolipoma is benign and non-functioning, screening confirms there is no coexisting functional pathology.
mcqs.hint: Screen the incidentaloma to be safe, even if it looks fatty/benign.

title: Pheochromocytoma – T2 MRI with Cystic Change
caption: Adrenal pheochromocytoma in a 46-year-old woman with poorly controlled hypertension and ventricular tachycardia due to the pheochromocytoma. Coronal T2-weighted MR image shows a right adrenal pheochromocytoma (arrowhead) with heterogeneous signal intensity and focal areas of cystic change. (Post-contrast T1 showed avid arterial enhancement; fused CT/MIBG showed focal intense uptake; histology showed a nesting 'Zellballen' pattern.)

mcqs.q: Poorly controlled hypertension with ventricular tachycardia and a heterogeneous, partly cystic T2-bright right adrenal mass suggests which diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: Catecholamine excess from a pheochromocytoma causes paroxysmal/severe hypertension and can trigger arrhythmias such as ventricular tachycardia. A heterogeneous, T2-bright, partly cystic, avidly enhancing adrenal mass is the classic imaging correlate.
mcqs.hint: Severe hypertension + arrhythmia + T2-bright adrenal mass.

mcqs.q: The tumor shows avid ARTERIAL enhancement on post-contrast T1. This reflects which property of pheochromocytomas?
mcqs.exp: Pheochromocytomas are hypervascular and enhance avidly, often in the arterial phase, with cystic/necrotic areas in larger tumors. This hypervascularity is part of their variable, 'great mimic' appearance.
mcqs.hint: Very vascular tumors light up early after contrast.

mcqs.q: Histology showed a nesting pattern ('Zellballen'). This describes the characteristic architecture of which tumor?
mcqs.exp: The 'Zellballen' (nests of chief cells surrounded by sustentacular cells) is the classic histologic pattern of pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma. Sustentacular cells stain for S100 and chief cells for chromogranin/synaptophysin.
mcqs.hint: German for 'cell balls' — the nesting pattern of chromaffin tumors.

title: Pheochromocytoma – Avid Arterial Enhancement (T1)
caption: Adrenal pheochromocytoma in a 46-year-old woman (same case). Axial post-contrast T1-weighted MR image shows avid enhancement in the arterial phase (arrowhead) of the right adrenal mass.

mcqs.q: Avid arterial-phase enhancement of an adrenal mass, as shown, is most in keeping with which lesion?
mcqs.exp: Avid early (arterial) enhancement reflects hypervascularity, characteristic of pheochromocytoma. Combined with T2 brightness, cystic change and elevated metanephrines, it supports the diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: Strong early enhancement = very vascular tumor.

mcqs.q: Because pheochromocytomas enhance avidly and often retain contrast, how do they typically behave on washout criteria?
opts: Low/variable washout, which can overlap with malignancy
mcqs.exp: Pheochromocytomas frequently show low or variable washout despite avid enhancement, so washout criteria cannot reliably exclude them. This is why biochemical exclusion of pheochromocytoma is essential for indeterminate masses.
mcqs.hint: They enhance strongly but don't wash out like adenomas.

mcqs.q: This patient's pheochromocytoma caused ventricular tachycardia. What is the mechanism linking the tumor to arrhythmia?
opts: Catecholamine excess causing sympathetic overstimulation of the heart
mcqs.exp: Excess catecholamines from the pheochromocytoma overstimulate cardiac beta-receptors, causing tachyarrhythmias, catecholamine cardiomyopathy and hypertensive crises. This underlies the need for alpha-blockade before surgery.
mcqs.hint: Flooding the heart with adrenaline-like hormones.

title: Pheochromocytoma – Fused CT/MIBG Confirmation
caption: Adrenal pheochromocytoma in a 46-year-old woman (same case). Coronal fused CT/MIBG (metaiodobenzylguanidine) image shows focal intense radiotracer uptake in the right adrenal mass (arrowhead), confirming the diagnosis.

mcqs.q: Focal intense MIBG uptake fused to the adrenal mass on CT does what for this case?
opts: Functionally confirms the mass is a catecholamine-avid pheochromocytoma
mcqs.exp: MIBG (a noradrenaline analog) concentrates in chromaffin tissue, so focal intense adrenal uptake functionally confirms a pheochromocytoma and localizes it precisely when fused to CT.
mcqs.hint: The catecholamine-analog tracer lighting up the mass confirms it.

mcqs.q: What is the advantage of FUSING MIBG (functional) with CT (anatomic) imaging?
opts: It precisely localizes functional uptake to a specific anatomic lesion, improving confidence and surgical planning
mcqs.exp: Hybrid functional-anatomic imaging (SPECT/CT, PET/CT) localizes tracer uptake to a specific structure, distinguishing physiologic from pathologic uptake and guiding surgery — more precise than planar functional imaging alone.
mcqs.hint: Function + anatomy together pinpoints the lesion.

mcqs.q: MIBG avidity, as demonstrated here, also has therapeutic significance because it identifies candidates for:
opts: ¹³¹I-MIBG targeted radionuclide therapy for unresectable/metastatic disease
mcqs.exp: MIBG-avid tumors can be treated with therapeutic ¹³¹I-MIBG, a theranostic approach for metastatic or unresectable pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma. Confirming avidity on the diagnostic scan selects suitable patients.
mcqs.hint: Avid on the scan means it can be treated with the therapeutic isotope.

title: Adrenal Cavernous Hemangioma
caption: Adrenal hemangioma in an 84-year-old woman with progressive left flank pain. Coronal post-contrast CT shows a cavernous adrenal hemangioma with well-circumscribed margins and capsular calcifications that demonstrates peripheral nodular discontinuous enhancement (arrowhead).

mcqs.q: Peripheral nodular discontinuous enhancement in a well-circumscribed adrenal mass is a classic pattern shared with which liver lesion, and here indicates:
mcqs.exp: Peripheral nodular discontinuous enhancement with progressive centripetal fill-in is the hallmark of a cavernous hemangioma (as in hepatic hemangiomas). In the adrenal it indicates a rare benign vascular tumor.
mcqs.hint: Same peripheral nodular fill-in pattern as a liver hemangioma.

mcqs.q: Which additional CT feature described here supports the benign vascular nature of this lesion?
opts: Well-circumscribed margins with capsular (often phlebolith-like) calcifications
mcqs.exp: Adrenal hemangiomas are typically well-circumscribed and may contain calcifications (including phleboliths). These, with the peripheral nodular enhancement pattern, favor a benign hemangioma over an aggressive tumor.
mcqs.hint: Smooth margins plus calcifications, like little vein-stones.

mcqs.q: Why are adrenal hemangiomas often still resected despite being benign?
opts: Imaging features overlap with malignancy and larger lesions can hemorrhage, so resection provides diagnosis and prevents complications
mcqs.exp: Because their imaging can be indeterminate and large hemangiomas may bleed, resection is often performed for definitive diagnosis and to prevent hemorrhage, particularly in symptomatic or large lesions.
mcqs.hint: Uncertain imaging + bleeding risk → often taken out.

title: Adrenal Lymphangioma
caption: Adrenal lymphangioma in a 35-year-old woman with flank pain. Axial post-contrast delayed-phase CT shows a right adrenal lymphangioma, characterized by a few enhancing septa (arrowhead) surrounding locules of hypoattenuating simple fluid.

mcqs.q: A right adrenal lesion with thin enhancing septa surrounding locules of simple fluid is characteristic of which benign lesion?
mcqs.exp: Adrenal lymphangioma is a benign cystic lymphatic malformation appearing as a multilocular fluid-filled lesion with thin enhancing septa but no solid enhancing nodules. The simple-fluid locules and thin septa favor benignity.
mcqs.hint: Multilocular fluid with thin septa = lymphatic (cystic) lesion.

mcqs.q: Which feature distinguishes this benign cystic lesion from a cystic malignancy?
opts: Only thin septal enhancement with simple-fluid locules and no solid nodule
mcqs.exp: Benign cystic lesions show only thin septa and simple fluid without enhancing solid/nodular tissue. Thick or nodular enhancing components would raise concern for a cystic neoplasm and prompt resection.
mcqs.hint: Thin septa and clear fluid, no solid lumps.

mcqs.q: What is the general management approach to an asymptomatic adrenal lymphangioma?
opts: Observation if characteristically benign; resection if large, symptomatic or indeterminate
mcqs.exp: Small, characteristically benign lymphangiomas can be observed. Large, symptomatic (e.g., flank pain) or indeterminate lesions are resected for symptom relief and definitive diagnosis. Hormonal screening is still done as part of incidentaloma work-up.
mcqs.hint: Watch if clearly benign; remove if big, painful or unclear.

title: Adrenal Schwannoma
caption: Adrenal schwannoma in a 46-year-old man with right flank pain. Axial post-contrast CT shows subtle heterogeneous enhancement (arrowhead) in a hypoattenuating right adrenal mass, which was shown to be a schwannoma.

mcqs.q: Adrenal schwannoma is a rare tumor arising from which cell type?
mcqs.exp: Schwannomas arise from the Schwann cells of nerve sheaths (here of the adrenal/periadrenal nerves). They are usually benign, well-circumscribed and often nonfunctional, presenting as an incidental or symptomatic hypoattenuating mass.
mcqs.hint: Nerve-sheath tumor — named after the cell that myelinates nerves.

mcqs.q: What is a typical CT feature of an adrenal schwannoma?
opts: A well-circumscribed hypoattenuating mass with mild/heterogeneous enhancement (sometimes cystic/degenerative change)
mcqs.exp: Adrenal schwannomas are usually well-defined and hypoattenuating with variable, often subtle heterogeneous enhancement, and may show cystic degeneration or calcification in larger lesions. Features overlap with other tumors, so diagnosis is often histologic.
mcqs.hint: Well-defined, low-density, mild patchy enhancement.

mcqs.q: Why are adrenal schwannomas usually diagnosed only after resection?
opts: Their imaging features are nonspecific and overlap with other adrenal masses, so histology is required
mcqs.exp: Because imaging is nonspecific and overlaps with adenoma, pheochromocytoma and other tumors, adrenal schwannomas are typically diagnosed histologically after resection. Pheochromocytoma is excluded biochemically first.
mcqs.hint: Nonspecific pictures → tissue diagnosis after removal.

title: Adrenal Ganglioneuroma – Whorled T2 Appearance
caption: Ganglioneuroma in a 69-year-old man in whom a previous CT for diverticulitis showed an incidental 6-cm left adrenal ganglioneuroma. Coronal T2-weighted MR image shows that the mass has heterogeneously increased signal intensity with a whorled appearance (arrowhead).

mcqs.q: A large adrenal/retroperitoneal mass with a 'whorled' appearance and heterogeneous high T2 signal is characteristic of which benign tumor?
mcqs.exp: Ganglioneuromas are benign sympathetic-ganglion tumors that often show a whorled appearance (interlacing Schwann cells and collagen) and heterogeneous high T2 signal. They tend to be soft and insinuate around vessels without occluding them.
mcqs.hint: A whorled, T2-bright, soft retroperitoneal mass.

mcqs.q: Ganglioneuromas characteristically demonstrate what enhancement behaviour?
opts: Progressive/delayed enhancement (often mild early, increasing on delayed images)
mcqs.exp: Ganglioneuromas typically show gradual, progressive enhancement on delayed imaging due to their myxoid/collagenous stroma, in contrast to the avid early enhancement of pheochromocytoma. This can be a helpful (though nonspecific) clue.
mcqs.hint: Slow, delayed fill-in rather than quick arterial blush.

mcqs.q: How does the behaviour of a ganglioneuroma differ from a neuroblastoma, despite a shared origin?
opts: Ganglioneuroma is malignant and metastasizes early
Ganglioneuroma is benign/indolent and fully differentiated, whereas neuroblastoma is malignant
mcqs.exp: Both arise from sympathetic neural-crest tissue, but ganglioneuroma is the benign, fully differentiated end of the spectrum (indolent, low FDG uptake), while neuroblastoma is the malignant, poorly differentiated end.
mcqs.hint: Same family, opposite ends: mature/benign vs. immature/malignant.

title: Adrenal Adenomatoid Tumor – T2 MRI
caption: Adenomatoid tumor in a 22-year-old man with right lower quadrant pain and an incidental left adrenal mass, subsequently resected and confirmed to be an adenomatoid tumor. Axial fat-saturated T2-weighted MR image shows a well-circumscribed solid and cystic 18-cm mass (arrowhead) with heterogeneous signal intensity.

mcqs.q: An adrenal adenomatoid tumor is a rare benign tumor of which origin?
mcqs.exp: Adenomatoid tumors are benign neoplasms of mesothelial origin, more commonly found in the genital tract but occasionally arising in the adrenal. They are benign but can be large and are usually diagnosed histologically.
mcqs.hint: Same benign mesothelial tumor seen in the genital tract.

mcqs.q: This 18-cm solid-and-cystic adrenal mass in a young man is indeterminate on imaging. What is the appropriate approach?
opts: Biochemical work-up (including exclusion of pheochromocytoma) followed by resection for definitive diagnosis
mcqs.exp: A large, heterogeneous, indeterminate adrenal mass warrants full biochemical work-up (excluding pheochromocytoma) and typically resection, both for diagnosis and because size/indeterminacy raises concern for malignancy. Here it proved to be a benign adenomatoid tumor.
mcqs.hint: Big and indeterminate → screen then resect.

mcqs.q: Why can imaging alone not confidently diagnose an adrenal adenomatoid tumor?
opts: Its solid-cystic, heterogeneous appearance is nonspecific and overlaps with other benign and malignant adrenal masses
It always shows opposed-phase signal drop
mcqs.exp: The heterogeneous solid-and-cystic appearance overlaps with many adrenal tumors, so imaging cannot be specific; histology after resection provides the diagnosis. This is common for rare mesenchymal adrenal tumors.
mcqs.hint: Nonspecific solid-cystic look → needs pathology.

title: Adrenal Adenomatoid Tumor – Post-Contrast T1
caption: Adenomatoid tumor in a 22-year-old man (same case). Axial post-contrast T1-weighted MR image shows heterogeneous enhancement of the solid components (arrowhead) of the large left adrenal mass.

mcqs.q: Heterogeneous enhancement of the solid components of this large adrenal mass indicates what?
opts: It contains viable, vascularized solid tissue (enhancing), so it is not a simple cyst
mcqs.exp: Enhancement of solid components confirms viable vascularized tissue, distinguishing a solid-and-cystic neoplasm from a simple cyst. Enhancing solid tissue in a large indeterminate mass mandates resection for diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: Enhancing solid tissue = real tumor, not just a cyst.

mcqs.q: For a large indeterminate adrenal mass like this, which pre-intervention test is mandatory?
opts: Exclusion of pheochromocytoma with plasma/urinary metanephrines
mcqs.exp: Any indeterminate adrenal mass requires biochemical exclusion of pheochromocytoma before biopsy or surgery, to prevent a catecholamine crisis during manipulation — regardless of the eventual histology.
mcqs.hint: Always rule out the catecholamine tumor first.

mcqs.q: What is the prognosis after complete resection of a benign adrenal adenomatoid tumor?
opts: Excellent — it is benign and cured by complete excision
mcqs.exp: Adenomatoid tumors are benign; complete surgical excision is curative with an excellent prognosis. The main challenge is preoperative diagnosis, since imaging is nonspecific.
mcqs.hint: Benign tumor, cured by taking it out.

title: Adrenal Oncocytoma (Borderline)
caption: Confirmed borderline adrenal oncocytoma in a 48-year-old woman with right flank pain and a right adrenal mass. Axial post-contrast CT shows a 12-cm well-demarcated heterogeneously enhancing solid mass (arrowhead). Histology showed diffuse oncocytic cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and high nuclear grade; melan A stain was avidly positive.

mcqs.q: An adrenal oncocytoma is a tumor composed of oncocytes — cells packed with what organelle giving eosinophilic cytoplasm?
mcqs.exp: Oncocytes are cells filled with abundant mitochondria, producing granular eosinophilic cytoplasm. Adrenal oncocytomas (oncocytic adrenocortical neoplasms) can be benign, borderline or malignant, graded by histologic criteria (e.g., Lin–Weiss–Bisceglia).
mcqs.hint: The organelle that makes cytoplasm pink and granular — the powerhouse.

mcqs.q: The melan A positivity confirms this tumor's origin. Melan A indicates which lineage?
mcqs.exp: Melan A (MART-1) is a marker of steroidogenic (adrenocortical) cells, confirming an adrenocortical (oncocytic) neoplasm rather than a medullary or metastatic tumor. Inhibin and SF-1 are other cortical markers.
mcqs.hint: It marks the steroid-making cortex, not the medulla.

mcqs.q: A 12-cm heterogeneous adrenal mass, even if 'borderline', warrants which management?
opts: Complete surgical resection with careful histologic grading and follow-up
mcqs.exp: Large oncocytic adrenal neoplasms are resected because they can be malignant and imaging cannot reliably grade them. Histologic grading (e.g., Lin–Weiss–Bisceglia) determines malignant potential and guides follow-up. Pheochromocytoma is excluded first.
mcqs.hint: Big and uncertain-grade → take it out and grade it.

title: Bilateral Adrenal Histoplasmosis
caption: Adrenal infection in a 73-year-old man with a 3-month history of fatigue, loss of appetite and unexplained weight loss. Coronal post-contrast delayed-phase CT shows symmetrically enlarged hypoattenuating adrenals (arrowheads). Biopsy (Gomori methenamine-silver stain) showed numerous small budding yeasts typical of Histoplasma capsulatum.

mcqs.q: Bilateral symmetric adrenal enlargement with fatigue, anorexia and weight loss should raise suspicion for which category of disease (besides malignancy)?
opts: Infiltrative/infectious disease causing adrenal insufficiency (e.g., histoplasmosis, TB)
mcqs.exp: Bilateral adrenal enlargement with constitutional symptoms suggests an infiltrative process — infection (histoplasmosis, TB), lymphoma, or metastasis — that can destroy the cortex and cause primary adrenal insufficiency. Disseminated histoplasmosis characteristically involves the adrenals.
mcqs.hint: Bilateral glands + weight loss → think infiltration/infection.

mcqs.q: Disseminated histoplasmosis involving both adrenals classically produces which endocrine consequence?
mcqs.exp: Like tuberculosis, disseminated histoplasmosis can bilaterally destroy the adrenal cortices, causing primary adrenal insufficiency. Recognizing this and giving glucocorticoids plus antifungal therapy is important.
mcqs.hint: Bilateral cortical destruction → Addison's.

mcqs.q: How was the diagnosis of adrenal histoplasmosis established here?
opts: Biopsy with special stains (GMS) showing the characteristic budding yeasts, plus clinical correlation
mcqs.exp: Because imaging is nonspecific, biopsy with fungal stains (GMS) demonstrating Histoplasma yeasts confirms the diagnosis, alongside cultures/serology. Adrenal function is assessed and antifungal therapy started; pheochromocytoma is excluded before biopsy.
mcqs.hint: Special fungal stains on biopsy tissue clinch it.

title: Functional Adrenocortical Carcinoma – Sex Hormone Excess
caption: Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) in a 67-year-old woman with metrorrhagia, hot flashes and breast pain; laboratory results confirmed abnormal sex hormone secretion. Coronal post-contrast CT shows an enhancing well-circumscribed ACC (arrowhead) originating from the left suprarenal fossa, displacing the left kidney inferiorly and the spleen and left hemidiaphragm superiorly.

mcqs.q: This ACC caused abnormal SEX HORMONE secretion. Which hormonal syndromes are classically produced by functional adrenocortical carcinomas?
opts: Cortisol excess (Cushing), androgen/estrogen excess (virilization/feminization), and sometimes aldosterone excess
mcqs.exp: Functional ACCs may secrete cortisol (Cushing), androgens (virilization) or estrogens (feminization), and less commonly aldosterone — often in combination. Sex-hormone excess (as here) is a red flag for adrenocortical carcinoma.
mcqs.hint: Cortex-derived cancers can pour out cortisol and sex steroids.

mcqs.q: The mass displaces the kidney inferiorly and the spleen/diaphragm superiorly. What does this large size and mass effect suggest?
opts: A large aggressive tumor (consistent with ACC) requiring surgical resection and staging
mcqs.exp: Large size with mass effect on adjacent organs, combined with hormone hypersecretion, is highly suspicious for ACC. Such tumors require staging for local invasion/venous thrombus and metastases, and complete surgical resection.
mcqs.hint: Big, hormone-secreting and pushing organs aside = cancer.

mcqs.q: Which combination is most concerning for adrenocortical carcinoma in a patient with an adrenal mass?
opts: Rapidly developing hormone excess (especially mixed/sex steroids) with a large heterogeneous mass
mcqs.exp: The clinical hallmark of ACC is a large adrenal mass with rapidly progressive and often mixed hormone excess (cortisol + androgens/estrogens). This constellation should prompt urgent specialist evaluation.
mcqs.hint: Fast, mixed hormone excess + big mass = ACC until proven otherwise.

title: Adrenocortical Carcinoma Containing Macroscopic Fat
caption: Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) in a 20-year-old man with early satiety and increasing abdominal girth. Axial post-contrast CT shows an ACC in the left retroperitoneum with peripheral nodular soft-tissue enhancement (white arrowhead) that also contains aggregates of macroscopic fat (black arrowhead), with punctate calcification and more fat extending anteriorly.

mcqs.q: This ACC contained aggregates of MACROSCOPIC FAT. Why is that an important pitfall?
opts: Fat always means a benign myelolipoma
Rarely, ACC (and other tumors) can contain macroscopic fat, so fat does not always guarantee a benign myelolipoma — other malignant features must be assessed
Fat means the lesion is a cyst
mcqs.exp: Although macroscopic fat usually indicates a benign myelolipoma, rare tumors — including ACC and, e.g., a collision tumor or retroperitoneal liposarcoma — can contain fat. When fat coexists with a large size, nodular enhancement, calcification and rapid growth, malignancy must be considered.
mcqs.hint: Fat usually means benign, but not always — check the other features.

mcqs.q: Which features here argue AGAINST a simple myelolipoma despite the presence of fat?
opts: Large size, peripheral nodular soft-tissue enhancement, calcification, and aggressive growth in a young patient
mcqs.exp: Solid enhancing soft-tissue nodularity, calcification, large aggressive size and rapid symptoms indicate malignancy (ACC), unlike the purely fatty, non-aggressive myelolipoma. The fat is a component, not the whole lesion.
mcqs.hint: Enhancing solid nodules and aggression override the fat.

mcqs.q: A large retroperitoneal fat-containing mass with enhancing soft tissue in a young adult should be worked up how?
opts: Full biochemical evaluation (excluding pheochromocytoma) and resection with careful histologic assessment for malignancy
mcqs.exp: Such an indeterminate, aggressive-appearing fat-containing mass needs biochemical work-up (including pheochromocytoma exclusion) and surgical resection with histology, because it may be a malignant tumor (ACC or fat-containing sarcoma) rather than a benign myelolipoma.
mcqs.hint: Don't assume benign just because there is fat — screen and resect.

title: Adrenocortical Carcinoma with Fat & Calcification
caption: Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) in a 20-year-old man (same case). Axial post-contrast CT shows inferomedial displacement of the left kidney, with punctate calcification (white arrowhead) and more macroscopic fat extending anteriorly (black arrowhead) within the large left retroperitoneal mass.

mcqs.q: Punctate calcification within this large fat-containing adrenal mass supports which interpretation?
opts: A heterogeneous aggressive tumor (ACC), since calcification plus solid enhancement and size favor malignancy
mcqs.exp: Calcification occurs in ~30% of ACCs. Combined with large size, solid enhancing components and mass effect, it favors malignancy — even though the lesion also contains fat. Fat alone does not confirm a benign myelolipoma here.
mcqs.hint: Calcification + solid nodularity + size overrides the fat.

mcqs.q: Inferomedial displacement of the kidney by this mass helps confirm what?
opts: Adrenal/suprarenal (retroperitoneal) origin, pushing the kidney down rather than arising from it
mcqs.exp: A suprarenal mass displaces the kidney inferiorly/medially without arising from renal parenchyma (no 'claw sign' of renal tissue). This organ-of-origin assessment distinguishes an adrenal mass from an exophytic renal tumor.
mcqs.hint: It pushes the kidney away rather than growing out of it.

mcqs.q: This case (ACC with fat) reinforces which key teaching point about adrenal fat?
opts: Macroscopic fat usually indicates a benign myelolipoma, but rare exceptions (ACC, fat-containing sarcoma, collision tumor) exist — assess the whole lesion
Fat means the lesion is a cyst
mcqs.exp: While fat strongly suggests a benign myelolipoma, aggressive features (solid enhancing nodularity, calcification, rapid growth, invasion) in a fat-containing mass should prompt consideration of malignancy and resection. Judge the whole lesion, not the fat alone.
mcqs.hint: Fat is usually benign, but not a free pass — weigh everything.

title: Bilateral Secondary Adrenal Lymphoma
caption: Adrenal lymphomas in a 77-year-old man with previous large B-cell lymphoma. Axial post-contrast CT shows bilateral well-circumscribed hypoattenuating adrenal masses (arrowheads), later confirmed to represent secondary involvement.

mcqs.q: Bilateral hypoattenuating adrenal masses in a patient with known large B-cell lymphoma most likely represent:
mcqs.exp: In a patient with known lymphoma, bilateral soft-tissue adrenal masses most likely represent secondary adrenal involvement. Adrenal lymphoma (usually aggressive B-cell types) is often bilateral and homogeneous.
mcqs.hint: Known lymphoma + bilateral adrenal masses = spread to the glands.

mcqs.q: Why can adrenal lymphoma appear 'hypoattenuating' yet be solid tumor rather than fluid?
opts: Densely cellular lymphomatous tissue can appear relatively low in attenuation but is solid and enhances mildly, unlike simple fluid
mcqs.exp: Lymphoma is composed of homogeneous, densely packed cells that can look relatively hypoattenuating but are solid soft tissue with mild enhancement and restricted diffusion — not fluid. This can mimic a cyst superficially, so enhancement/DWI help.
mcqs.hint: Homogeneous solid cells can look low-density but still enhance.

mcqs.q: What is the appropriate next step to confirm secondary adrenal lymphoma?
opts: Correlate with the known lymphoma and obtain biopsy/PET if it changes management (after excluding pheochromocytoma)
mcqs.exp: Given the known primary, FDG-PET (lymphoma is avid) and/or biopsy confirm adrenal involvement and guide chemotherapy. Pheochromocytoma should be excluded biochemically before any biopsy.
mcqs.hint: Confirm with PET/biopsy in the context of the known lymphoma.

title: Solitary Adrenal Lymphoma – Recurrence
caption: Adrenal lymphoma in a 57-year-old man. Axial CT shows a solitary homogeneously hypoattenuating left adrenal mass (arrowhead), consistent with known recurrence of lymphoma.

mcqs.q: A solitary homogeneously hypoattenuating adrenal mass in a patient with known lymphoma is most consistent with:
mcqs.exp: Although adrenal lymphoma is often bilateral, unilateral (solitary) involvement occurs. In a patient with known lymphoma, a homogeneous soft-tissue adrenal mass is consistent with lymphomatous involvement or recurrence.
mcqs.hint: Lymphoma can also present as a single homogeneous adrenal mass.

mcqs.q: How is adrenal lymphoma best distinguished from a lipid-rich adenoma when both can look low in attenuation?
opts: Lymphoma is solid soft tissue (usually >10 HU, FDG-avid, restricted diffusion), whereas a lipid-rich adenoma is ≤10 HU with opposed-phase signal drop
mcqs.exp: A lipid-rich adenoma measures ≤10 HU and drops signal on opposed-phase MRI. Lymphoma is solid (typically >10 HU), FDG-avid and shows restricted diffusion without lipid signal drop. These features separate them.
mcqs.hint: Adenoma = low HU + fat dropout; lymphoma = solid, PET-hot, no fat.

mcqs.q: What clinical vigilance is needed in bilateral or extensive adrenal lymphoma specifically?
opts: Monitor for primary adrenal insufficiency from gland replacement
mcqs.exp: Extensive adrenal replacement by lymphoma (especially bilateral) can cause primary adrenal insufficiency. Even with unilateral disease, awareness of adrenal function during treatment is prudent.
mcqs.hint: Enough tumor replacing cortex → risk of adrenal failure.

title: Adrenal Leiomyosarcoma
caption: Adrenal leiomyosarcoma in a 75-year-old woman with abdominal tenderness. Axial post-contrast CT shows a heterogeneous solid and cystic leiomyosarcoma (arrowhead) which invaded the adrenal and renal capsule.

mcqs.q: Adrenal leiomyosarcoma is a rare malignant tumor arising from which tissue?
opts: Smooth muscle (e.g., of the adrenal/central vein wall)
mcqs.exp: Leiomyosarcoma is a malignant smooth-muscle tumor; in the adrenal it may arise from the smooth muscle of the adrenal (central) vein wall. It is rare, aggressive, and presents as a large heterogeneous mass.
mcqs.hint: Smooth-muscle sarcoma — think vessel wall origin.

mcqs.q: Which imaging feature here indicates an aggressive malignant tumor?
opts: A heterogeneous solid-and-cystic mass invading the adrenal and renal capsule
mcqs.exp: Invasion of adjacent structures (adrenal and renal capsule), large size and heterogeneity indicate an aggressive malignancy. Local invasion is a hallmark of malignant tumors, distinguishing them from benign adrenal lesions.
mcqs.hint: Invading neighboring organs = malignant.

mcqs.q: How is a rare aggressive adrenal sarcoma like this typically managed and diagnosed?
opts: Surgical resection with wide margins and histologic diagnosis (immunostains for smooth-muscle markers), after excluding pheochromocytoma
mcqs.exp: Treatment is complete surgical resection with negative margins; diagnosis is histologic (smooth-muscle markers such as desmin/SMA). As with any indeterminate adrenal mass, pheochromocytoma is excluded biochemically before intervention.
mcqs.hint: Wide resection + immunostains; screen out pheo first.

title: Adrenal Metastasis – Indeterminate CT
caption: Adrenal metastasis in a 61-year-old man with known cutaneous melanoma. Axial post-contrast CT shows a left adrenal nodule (arrowhead) that is nonspecific in appearance, resulting in an indeterminate classification and leading to further assessment.

mcqs.q: In a patient with known melanoma, a nonspecific left adrenal nodule on contrast CT should be classified as:
mcqs.exp: A nodule that does not meet benign criteria (e.g., not clearly a lipid-rich adenoma, myelolipoma or cyst) in a patient with a known primary malignancy is indeterminate. It cannot be called benign or malignant on morphology alone and needs further characterization (washout CT, chemical-shift MRI, PET, or biopsy).
mcqs.hint: Doesn't meet benign criteria + known cancer = can't be dismissed.

mcqs.q: Why is a single contrast-enhanced CT often insufficient to characterize such an adrenal nodule?
opts: Because CT cannot show adrenal glands
Because a single post-contrast phase gives no unenhanced attenuation or washout data, both of which are needed to identify a lipid-rich or adenomatous lesion
Because metastases are invisible on CT
mcqs.exp: Characterization needs either an unenhanced HU (≤10 HU = lipid-rich adenoma) or a dedicated washout protocol (unenhanced, portal venous, and 15-minute delayed). A lone post-contrast image lacks both, so the lesion stays indeterminate.
mcqs.hint: No unenhanced HU and no washout numbers means no benign proof.

mcqs.q: What is the most appropriate next step for this indeterminate adrenal nodule in a melanoma patient?
opts: Further assessment such as PET/CT (or washout CT / chemical-shift MRI / biopsy) to determine whether it is a metastasis
mcqs.exp: Because it affects staging and management, an indeterminate adrenal nodule in a patient with a known primary is further assessed — commonly with FDG-PET/CT (melanoma is FDG-avid), and if still unclear, image-guided biopsy after excluding pheochromocytoma.
mcqs.hint: Take it to a functional test like PET to settle the question.

title: Adrenal Metastasis – FDG-Avid on PET/CT
caption: Adrenal metastasis in a 61-year-old man with known cutaneous melanoma (same case). Axial PET/CT image shows markedly increased metabolic activity (arrowhead) in the left adrenal nodule, which was shown to represent a melanoma metastasis.

mcqs.q: Markedly increased FDG uptake in the previously indeterminate adrenal nodule indicates:
opts: A hypermetabolic lesion consistent with malignancy/metastasis
mcqs.exp: Most adrenal metastases and other malignancies are FDG-avid, whereas most benign adenomas show uptake at or below liver background. Markedly increased uptake in this melanoma patient indicates a metastasis, resolving the earlier indeterminate CT.
mcqs.hint: Hot on PET = metabolically active = malignant here.

mcqs.q: How is FDG uptake typically interpreted when distinguishing adrenal adenoma from metastasis?
opts: Uptake below or equal to liver favors benign; uptake clearly greater than liver favors malignancy
Uptake is meaningless in the adrenal
mcqs.exp: A common qualitative rule compares adrenal to hepatic uptake: adrenal ≤ liver background suggests a benign adenoma, while adrenal clearly > liver suggests malignancy. SUV thresholds and the adrenal-to-liver ratio are also used, with some overlap.
mcqs.hint: Compare the adrenal to the liver as the reference.

mcqs.q: Which benign adrenal lesion can occasionally be FDG-avid and cause a false-positive PET result?
opts: Some adenomas, and inflammatory/infectious lesions (e.g., adrenal infection) or pheochromocytoma can show uptake
mcqs.exp: Although most adenomas are not avid, a minority show mild uptake, and inflammatory/infectious processes and pheochromocytomas can be FDG-avid — potential false positives. Correlation with morphology, HU/washout, and clinical context is important.
mcqs.hint: Inflammation, some adenomas, and pheo can also light up.

title: Adrenal Adenoma – Attenuation, Chemical Shift & Washout (3 patients)
caption: Three patients with adrenal adenoma. (A) 33-year-old woman with a right adrenal mass incidentally found on unenhanced CT for flank pain; mean attenuation −5.92 HU (ROI) is diagnostic of a benign adenoma or cyst. (B, C) 66-year-old woman on surveillance MRI: in-phase (B) and opposed-phase (C) images show a right adrenal mass; the opposed-phase image shows decreased signal, diagnostic of a lipid-rich adenoma. (D–F) 53-year-old man with hyperaldosteronism and refractory hypertension: adrenal-protocol CT — unenhanced (D) 21.69 HU, portal venous phase (E) 59.58 HU, and 15-minute delayed (F) 32.17 HU — all diagnostic of a lipid-poor adenoma. Absolute percentage washout was 72% and relative percentage washout 46%. Ovals denote ROIs.

mcqs.q: In panel A, the right adrenal mass measures a mean of −5.92 HU on unenhanced CT. What does this establish?
opts: A benign lipid-rich adenoma (or cyst) — attenuation ≤10 HU is diagnostic of benignity
mcqs.exp: An unenhanced attenuation ≤10 HU indicates abundant intracytoplasmic lipid (or fluid), diagnostic of a benign lipid-rich adenoma (or cyst). A value below 0 HU is even more confidently benign. No further work-up is generally needed.
mcqs.hint: ≤10 HU unenhanced = benign lipid-rich adenoma.

mcqs.q: Panels B and C are in-phase and opposed-phase MRI. The opposed-phase (C) mass loses signal. What does this signal drop indicate?
opts: Intracytoplasmic (microscopic) lipid, diagnostic of a lipid-rich adenoma
mcqs.exp: Chemical-shift MRI exploits fat and water within the same voxel: on opposed-phase images their signals cancel, so a lesion containing intracytoplasmic lipid (a lipid-rich adenoma) drops in signal relative to in-phase. This is one of the most reliable signs of a benign adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Signal falling on opposed-phase = fat and water in the same voxel.

mcqs.q: Panels D–F are an adrenal-protocol washout CT (unenhanced 21.69 HU, portal venous 59.58 HU, delayed 32.17 HU). Absolute washout was 72%. How is absolute percentage washout (APW) calculated and what threshold indicates an adenoma?
opts: APW = (enhanced − delayed)/(enhanced − unenhanced) × 100; ≥60% indicates an adenoma
mcqs.exp: APW = (portal-venous HU − delayed HU) / (portal-venous HU − unenhanced HU) × 100. Here (59.58 − 32.17)/(59.58 − 21.69) × 100 ≈ 72%. APW ≥ 60% is diagnostic of an adenoma (lipid-poor adenomas wash out rapidly).
mcqs.hint: Numerator uses the enhanced-minus-delayed drop; denominator subtracts the unenhanced baseline; ≥60%.

mcqs.q: Relative percentage washout (RPW) here was 46%. When is RPW used and what is its adenoma threshold?
opts: RPW is used when no unenhanced scan is available; RPW = (enhanced − delayed)/enhanced × 100, with ≥40% indicating an adenoma
RPW requires an unenhanced scan; threshold 60%
mcqs.exp: RPW = (portal-venous HU − delayed HU) / portal-venous HU × 100, and does NOT need an unenhanced acquisition. Here (59.58 − 32.17)/59.58 × 100 ≈ 46%. RPW ≥ 40% indicates an adenoma. It is used when only post-contrast and delayed images are available.
mcqs.hint: No unenhanced scan needed; divide the wash-out by the enhanced value; ≥40%.

mcqs.q: The panel D–F patient has hyperaldosteronism and refractory hypertension with a lipid-poor adenoma. What is the key teaching point linking the panels A, B–C, and D–F together?
opts: Only unenhanced HU can ever diagnose an adenoma

An adenoma can be confirmed by ANY of several benign signs — low unenhanced HU, opposed-phase signal drop, or rapid contrast washout — so a lipid-poor adenoma with HU >10 can still be proven benign by washout
mcqs.exp: Lipid-rich adenomas are proven by ≤10 HU or opposed-phase signal drop. Lipid-poor adenomas exceed 10 HU and lack marked signal drop, but still wash out rapidly (APW ≥60%, RPW ≥40%). Having multiple complementary tools lets almost any adenoma be confidently characterized. This aldosterone-producing adenoma is lipid-poor yet confirmed benign by 72% APW.
mcqs.hint: Low HU, opposed-phase dropout, and fast washout are three routes to the same benign call.

title: Adrenal Myelolipoma – Macroscopic Fat
caption: 76-year-old woman with a left adrenal mass (arrow) incidentally discovered on unenhanced CT performed for abdominal pain. Note the significant amount of macroscopic fat throughout the mass, which is diagnostic of myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: A left adrenal mass containing a significant amount of macroscopic fat on unenhanced CT is diagnostic of:
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic (bulk) fat — measuring around −30 to −100 HU — within an adrenal mass is essentially diagnostic of a benign myelolipoma, a tumor composed of mature fat and hematopoietic (myeloid) elements. This differs from the microscopic intracytoplasmic lipid of an adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Bulk fat density in the adrenal = myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: How does the fat in a myelolipoma differ from the lipid in a lipid-rich adenoma?
opts: A myelolipoma contains macroscopic (bulk) fat measuring very negative HU, whereas an adenoma contains microscopic intracytoplasmic lipid giving ≤10 HU and opposed-phase signal drop
mcqs.exp: Myelolipomas have gross fat (macroscopic, markedly negative HU, and India-ink/opposed-phase etching only at fat–water interfaces). Lipid-rich adenomas contain microscopic intracytoplasmic lipid, giving ≤10 HU and diffuse opposed-phase signal loss — a different mechanism.
mcqs.hint: Bulk fat vs. fat mixed inside cells.

mcqs.q: What is the usual management of an incidental, asymptomatic adrenal myelolipoma?
opts: Conservative observation, with resection reserved for large, symptomatic, growing or hemorrhage-prone lesions
mcqs.exp: Because myelolipomas are benign and usually non-functioning, small asymptomatic lesions are observed. Large (often >4–6 cm), symptomatic or hemorrhage-prone lesions may be resected. A brief hormonal screen is still part of the incidentaloma work-up.
mcqs.hint: Benign and quiet — watch unless big/symptomatic.

title: Post-Traumatic Adrenal Hematoma – Follow-up Regression
caption: 59-year-old man who presented with pain after a motor vehicle collision. (A) Trauma-protocol contrast-enhanced CT (portal venous phase) shows an indeterminate adrenal mass (arrow) measuring 3.3 × 2.3 cm, attenuation 52 HU. (B) Six-week follow-up in-phase T1-weighted MR image shows the mass has decreased in size to 2.2 × 1.2 cm; a hypointense rim surrounding the mass indicates chronic blood products (e.g., hemosiderin).

mcqs.q: In a trauma patient, an adrenal mass measuring 52 HU that decreases in size on follow-up is most consistent with:
mcqs.exp: Acute adrenal hemorrhage is typically hyperattenuating (often 50–90 HU) and, unlike a neoplasm, decreases in size and attenuation over weeks. The trauma history plus interval regression is characteristic of a hematoma.
mcqs.hint: High density in a trauma patient that shrinks over time = clot.

mcqs.q: On the 6-week follow-up MRI, a hypointense rim surrounds the mass. What does this represent?
opts: Hemosiderin (chronic blood products) from prior hemorrhage
mcqs.exp: A hypointense (dark) rim on T1/T2 reflects hemosiderin deposition from breakdown of blood products, a hallmark of an evolving/organizing hematoma. Its presence supports a benign hemorrhagic etiology.
mcqs.hint: Dark rim = iron left behind by old blood.

mcqs.q: Why is short-interval follow-up imaging the appropriate strategy for an indeterminate adrenal mass after trauma?
opts: Because a true hematoma will decrease/resolve over weeks, whereas a persistent or enlarging lesion would suggest an underlying tumor needing further work-up
Because biopsy is always required first
mcqs.exp: Follow-up documents the expected regression of a hematoma. A lesion that fails to shrink, persists, or enlarges raises concern for an underlying neoplasm (sometimes hemorrhage occurs into a tumor) and prompts further characterization or resection.
mcqs.hint: Let time sort it out — clots shrink, tumors don't.

title: Adrenal Metastases – Necrosis, Indeterminate CT & FDG-PET
caption: Two patients with metastatic disease in the adrenal gland. (A) 62-year-old man with widely metastatic melanoma: left adrenal metastasis with central necrosis (arrow). (B, C) 48-year-old woman with recurrent colon cancer metastatic to the right adrenal gland: contrast-enhanced CT (portal venous phase, B) shows a heterogeneously enhancing indeterminate mass (arrow), attenuation 55 HU; FDG-PET (C) shows significant metabolic activity (arrow), SUV 10.8, diagnostic of metastatic disease in this patient with known malignancy.

mcqs.q: In panel A, the left adrenal metastasis from melanoma shows central necrosis. Why does central necrosis favor malignancy over a benign adenoma?
opts: Rapidly growing malignant tissue outgrows its blood supply, producing central necrosis, whereas small benign adenomas are typically homogeneous
Necrosis proves it is a cyst
mcqs.exp: Central necrosis reflects a tumor outgrowing its vascular supply — a feature of larger/aggressive malignancies (metastases, ACC) and pheochromocytoma. Benign lipid-rich adenomas are usually small and homogeneous, so heterogeneity/necrosis is a red flag.
mcqs.hint: Fast-growing tumors outstrip their blood supply and die centrally.

mcqs.q: The colon-cancer metastasis in panel B measures 55 HU on contrast-enhanced CT and is 'indeterminate'. Why can't this CT confirm it is benign?
opts: Because 55 HU on contrast CT does not meet any benign criterion (it is not ≤10 HU unenhanced), so the lesion needs washout, MRI, or PET
Because CT cannot show the adrenal
mcqs.exp: The ≤10 HU benign threshold applies to UNENHANCED CT. A post-contrast value of 55 HU says nothing about benignity; without unenhanced/washout data the mass is indeterminate and needs further characterization — here FDG-PET.
mcqs.hint: The 10 HU rule is for unenhanced scans, not this one.

mcqs.q: Panel C shows the colon-cancer metastasis with an SUV of 10.8 on FDG-PET. How does this help?
opts: Markedly increased FDG uptake (well above liver) indicates a hypermetabolic malignant lesion — a metastasis in this patient with known cancer
mcqs.exp: An SUV of 10.8 with uptake far above liver background indicates malignancy. In a patient with known colon cancer this confirms an adrenal metastasis, resolving the indeterminate CT and changing staging/management.
mcqs.hint: Very high SUV, far above liver = malignant.

mcqs.q: Both patients had a KNOWN primary malignancy. How does that pretest context change interpretation of an indeterminate adrenal mass?
opts: It raises the pretest probability of metastasis, lowering the threshold to further characterize or biopsy an indeterminate adrenal lesion
It means the mass is always benign
mcqs.exp: In patients with a known malignancy, a substantial fraction of new adrenal masses are metastases, so an indeterminate lesion is pursued more aggressively (washout CT, chemical-shift MRI, PET, or biopsy) because the result affects staging and treatment.
mcqs.hint: Known cancer raises the odds the adrenal lesion is a metastasis.

mcqs.q: Which combination of features across these two patients is most typical of adrenal metastasis rather than adenoma?
opts: Heterogeneous/necrotic mass, attenuation well above 10 HU, FDG-avid, in a patient with a known primary
mcqs.exp: Adrenal metastases tend to be heterogeneous (sometimes necrotic), soft-tissue attenuation (>10 HU), FDG-avid, and occur in patients with a known cancer. Adenomas are usually small, homogeneous, low-attenuation and not avid. Washout is variable, so it is less reliable here.
mcqs.hint: Heterogeneous, dense, PET-hot, plus a known cancer.

title: Pheochromocytoma in Neurofibromatosis – Nonspecific CT, Biochemical Diagnosis
caption: 45-year-old man with a history of neurofibromatosis and syncope. Routine contrast-enhanced CT shows a solid left adrenal mass with nonspecific imaging characteristics (mean attenuation 71.15 HU, ROI). Biochemical evaluation found a urine metanephrine level of 2320 µg/d (normal 30–350 µg/d). Findings were diagnostic of pheochromocytoma.

mcqs.q: A solid left adrenal mass of 71 HU with nonspecific imaging in a patient with neurofibromatosis and syncope should prompt which key biochemical test?
mcqs.exp: Neurofibromatosis type 1 is associated with pheochromocytoma, and syncope may reflect catecholamine surges. A solid, high-attenuation, nonspecific adrenal mass in this setting must be evaluated with metanephrines before any intervention.
mcqs.hint: NF1 + adrenal mass + spells → measure metanephrines.

mcqs.q: The urine metanephrine level was 2320 µg/d (normal 30–350). What does this establish and why does it matter before any biopsy or surgery?
opts: It is markedly elevated, diagnostic of a catecholamine-secreting pheochromocytoma — which mandates alpha-blockade and avoids unprepared biopsy/manipulation
mcqs.exp: A metanephrine level ~7× the upper limit is diagnostic of pheochromocytoma. This mandates preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade (then beta-blockade) to prevent hypertensive crisis, and biopsy is avoided because manipulation can trigger a catecholamine crisis.
mcqs.hint: Sky-high metanephrines = pheo → block alpha-receptors, don't poke it.

mcqs.q: Neurofibromatosis type 1 belongs to which group of conditions predisposing to pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma?
opts: Hereditary syndromes (with VHL, MEN2, and SDHx-related paraganglioma syndromes)
mcqs.exp: NF1, von Hippel–Lindau (VHL), MEN2 (RET), and SDHx mutations are hereditary predispositions to pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma. Recognizing a syndromic context prompts genetic evaluation and screening for multifocal or bilateral disease.
mcqs.hint: NF1 joins VHL, MEN2 and SDH as inherited pheo syndromes.

title: Adrenocortical Carcinoma – Central Necrosis & Venous Invasion
caption: 22-year-old man with vague abdominal pain. Transverse (A) and coronal (B) CT images show a large, centrally necrotic left adrenal cortical carcinoma (asterisk). Note vascular invasion (arrows) through the adrenal vein into the left renal vein and inferior vena cava.

mcqs.q: A large, centrally necrotic left adrenal mass with tumor extending through the adrenal vein into the renal vein and IVC is most consistent with:
mcqs.exp: Large size, central necrosis (heterogeneity), and venous (adrenal vein → renal vein → IVC) invasion are hallmarks of adrenocortical carcinoma. Venous tumor thrombus in particular indicates an aggressive malignancy.
mcqs.hint: Big, necrotic, invading veins in a young adult = ACC.

mcqs.q: Why is identifying tumor extension into the renal vein and IVC critical for management?
opts: Venous tumor thrombus alters surgical planning and staging (it may require IVC thrombectomy) and indicates advanced disease/worse prognosis
mcqs.exp: Recognizing venous invasion/tumor thrombus is essential for surgical planning (en-bloc resection, possible IVC thrombectomy, vascular surgery involvement) and for staging and prognosis. Missing it can lead to incomplete resection.
mcqs.hint: The surgeon needs to know the tumor is growing up the vein.

mcqs.q: ACC in a young adult with a large heterogeneous adrenal mass warrants which work-up in addition to staging?
opts: Biochemical evaluation for hormone hypersecretion (cortisol, androgens/estrogens, aldosterone) and exclusion of pheochromocytoma
mcqs.exp: Many ACCs are functional (cortisol and/or sex-steroid excess), so hormonal work-up is important for perioperative management and follow-up (steroidogenic markers can track recurrence). Pheochromocytoma is excluded biochemically before surgery/biopsy.
mcqs.hint: Cortex cancers often secrete hormones — screen them.

title: ACR Algorithm for the Incidental Adrenal Mass
caption: Flowchart for evaluation of an adrenal incidentaloma (≥1 cm) detected on CT or MRI, developed by the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee (Berland et al., J Am Coll Radiol 2010). Key branches: diagnostic benign features (myelolipoma with macroscopic fat/calcification, or HU ≤10 / signal drop on chemical-shift MRI = adenoma) require no follow-up; masses >4 cm are considered for resection (no cancer history) or PET/biopsy (cancer history); 1–4 cm lesions are triaged by prior imaging, cancer history, and washout CT (APW/RPW ≥60%/40% = adenoma). Consider biochemical evaluation if there are signs of hyperfunction, and biochemical testing to exclude pheochromocytoma.

mcqs.q: According to the ACR algorithm, an incidental adrenal mass containing macroscopic fat (myelolipoma) or measuring HU ≤10 (adenoma) should be managed how?
opts: Classified as benign with no imaging follow-up required
mcqs.exp: Lesions with diagnostic benign features — macroscopic fat/calcification (myelolipoma) or HU ≤10 / opposed-phase signal drop (adenoma) — are considered benign and need no follow-up. The algorithm reserves further work-up for indeterminate lesions.
mcqs.hint: Clearly benign signs = done, no follow-up.

mcqs.q: In the ACR algorithm, how is a mass LARGER than 4 cm without diagnostic benign features handled?
opts: Consider resection if there is no cancer history; consider PET or biopsy if there is a history of cancer
mcqs.exp: Size >4 cm raises concern for malignancy (ACC risk rises with size). Without a cancer history, resection is considered; with a cancer history, PET or biopsy is considered to assess for metastasis. Biochemical testing (including pheochromocytoma exclusion) accompanies this.
mcqs.hint: Big lesions: resect (no cancer history) or PET/biopsy (known cancer).

mcqs.q: For an indeterminate 1–4 cm adrenal mass with no prior imaging and no cancer history, what does the algorithm suggest?
opts: If it has benign imaging features, presume benign and consider 12-month follow-up CT/MR
mcqs.exp: A 1–4 cm lesion with benign features (homogeneous, low density, smooth margins) and no prior imaging or cancer history can be presumed benign with a suggested ~12-month follow-up to confirm stability. Suspicious features route to washout CT/CS-MR or biopsy.
mcqs.hint: Benign-looking and no red flags → short-term follow-up to confirm stability.

mcqs.q: In the washout-CT branch, which result confirms an adenoma?
mcqs.exp: On adrenal washout CT, an absolute percentage washout ≥60% (or relative ≥40%) is diagnostic of an adenoma (rapid contrast washout). Values below these thresholds are suspicious and prompt biopsy (if appropriate) or chemical-shift MRI. Note: no enhancement (≤10 HU) indicates a cyst/hemorrhage.
mcqs.hint: Rapid washout — APW ≥60% or RPW ≥40% — means adenoma.

mcqs.q: The algorithm's footnotes emphasize which two biochemical considerations for adrenal incidentalomas?
opts: Consider biochemical evaluation if there are signs/symptoms of adrenal hyperfunction, and biochemical testing to exclude pheochromocytoma
mcqs.exp: The ACR algorithm notes that biochemical evaluation should be considered when there are clinical signs of adrenal hyperfunction (e.g., Cushing, hyperaldosteronism), and that pheochromocytoma should be biochemically excluded — particularly before any biopsy or surgery. Imaging and biochemistry are complementary.
mcqs.hint: Screen for hyperfunction, and always rule out pheo before intervention.

title: Modified Incidentaloma Algorithm – Biochemistry & Follow-up
caption: Modified (from Fig 7) algorithm for evaluation of an incidental adrenal mass detected by CT or MRI (with permission from Elsevier). Adds practical detail: hormonal evaluation uses a 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test (or 24-h urinary cortisol), plasma/urinary metanephrines, and the aldosterone-to-renin ratio (if hypertension or low potassium). Enlarging lesions prompt further imaging or resection after biochemical evaluation for pheochromocytoma. Functional lesions are considered for resection; nonfunctional lesions >2.4 cm get yearly cortisol follow-up for 4 years. Some experts recommend resecting masses ≥4 cm even if nonfunctional because of increased adrenal-cancer risk.

mcqs.q: According to this algorithm, the hormonal evaluation of an adrenal incidentaloma routinely includes which tests?
opts: 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test (or 24-h urinary cortisol), plasma/urinary metanephrines, and aldosterone-to-renin ratio if hypertensive or hypokalemic
mcqs.exp: Standard biochemical work-up screens for autonomous cortisol (1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test or 24-h urinary free cortisol), pheochromocytoma (plasma/urinary metanephrines), and primary aldosteronism (aldosterone-to-renin ratio) in patients with hypertension or hypokalemia.
mcqs.hint: Cortisol autonomy, catecholamines, and aldosterone (if hypertensive/low K+).

mcqs.q: The algorithm advises that an ENLARGING adrenal mass should prompt what — and with which precaution?
opts: Further imaging or resection, but only after biochemical evaluation to exclude pheochromocytoma
mcqs.exp: An enlarging lesion is concerning for malignancy and is considered for resection, but pheochromocytoma must be biochemically excluded first so that a catecholamine crisis is not provoked during biopsy or surgery.
mcqs.hint: Growth → intervene, but rule out pheo before touching it.

mcqs.q: For a NONFUNCTIONAL adrenal mass with benign (adenoma) features, when does this algorithm suggest yearly cortisol follow-up for 4 years?
opts: For all lesions regardless of size
When the mass is larger than 2.4 cm
Only if the mass is under 1 cm
mcqs.exp: The algorithm suggests yearly follow-up for excess cortisol for 4 years if a nonfunctional adenoma-appearing mass is >2.4 cm, because larger lesions carry a higher chance of developing autonomous cortisol secretion over time.
mcqs.hint: Larger benign-appearing adenomas (>2.4 cm) get repeat cortisol screening.

mcqs.q: The footnote notes some experts recommend resecting nonfunctional masses ≥4 cm. Why?
opts: Because larger size carries an increased risk of adrenocortical carcinoma
mcqs.exp: The risk of adrenocortical carcinoma rises with lesion size, so some experts resect masses ≥4 cm even when nonfunctional. The guidelines acknowledge that masses with clearly benign adenoma features may alternatively be followed with imaging and biochemistry.
mcqs.hint: Bigger lesions carry more cancer risk, so some remove them.

mcqs.q: How does this modified algorithm say unenhanced CT or chemical-shift MRI can be used in patients with a cancer history?
opts: They cannot be used in cancer patients
They may alternatively be used to characterize 1–4 cm masses in patients with a history of cancer and no prior imaging
They only work for masses >4 cm
mcqs.exp: In patients with a cancer history and no prior imaging, unenhanced CT (HU ≤10) or chemical-shift MRI (opposed-phase signal drop) can characterize an indeterminate 1–4 cm mass as a benign adenoma, potentially avoiding biopsy. Washout CT is added if results remain indeterminate.
mcqs.hint: Low HU or opposed-phase dropout can still prove adenoma even with known cancer.

title: Right Adrenal ACC – No Opposed-Phase Signal Drop
caption: 50-year-old woman with left loin pain of gradual onset, progressive over months. (a) Axial unenhanced CT shows a rather large right suprarenal soft-tissue mass measuring 6.6 × 5.2 cm with mean attenuation 19 HU. Adrenal-protocol MRI shows intermediate signal on T1 (b) and T2 (c), bright signal on diffusion (d) denoting restricted diffusion, and NO signal drop from in-phase (e) to opposed-phase (f). Diagnosis: right suprarenal malignant neoplasm, pathologically confirmed adrenocortical carcinoma.

mcqs.q: This 6.6 × 5.2 cm right adrenal mass measures 19 HU on unenhanced CT. Does that attenuation help characterize it as benign?
opts: Yes, 19 HU is diagnostic of adenoma
No — 19 HU is above the ≤10 HU benign threshold, so it does not indicate a lipid-rich adenoma and the lesion remains indeterminate/suspicious
mcqs.exp: Only unenhanced attenuation ≤10 HU is diagnostic of a lipid-rich adenoma. At 19 HU the mass is lipid-poor and cannot be called benign on density alone; its large size and other features raise concern for malignancy.
mcqs.hint: 19 HU is over the 10 HU cutoff — not a lipid-rich adenoma.

mcqs.q: On chemical-shift MRI this mass shows NO signal drop from in-phase to opposed-phase. What does the absence of signal drop indicate?
opts: Lack of intracytoplasmic lipid — the lesion is not a lipid-rich adenoma, keeping malignancy in the differential
mcqs.exp: Opposed-phase signal DROP indicates intracytoplasmic lipid (adenoma). No signal drop means little/no intracellular lipid, so an adenoma is not supported; combined with size and restricted diffusion this favors a malignant lesion (here ACC).
mcqs.hint: No opposed-phase dropout = no intracellular fat = not an adenoma.

mcqs.q: The mass shows restricted diffusion (bright on DWI). In this context, what does that suggest?
opts: High cellularity typical of a malignant/solid tumor
mcqs.exp: Restricted diffusion reflects dense cellularity, common in malignant tumors. While not specific, in a large lipid-poor mass without opposed-phase signal drop it reinforces concern for malignancy such as adrenocortical carcinoma.
mcqs.hint: Bright on DWI = densely packed cells, seen in malignancy.

mcqs.q: Which single feature here is MOST worrisome for adrenocortical carcinoma?
opts: The combination of large size (>4 cm), lack of intracytoplasmic lipid, and restricted diffusion in a solid mass
mcqs.exp: No single feature is diagnostic, but a large (>4 cm) solid mass that is lipid-poor (19 HU, no opposed-phase drop) with restricted diffusion is highly suspicious for ACC and warrants resection and histology, which confirmed the diagnosis.
mcqs.hint: Big + lipid-poor + restricted diffusion together point to ACC.

mcqs.q: Given these findings, what is the appropriate management?
opts: Biochemical work-up (including pheochromocytoma exclusion) and surgical resection with histopathology
mcqs.exp: A large, indeterminate, lipid-poor adrenal mass with suspicious features needs full biochemical evaluation (excluding pheochromocytoma) and resection for definitive histologic diagnosis — which established adrenocortical carcinoma here.
mcqs.hint: Suspicious solid mass → screen hormones, exclude pheo, then resect.

title: Bilateral Lipid-Rich Adenomas in a Cancer Patient
caption: 60-year-old man with progressive hematuria, diagnosed with bladder carcinoma. (a) Precontrast and (b) post-contrast MDCT show bilateral hypodense suprarenal nodules measuring 1.6 cm (right) and 1.7 cm (left); RPW = 63% on both sides. MRI (c) axial T1, (d) axial T2 show bilateral nodules of intermediate signal; (e, f) in-phase and opposed-phase images show remarkable loss of signal on opposed-phase, denoting intracytoplasmic fat. Small size favors lipid-rich adenomas. Diagnosis was further proven by PET/CT showing no suprarenal metastasis.

mcqs.q: Bilateral small hypodense adrenal nodules with remarkable opposed-phase signal loss are most consistent with:
mcqs.exp: Opposed-phase signal loss indicates intracytoplasmic lipid — the signature of lipid-rich adenomas. Small size and bilaterality with clear signal drop favor benign adenomas over metastases, even in a patient with a known cancer.
mcqs.hint: Opposed-phase dropout + small size = lipid-rich adenomas.

mcqs.q: The RPW was 63% on both sides. What does a relative percentage washout of 63% indicate, and why is RPW used here?
opts: It is below threshold, indicating malignancy
It is well above the 40% threshold, indicating an adenoma; RPW is used because it does not require an unenhanced scan
mcqs.exp: RPW ≥40% is diagnostic of an adenoma. At 63% these nodules wash out rapidly, confirming adenomas. RPW = (enhanced − delayed)/enhanced × 100 and needs only post-contrast and delayed images (no unenhanced acquisition).
mcqs.hint: 63% clears the 40% RPW bar for adenoma; no unenhanced scan needed.

mcqs.q: This patient has bladder carcinoma. Why is it important to correctly characterize these nodules as adenomas rather than metastases?
opts: Because misclassifying benign adenomas as metastases would incorrectly upstage the cancer and could change (or deny) curative treatment
mcqs.exp: In a cancer patient, calling benign adenomas 'metastases' would falsely upstage disease and could withhold curative therapy or prompt unnecessary treatment. Proper characterization (chemical-shift MRI, washout, PET) prevents this error.
mcqs.hint: Wrongly calling them metastases would upstage a potentially curable cancer.

mcqs.q: PET/CT here showed no suprarenal uptake. How does that support the diagnosis?
opts: Absence of FDG uptake (at/below liver) supports benign adenomas rather than metastatic disease
mcqs.exp: Metastases are typically FDG-avid, whereas benign adenomas usually show uptake at or below liver background. No suprarenal uptake corroborates the chemical-shift and washout findings that these are benign adenomas.
mcqs.hint: No PET uptake = not metabolically active = supports benign.

mcqs.q: Which combination of features in this case most confidently establishes benign adenomas?
opts: Small size, opposed-phase signal drop, RPW >40%, and no FDG uptake
mcqs.exp: Multiple concordant benign signs — small size, opposed-phase signal loss (intracytoplasmic lipid), rapid washout (RPW 63%), and lack of FDG avidity — together confidently establish lipid-rich adenomas, even against a background malignancy.
mcqs.hint: Several benign signs agreeing: dropout + fast washout + PET-negative + small.

title: Right Adrenal Metastasis – Poor Washout, No Signal Drop
caption: 19-year-old man with a brain space-occupying lesion surgically removed and diagnosed as lymphoma. (a) MDCT shows a right suprarenal soft-tissue mass measuring 6.2 × 4.8 cm, mean attenuation 33 HU on unenhanced images; APW = 22%, RPW = 4%, denoting poor lipid content. MRI adrenal protocol shows iso-signal on T1 (b), intermediate T2 (c), diffusion restriction (d), and NO drop of signal on opposed-phase (f) compared to in-phase (e). Diagnosis: right suprarenal metastatic lesion.

mcqs.q: This right adrenal mass measures 33 HU unenhanced with APW 22% and RPW 4%. How do these washout values classify it?
opts: As NON-adenoma / indeterminate-to-suspicious, because APW <60% and RPW <40% (poor washout)
mcqs.exp: Adenomas require APW ≥60% or RPW ≥40%. Here APW 22% and RPW 4% are well below threshold (poor washout), so the lesion is not an adenoma and is suspicious — in this cancer patient, a metastasis.
mcqs.hint: Low APW/RPW = does not wash out like an adenoma.

mcqs.q: The mass shows NO opposed-phase signal drop and restricted diffusion. Together with 33 HU, what does this indicate?
opts: A lipid-poor, cellular solid mass — not an adenoma; suspicious for malignancy/metastasis
mcqs.exp: No opposed-phase signal drop means little intracytoplasmic lipid; 33 HU confirms it is lipid-poor; restricted diffusion indicates high cellularity. This constellation argues against adenoma and, in a patient with known malignancy, favors metastasis.
mcqs.hint: Lipid-poor + cellular (restricted diffusion) + no dropout = not an adenoma.

mcqs.q: How does this metastasis differ on chemical-shift MRI from the lipid-rich adenomas of the bladder-cancer patient?
opts: Adenomas show opposed-phase signal drop (intracytoplasmic lipid) whereas this metastasis shows NO drop
mcqs.exp: The key contrast: lipid-rich adenomas lose signal on opposed-phase images (fat–water cancellation), while this metastasis retains signal (no intracytoplasmic lipid). Chemical-shift behavior thus separates benign adenoma from metastasis.
