mcqs.hint: Adenoma dims on opposed-phase; this metastasis does not.

mcqs.q: Why does the patient's history (brain lymphoma) matter in interpreting this adrenal mass?
opts: It makes benign adenoma more likely
A known primary malignancy raises the pretest probability that a lipid-poor, poorly washing-out adrenal mass is a metastasis
mcqs.exp: With a known malignancy, an indeterminate adrenal mass that lacks benign features (lipid-poor, poor washout, no signal drop, restricted diffusion) is much more likely to be a metastasis, warranting confirmation and staging.
mcqs.hint: Known cancer raises the odds a non-adenoma mass is metastatic.

mcqs.q: Which management is most appropriate for this indeterminate, suspicious adrenal mass in a young cancer patient?
opts: Further confirmation (PET and/or biopsy after excluding pheochromocytoma) and treatment directed at the underlying malignancy
mcqs.exp: A suspicious, non-adenomatous adrenal mass in a patient with known cancer is confirmed with PET and/or image-guided biopsy (after biochemical exclusion of pheochromocytoma) and managed as metastatic disease with appropriate systemic/local therapy.
mcqs.hint: Confirm with PET/biopsy, exclude pheo, treat the cancer.

title: Simple Right Adrenal Cyst – No Solid Component
caption: 63-year-old man with occasional right upper abdominal pain of long duration. (a) Contrast-enhanced CT shows a thin-walled cystic right suprarenal lesion measuring 7 × 6 cm, with no solid component or significant mural enhancement and homogeneous internal fluid of 5 HU. Adrenal-protocol MRI shows low T1 signal (b), bright T2 signal (c), no diffusion restriction (d) — eliminating an abscess — and no signal drop on opposed-phase (f) versus in-phase (e). Diagnosis: simple right suprarenal cyst; no further evaluation required.

mcqs.q: A thin-walled right adrenal lesion of 5 HU with no solid component and no significant mural enhancement is diagnostic of:
mcqs.exp: Homogeneous near-water attenuation (≤10–20 HU, here 5 HU), a thin wall, no enhancing solid/nodular component, and no significant mural enhancement are diagnostic of a benign simple cyst. Such lesions need no further evaluation.
mcqs.hint: Water-density, thin wall, no solid enhancing tissue = simple cyst.

mcqs.q: On MRI the lesion is low on T1, bright on T2, with NO diffusion restriction. Why is the lack of restricted diffusion specifically noted?
opts: Because it helps exclude an abscess (which would restrict diffusion) and supports simple fluid
mcqs.exp: An abscess contains viscous pus that restricts diffusion (bright on DWI, low ADC). The absence of restricted diffusion argues against infection, supporting a simple (sterile) fluid-filled cyst — important when there is abdominal pain.
mcqs.hint: No diffusion restriction rules out pus/abscess.

mcqs.q: The cyst shows bright T2 and low T1 signal. What does this signal pattern indicate?
mcqs.exp: Simple fluid follows water signal — low on T1 and bright on T2 — matching a benign cyst. Complicated/hemorrhagic or proteinaceous cysts would show higher T1 signal, and solid tumors would enhance and have intermediate signal.
mcqs.hint: Water signal: dark T1, bright T2.

mcqs.q: Absence of opposed-phase signal drop in this lesion is expected because:
opts: A simple cyst contains fluid, not intracytoplasmic lipid, so there is no fat–water cancellation to cause signal drop
mcqs.exp: Opposed-phase signal drop requires fat and water within the same voxel (intracytoplasmic lipid, as in adenomas). A simple fluid-filled cyst has no such lipid, so it does not lose signal on opposed-phase imaging — consistent with a cyst, not an adenoma.
mcqs.hint: No intracellular fat in fluid → no opposed-phase dropout.

mcqs.q: The report concludes 'no further evaluation required.' What justifies this for a 7-cm cyst?
opts: All large adrenal lesions need surgery
Because it meets strict benign simple-cyst criteria (thin wall, no enhancing solid component, simple fluid, no restriction), size alone does not mandate intervention
mcqs.exp: A lesion meeting all simple-cyst criteria is confidently benign regardless of size; intervention is reserved for cysts with worrisome features (thick/nodular enhancing walls, solid components, hemorrhage) or symptoms. This asymptomatic simple cyst needs no further work-up.
mcqs.hint: If it fully meets simple-cyst criteria, size alone isn't a red flag.

title: Giant Left Adrenal Myelolipoma – Macroscopic Fat (−70 HU)
caption: 50-year-old woman with SLE and elevated creatinine from associated nephropathy, referred for CT urography. (a) Unenhanced CT shows a left suprarenal soft-tissue mass measuring 10 cm with heterogeneous attenuation and areas of pure fat measuring −70 HU. Adrenal-protocol MRI shows areas of high T1 (b), less bright T2 (c), and facilitated diffusion (d); these areas show marked drop of signal on fat-suppressed images. Diagnosis: left suprarenal myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: A large left adrenal mass containing areas of pure fat measuring −70 HU on unenhanced CT is diagnostic of:
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic (bulk) fat at markedly negative attenuation (−70 HU) is essentially diagnostic of a myelolipoma, a benign tumor of mature fat and hematopoietic tissue. Even at 10 cm, the macroscopic fat establishes benignity.
mcqs.hint: Pure fat density (very negative HU) = myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: The fatty areas show HIGH T1 signal that DROPS markedly on FAT-SUPPRESSED images. What does this behavior confirm?
opts: Macroscopic (bulk) fat, since fat is bright on T1 and suppresses on fat-saturated sequences
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic fat is bright on T1 and loses signal on frequency-selective fat-suppressed sequences. This differs from the intracytoplasmic lipid of an adenoma, which drops on OPPOSED-PHASE (not primarily on fat suppression). The fat-suppression drop confirms bulk fat.
mcqs.hint: Bright on T1, dark on fat-sat = macroscopic fat.

mcqs.q: How does a myelolipoma's fat behave differently from a lipid-rich adenoma on MRI?
opts: Myelolipoma has macroscopic fat (bright T1, suppresses on fat-sat, India-ink etching only at fat–water borders); adenoma has intracytoplasmic lipid (diffuse opposed-phase signal drop)
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic fat (myelolipoma) suppresses on fat-saturated sequences and shows India-ink artifact only at fat–water interfaces. Intracytoplasmic lipid (adenoma) causes diffuse signal loss across the whole lesion on opposed-phase images. The mechanisms and appearances differ.
mcqs.hint: Bulk fat suppresses on fat-sat; cellular lipid dims on opposed-phase.

mcqs.q: This patient has SLE with nephropathy and elevated creatinine. Why does the confident CT/MRI diagnosis of myelolipoma matter clinically here?
opts: A confident benign diagnosis avoids further contrast-heavy or invasive work-up, which is desirable given her renal impairment
mcqs.exp: Because macroscopic fat gives a confident benign diagnosis, no further characterization (e.g., contrast washout studies risky in renal impairment, or biopsy) is needed. This spares a patient with nephropathy additional contrast exposure and procedures.
mcqs.hint: A sure benign call avoids extra contrast/biopsy in a patient with poor kidneys.

mcqs.q: What is the general management of this large but characteristically benign myelolipoma?
opts: Observation, with resection considered if large/symptomatic or at risk of hemorrhage
mcqs.exp: Myelolipomas are benign; management is conservative, though large lesions (often >4–6 cm) may be resected if symptomatic, growing, or at risk of spontaneous hemorrhage. A brief hormonal screen is still part of incidentaloma work-up.
mcqs.hint: Benign — observe, resect only if large/symptomatic/bleeding risk.

title: Left Adrenal Adenoma – Low HU & Opposed-Phase Signal Loss
caption: Adrenal adenoma in a 45-year-old woman. (a) Unenhanced CT shows a left adrenal mass (arrow) with attenuation lower than 10 HU. Axial T1-weighted in-phase (b) and opposed-phase (c) gradient-recalled-echo MR images show prominent loss of signal on opposed-phase.

mcqs.q: A left adrenal mass measuring less than 10 HU on unenhanced CT is:
opts: Diagnostic of a benign lipid-rich adenoma (or cyst)
mcqs.exp: Unenhanced attenuation ≤10 HU indicates abundant intracytoplasmic lipid (or fluid) and is diagnostic of a benign lipid-rich adenoma (or cyst). This single criterion characterizes the majority of adenomas without further imaging.
mcqs.hint: ≤10 HU unenhanced settles it as a lipid-rich adenoma.

mcqs.q: Prominent loss of signal on the OPPOSED-PHASE image (relative to in-phase) indicates what?
opts: Intracytoplasmic lipid, confirming a lipid-rich adenoma
mcqs.exp: Signal loss on opposed-phase images results from fat–water cancellation within voxels containing intracytoplasmic lipid — the hallmark of a lipid-rich adenoma. This corroborates the low CT attenuation.
mcqs.hint: Opposed-phase dropout = intracellular fat = adenoma.

mcqs.q: In this case CT and MRI agree. What is the practical value of chemical-shift MRI when CT already shows ≤10 HU?
opts: CT ≤10 HU is already diagnostic, but chemical-shift MRI is especially valuable for INDETERMINATE (lipid-poor, 10–30 HU) lesions or when CT is equivocal or contrast is contraindicated
mcqs.exp: When unenhanced CT already shows ≤10 HU, the adenoma is confirmed. Chemical-shift MRI adds most value for lipid-poor adenomas (10–30 HU) that are indeterminate on CT, or when radiation/contrast should be avoided, by detecting intracytoplasmic lipid.
mcqs.hint: MRI earns its keep on the borderline lipid-poor lesions, not the already-obvious ≤10 HU ones.

title: Bilateral Adrenal Hyperplasia – Nodular Enlargement with Lipid
caption: Bilateral adrenal hyperplasia in a 75-year-old woman. Axial T1-weighted in-phase (a) and out-of-phase gradient-recalled-echo (c), T2-weighted fast spin echo (b/c), and three-dimensional fat-suppressed T1-weighted gradient-recalled-echo image in the portal phase after contrast (d) show nodular enlargement of both adrenal glands (arrows) with loss of signal on out-of-phase imaging.

mcqs.q: Nodular enlargement of BOTH adrenal glands with loss of signal on out-of-phase imaging is most consistent with:
mcqs.exp: Bilateral, relatively symmetric nodular gland enlargement that loses signal on opposed-/out-of-phase imaging (intracytoplasmic lipid) suggests hyperplasia. The lipid-containing tissue behaves like adenomatous tissue, and the bilateral diffuse/nodular pattern favors hyperplasia over discrete tumors.
mcqs.hint: Both glands enlarged + opposed-phase dropout = lipid-rich hyperplasia.

mcqs.q: The out-of-phase signal loss in these hyperplastic adrenals reflects what, and how does it resemble an adenoma?
opts: Intracytoplasmic lipid — the same fat–water cancellation that makes lipid-rich adenomas lose signal on opposed-phase
mcqs.exp: Hyperplastic adrenal cortical tissue can contain intracytoplasmic lipid, so it loses signal on opposed-phase images just as a lipid-rich adenoma does. The distinction from adenoma rests on the bilateral, diffuse/nodular gland enlargement rather than a solitary mass.
mcqs.hint: Same intracellular-fat mechanism as an adenoma, but affecting whole glands.

mcqs.q: Which pattern best distinguishes bilateral hyperplasia from a solitary adenoma?
opts: A single discrete mass in one gland
Diffuse and/or nodular enlargement of BOTH adrenal glands rather than a single dominant nodule
mcqs.exp: Hyperplasia typically enlarges both glands diffusely or with multiple nodules, preserving the adreniform shape, whereas an adenoma is usually a solitary discrete nodule. Bilaterality and gland-wide involvement are the key clues.
mcqs.hint: Both glands enlarged vs. one discrete nodule.

mcqs.q: What biochemical/clinical evaluation is appropriate when bilateral adrenal hyperplasia is found?
opts: Screen for hormone excess (e.g., cortisol autonomy, aldosterone if hypertensive/hypokalemic, and consider ACTH-driven or congenital causes)
mcqs.exp: Bilateral hyperplasia can be associated with hormone excess (e.g., ACTH-dependent Cushing, primary aldosteronism from bilateral hyperplasia, or congenital adrenal hyperplasia). Biochemical screening guides diagnosis and management, and correlation with ACTH and clinical context is important.
mcqs.hint: Look for hormone excess and its cause (ACTH-driven, aldosterone, CAH).

mcqs.q: Why does contrast-enhanced fat-suppressed imaging (panel d) complement the chemical-shift images here?
opts: It confirms enhancing adrenal tissue (viable gland) and helps map the bilateral nodular enlargement, while chemical-shift confirms the lipid content
mcqs.exp: Post-contrast fat-suppressed imaging demonstrates the enhancing, viable adrenal tissue and delineates the nodular gland enlargement, while the in-/out-of-phase images establish intracytoplasmic lipid. Together they characterize hyperplasia and exclude a discrete suspicious mass.
mcqs.hint: Contrast shows viable enhancing glands; chemical-shift shows the lipid.

title: Right Adrenal Hemorrhage on Anticoagulation
caption: Right adrenal hemorrhage in a 61-year-old man with acute abdominal pain after starting anticoagulant therapy. Axial unenhanced (a) and arterial-phase (b) CT images show a large right adrenal hematoma (arrow) with relatively high attenuation values and without enhancement after contrast injection.

mcqs.q: A large right adrenal mass with relatively HIGH attenuation on unenhanced CT and NO enhancement, in a patient who just started anticoagulation, is most consistent with:
mcqs.exp: Acute hematoma is typically hyperattenuating on unenhanced CT (clotted blood) and shows NO enhancement (no viable vascularized tumor). The clinical context of new anticoagulation and acute pain strongly supports hemorrhage.
mcqs.hint: High density + no enhancement + new anticoagulant = bleed.

mcqs.q: Why is the ABSENCE of enhancement after contrast important in distinguishing a hematoma from a tumor?
opts: A hematoma contains non-viable clot that does not enhance, whereas a solid tumor has vascularized tissue that enhances
mcqs.exp: Viable tumor tissue is perfused and enhances after contrast; clotted blood is avascular and does not. Lack of internal enhancement therefore favors a hematoma — although hemorrhage into an underlying tumor must be excluded on follow-up.
mcqs.hint: No perfusion, no enhancement — clot rather than living tumor.

mcqs.q: What is the appropriate follow-up strategy for this adrenal hematoma?
opts: Short-interval follow-up imaging to confirm expected regression, with further work-up if it fails to resolve (to exclude an underlying mass)
mcqs.exp: Uncomplicated hematomas decrease over weeks. Follow-up imaging confirms regression; a lesion that persists or enlarges raises concern for hemorrhage into an underlying tumor and prompts further characterization. Bilateral adrenal hemorrhage additionally requires assessment for adrenal insufficiency.
mcqs.hint: Re-image to confirm it shrinks; investigate if it doesn't.

title: Right Adrenal Simple Cyst – No Enhancement or Septations
caption: Right adrenal cyst in a 50-year-old woman. Axial T2-weighted fat-saturated MR image (a), and 3D fat-suppressed T1-weighted gradient-recalled-echo images before (b) and after (c) paramagnetic contrast administration show a right adrenal cystic lesion (arrow) with low T1 signal intensity, high T2 signal intensity, no contrast enhancement, and no septations, blood products, soft-tissue components, or calcifications.

mcqs.q: A right adrenal lesion with low T1, high T2 signal, no enhancement, and no septations/soft-tissue/calcification is diagnostic of:
mcqs.exp: Simple fluid signal (dark T1, bright T2) with no enhancing solid component, septations, blood products, or calcification is diagnostic of a benign simple cyst. The absence of complicating features confirms benignity.
mcqs.hint: Water signal + no enhancing/solid/septal features = simple cyst.

mcqs.q: Why is the absence of contrast enhancement critical to calling this a simple cyst?
opts: Enhancement would indicate viable solid/septal tissue and raise concern for a cystic neoplasm; its absence supports pure fluid
mcqs.exp: Any enhancing solid component, thick/nodular septum, or mural nodularity would raise concern for a cystic neoplasm. A purely non-enhancing fluid collection with a thin wall is characteristic of a benign simple cyst.
mcqs.hint: No enhancing tissue means no worrisome solid component.

mcqs.q: Which additional features are specifically noted as ABSENT here, and why does that matter?
opts: Fat and calcification, which would indicate myelolipoma
Septations, blood products, soft-tissue components, and calcifications — their absence distinguishes a simple cyst from a complicated/hemorrhagic cyst or cystic tumor
mcqs.exp: Septations, blood products, soft-tissue nodularity, and calcification would suggest a complicated cyst, prior hemorrhage, or a cystic neoplasm. Their absence confirms an uncomplicated simple cyst that needs no further work-up.
mcqs.hint: None of the 'complicated' features are present, so it's a simple cyst.

title: Left Adrenal Myelolipoma – Fat & Myeloid Components on MRI
caption: Left adrenal myelolipoma incidentally discovered on a lumbar CT in a 51-year-old man. Unenhanced CT (a) shows a left adrenal mass with macroscopic fat (arrowhead) and a soft-tissue myeloid component (arrow). Axial T2-weighted fast spin echo (b), T2-weighted fat-saturated (c), T1-weighted in-phase (d), and out-of-phase gradient-recalled-echo (e) images show the signal drop of macroscopic fat on fat-suppressed images (c) and loss of signal (India-ink) at the interface of the myeloid element on out-of-phase imaging (e).

mcqs.q: A left adrenal mass with macroscopic fat AND a soft-tissue myeloid component is characteristic of which tumor?
mcqs.exp: A myelolipoma is composed of mature adipose tissue and hematopoietic (myeloid) elements. The combination of macroscopic fat and a soft-tissue myeloid component on imaging is characteristic and benign.
mcqs.hint: Fat plus a marrow-like soft-tissue component = myelolipoma.

mcqs.q: The macroscopic fat DROPS in signal on FAT-SATURATED images (panel c). What does this confirm?
opts: Bulk macroscopic fat, which suppresses on frequency-selective fat-saturation sequences
mcqs.exp: Macroscopic fat loses signal on fat-saturated sequences (which selectively null fat). This confirms the fatty component is bulk fat (as in myelolipoma), distinct from the intracytoplasmic lipid of an adenoma.
mcqs.hint: Fat suppresses on fat-sat — that's bulk fat.

mcqs.q: On the out-of-phase image (panel e), signal loss occurs at the INTERFACE of the myeloid element (India-ink artifact). Why does this pattern differ from an adenoma?
opts: It is identical to an adenoma
India-ink (etching) artifact occurs only at fat–water BORDERS (macroscopic fat), whereas an adenoma loses signal DIFFUSELY throughout the lesion (intracytoplasmic lipid)
mcqs.exp: The India-ink/etching artifact is a thin dark line at macroscopic fat–water interfaces on opposed-phase images. In contrast, a lipid-rich adenoma shows DIFFUSE signal loss throughout because fat and water coexist within the same voxels. The location of signal loss distinguishes them.
mcqs.hint: Border etching (bulk fat) vs. whole-lesion dropout (adenoma).

mcqs.q: This myelolipoma was found incidentally on a lumbar CT. What is the appropriate management of a small, asymptomatic myelolipoma?
opts: Conservative observation, since it is benign and non-functioning
mcqs.exp: Small, asymptomatic myelolipomas are managed conservatively because they are benign. Resection is reserved for large (often >4–6 cm), symptomatic, growing, or hemorrhage-prone lesions. A brief hormonal screen is still part of the incidentaloma work-up.
mcqs.hint: Benign incidental fat lesion — observe.

mcqs.q: Which imaging feature would prompt reconsideration of the benign myelolipoma diagnosis?
opts: A large enhancing solid component out of proportion to fat, rapid growth, calcification with nodularity, or invasion — raising concern for a rare fat-containing malignancy
mcqs.exp: While macroscopic fat usually confirms a benign myelolipoma, a disproportionate enhancing solid mass, rapid growth, aggressive calcification/nodularity, or local invasion should raise concern for a rare fat-containing malignancy (e.g., ACC or liposarcoma) and prompt further evaluation.
mcqs.hint: Aggressive enhancing/invasive features override the reassurance of fat.

title: Bilateral Pheochromocytoma in MEN2
caption: Bilateral adrenal pheochromocytoma in a 27-year-old woman with multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 (MEN2). Axial T2-weighted fat-saturated (a), and 3D fat-suppressed T1-weighted gradient-recalled-echo images before (b) and after (c) contrast injection show a bilateral adrenal mass with high T2 signal intensity and strong, heterogeneous contrast enhancement. The signal intensity is typically more inhomogeneous in larger lesions (white arrow, right adrenal gland) than in smaller ones (black arrow, left adrenal gland).

mcqs.q: Bilateral adrenal masses with high T2 signal and strong enhancement in a 27-year-old with MEN2 are most consistent with:
mcqs.exp: MEN2 (RET mutation) predisposes to pheochromocytoma, which is frequently BILATERAL in hereditary syndromes. High T2 signal with avid heterogeneous enhancement is the classic MRI appearance of pheochromocytoma.
mcqs.hint: MEN2 + bilateral T2-bright enhancing adrenal masses = pheos.

mcqs.q: Why are pheochromocytomas particularly likely to be BILATERAL in this patient?
opts: Because a hereditary syndrome (MEN2) predisposes to multifocal/bilateral disease
mcqs.exp: Hereditary pheochromocytoma syndromes (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx) are associated with bilateral and multifocal tumors, often at a younger age. Recognizing bilaterality should prompt genetic evaluation and syndrome-directed screening.
mcqs.hint: Inherited syndromes drive bilateral, younger-onset tumors.

mcqs.q: The larger (right) lesion is more inhomogeneous than the smaller (left) one. Why do larger pheochromocytomas look more heterogeneous?
opts: Larger tumors develop cystic/necrotic and hemorrhagic areas, producing heterogeneous signal and enhancement
mcqs.exp: As pheochromocytomas enlarge, they outgrow their blood supply and develop cystic, necrotic, and hemorrhagic areas, making signal and enhancement more heterogeneous. Smaller lesions appear more homogeneous.
mcqs.hint: Bigger tumors necrose and bleed, so they look patchier.

mcqs.q: Before any surgery or biopsy of these pheochromocytomas, what preparation is essential?
opts: Biochemical confirmation (metanephrines) and preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade (then beta-blockade) to prevent hypertensive crisis
mcqs.exp: Catecholamine excess must be confirmed biochemically and controlled with alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine/doxazosin), followed by beta-blockade if needed, to prevent an intraoperative catecholamine/hypertensive crisis. Biopsy is avoided.
mcqs.hint: Confirm metanephrines and block alpha-receptors first.

mcqs.q: In MEN2, which surgical consideration applies to bilateral adrenal pheochromocytomas?
opts: Always total bilateral adrenalectomy, accepting lifelong steroid dependence, is mandatory
Cortical-sparing (subtotal) adrenalectomy may be considered to preserve adrenal function while removing tumor, balancing recurrence risk against avoiding permanent adrenal insufficiency
Radiotherapy or chemotherapy alone, without surgery
mcqs.exp: In hereditary bilateral pheochromocytoma, cortical-sparing adrenalectomy is often considered to preserve enough cortex to avoid lifelong glucocorticoid dependence and adrenal insufficiency, accepting a small risk of ipsilateral recurrence. Management is individualized with genetic and endocrine input.
mcqs.hint: Spare cortex when possible to avoid lifelong steroid dependence.

title: Right Adrenal Hemangioma – Peripheral Centripetal Enhancement
caption: Right adrenal hemangioma in a 49-year-old woman. Coronal reformatted CT images before (a) and after contrast in arterial (b) and late phase (c) show a well-encapsulated large right adrenal lesion (arrow) with peripheral contrast enhancement, progressive centripetal filling, and a hypodense center.

mcqs.q: A well-encapsulated adrenal lesion with peripheral enhancement and progressive centripetal filling is characteristic of:
mcqs.exp: Peripheral nodular enhancement with progressive centripetal (outside-in) fill-in on delayed phases is the hallmark of a cavernous hemangioma, mirroring the pattern of hepatic hemangiomas. In the adrenal it indicates a rare benign vascular tumor.
mcqs.hint: Fills in from the outside toward the center over time — like a liver hemangioma.

mcqs.q: The lesion retains a hypodense center despite delayed enhancement. What does this typically represent?
opts: A central scar or thrombosed/fibrotic area that fills slowly or not at all
mcqs.exp: Hemangiomas often have a central non-enhancing region (fibrosis, thrombosis, or a scar) that fills slowly or remains hypodense even on delayed images, consistent with the slow-flow vascular architecture. Phleboliths/calcification may also be present.
mcqs.hint: A slow-filling fibrotic/thrombosed core stays dark.

mcqs.q: Why might an adrenal hemangioma still be resected despite being benign?
opts: Its imaging can be indeterminate and larger lesions may hemorrhage, so resection provides diagnosis and prevents complications
mcqs.exp: Because features can overlap with other masses and large hemangiomas carry a risk of hemorrhage, resection is often undertaken for definitive diagnosis and to prevent bleeding, particularly in large or symptomatic lesions.
mcqs.hint: Uncertain imaging plus bleeding risk → often removed.

title: Right Adrenocortical Carcinoma – Necrosis & Hemorrhage
caption: Right adrenocortical carcinoma in a 70-year-old woman admitted with atraumatic abdominal pain. US (a) shows a large heterogeneous right adrenal mass (arrow). Axial T2-weighted spin echo (b), 3D fat-suppressed T1-weighted gradient-recalled-echo arterial phase (d) images confirm a large mass with necrotic and hemorrhagic components — high T2 signal, high T1 signal (hemorrhage), heterogeneous peripheral enhancement, and non-enhancing central areas. Relative percentage washout was reported as less than 40%.

mcqs.q: A large heterogeneous adrenal mass with necrosis, hemorrhage, and heterogeneous peripheral enhancement is most concerning for:
mcqs.exp: Large size, heterogeneity, central necrosis, hemorrhage, and heterogeneous enhancement are hallmarks of adrenocortical carcinoma. Benign adenomas are typically small and homogeneous, so this constellation strongly favors malignancy.
mcqs.hint: Big, necrotic, hemorrhagic, heterogeneous = malignant (ACC).

mcqs.q: High T1 signal within this mass most likely represents:
mcqs.exp: Intrinsic high T1 signal in a solid adrenal mass usually reflects hemorrhage (methemoglobin), common in aggressive tumors like ACC. Macroscopic fat is also T1-bright but would suppress on fat-saturated sequences, which hemorrhage does not.
mcqs.hint: Bright T1 that doesn't suppress on fat-sat = blood, not fat.

mcqs.q: The relative percentage washout was less than 40%. How does that affect characterization?
opts: Low washout (RPW <40%) fails the adenoma criterion, so the mass is NOT a benign adenoma and remains suspicious for malignancy
mcqs.exp: Adenomas require RPW ≥40% (or APW ≥60%). An RPW <40% is non-adenomatous; combined with size, necrosis, and hemorrhage, it supports a malignant lesion such as ACC. (Pheochromocytoma can also show low washout and must be excluded.)
mcqs.hint: RPW under 40% doesn't meet adenoma criteria — stays suspicious.

mcqs.q: The central areas do NOT enhance while the periphery does. What does this pattern indicate?
opts: Central necrosis (non-viable) with viable, vascularized enhancing tumor at the periphery
mcqs.exp: Non-enhancing central regions represent necrosis (outgrown blood supply), while enhancing peripheral tissue is viable tumor. This peripheral-viable/central-necrotic pattern is typical of large aggressive malignancies like ACC.
mcqs.hint: Dead center, living rim — necrotic core of an aggressive tumor.

mcqs.q: What is the appropriate management of this suspected adrenocortical carcinoma?
opts: Biochemical evaluation (including exclusion of pheochromocytoma and assessment for hormone hypersecretion), staging, and surgical resection with histology
mcqs.exp: Suspected ACC requires hormonal work-up (cortisol, androgens/estrogens, aldosterone; and exclusion of pheochromocytoma), staging for local invasion/venous thrombus and metastases, and complete surgical resection with histopathologic confirmation.
mcqs.hint: Screen hormones, exclude pheo, stage, and resect.

title: Left Adrenal Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma with Bowel Involvement
caption: Left adrenal lymphomatous lesion in a 70-year-old man with non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Coronal reformatted CT before (a) and after contrast in portal phase (b) show a large left adrenal mass (arrow) with inhomogeneous enhancement. Axial CT of the lower abdomen in portal phase (c) shows gastrointestinal involvement of lymphoma as aneurysmal dilatation of some loops of the small bowel.

mcqs.q: A large left adrenal mass with inhomogeneous enhancement in a patient with known non-Hodgkin lymphoma most likely represents:
mcqs.exp: In a patient with known non-Hodgkin lymphoma, a soft-tissue adrenal mass most likely reflects secondary adrenal involvement. Adrenal lymphoma is usually a homogeneous-to-inhomogeneous soft-tissue mass and is more often secondary than primary.
mcqs.hint: Known NHL + adrenal soft-tissue mass = spread to the gland.

mcqs.q: Panel c shows 'aneurysmal dilatation' of small-bowel loops from lymphomatous involvement. Why does GI lymphoma classically produce aneurysmal dilatation rather than obstruction?
opts: Because lymphoma infiltrates and destroys the bowel wall/myenteric plexus, causing the lumen to dilate (aneurysmal) rather than stricture
mcqs.exp: Lymphoma infiltrates the bowel wall and destroys the muscularis/autonomic plexus, so instead of causing a stricture the affected segment dilates — the classic 'aneurysmal dilatation' of small-bowel lymphoma. This helps distinguish it from adenocarcinoma, which tends to obstruct.
mcqs.hint: It eats the muscle layer, so the bowel balloons out rather than narrowing.

mcqs.q: How can adrenal lymphoma be distinguished from a lipid-rich adenoma on imaging?
opts: Lymphoma is solid soft tissue (usually >10 HU, FDG-avid, restricted diffusion) without opposed-phase signal drop, whereas a lipid-rich adenoma is ≤10 HU with opposed-phase signal loss
mcqs.exp: A lipid-rich adenoma measures ≤10 HU and drops signal on opposed-phase MRI. Lymphoma is solid (typically >10 HU), FDG-avid, shows restricted diffusion, and lacks intracytoplasmic lipid signal drop.
mcqs.hint: Adenoma = low HU + opposed-phase dropout; lymphoma = solid, PET-hot, no dropout.

mcqs.q: What is the appropriate work-up to confirm adrenal (and bowel) lymphomatous involvement?
opts: Correlate with the known lymphoma and use FDG-PET (lymphoma is avid) and/or biopsy, after excluding pheochromocytoma before any biopsy
mcqs.exp: FDG-PET (lymphoma is highly avid) and/or image-guided biopsy confirm involvement and guide chemotherapy. As always, pheochromocytoma is biochemically excluded before biopsy of an adrenal mass.
mcqs.hint: PET and/or biopsy in the context of known lymphoma; exclude pheo first.

mcqs.q: In extensive or bilateral adrenal lymphoma, which endocrine complication should be monitored?
opts: Primary adrenal insufficiency from gland replacement
mcqs.exp: Extensive adrenal replacement by lymphoma (especially bilateral) can destroy enough cortex to cause primary adrenal insufficiency. Awareness and monitoring of adrenal function during treatment is important.
mcqs.hint: Enough tumor replacing cortex can cause adrenal failure.

title: Left Adrenal Metastasis from Lung Cancer – PET Follow-up
caption: Left adrenal metastasis in a 62-year-old woman with lung cancer. Unenhanced (a), portal (b), and late-phase (c) CT show a left adrenal mass (arrow) with attenuation 26 HU on unenhanced CT and an absolute percentage washout of 15%. Staging ¹⁸F-FDG-PET/CT (d) revealed a left adrenal lesion with high metabolic activity (SUVmax 14 g/mL) that increased in size compared with CT. After chemotherapy, restaging ¹⁸F-FDG-PET/CT (e) showed morpho-functional disease progression with central necrosis (SUVmax 19 g/mL) and a central necrotic area of absent FDG uptake.

mcqs.q: A left adrenal mass measuring 26 HU unenhanced with an absolute percentage washout of only 15% in a lung-cancer patient is best classified as:
opts: A non-adenoma / suspicious lesion, since 26 HU is >10 HU and APW 15% is well below the 60% adenoma threshold
mcqs.exp: The lesion is lipid-poor (26 HU >10 HU) and shows poor washout (APW 15% << 60%), so it fails adenoma criteria. In a patient with lung cancer this is suspicious for metastasis and needs confirmation.
mcqs.hint: 26 HU and 15% washout both fail the adenoma rules.

mcqs.q: Staging FDG-PET showed the adrenal lesion with SUVmax 14 and interval size increase. How does this contribute?
opts: High FDG avidity plus growth confirms a metabolically active malignant lesion (metastasis)
mcqs.exp: An SUVmax of 14 (well above liver) with interval enlargement indicates a hypermetabolic, growing malignant lesion — a metastasis in this lung-cancer patient. FDG-PET both characterizes the lesion and stages disease.
mcqs.hint: Very FDG-avid and growing = active metastasis.

mcqs.q: On restaging after chemotherapy, SUVmax rose to 19 with a central necrotic area of ABSENT FDG uptake. What does the central photopenia represent?
opts: Central necrosis (non-viable tissue) within a progressing tumor whose viable portions remain highly FDG-avid
mcqs.exp: A central area lacking FDG uptake represents necrosis (no viable metabolically active cells), while rising peripheral SUVmax indicates viable, progressing tumor. Overall this is morpho-functional disease PROGRESSION despite therapy.
mcqs.hint: Dead necrotic center is cold; the live rim is hotter — the tumor progressed.

mcqs.q: Taken together, how do the CT and PET findings illustrate a complementary characterization strategy?
opts: CT established the lesion was lipid-poor with poor washout (non-adenoma), and PET confirmed malignancy and tracked treatment response/progression — anatomic plus functional information
mcqs.exp: CT provided anatomic characterization (lipid-poor, low washout → not an adenoma), and FDG-PET added functional data confirming malignancy and monitoring response. Combining anatomic and functional imaging is powerful for indeterminate adrenal masses in cancer patients.
mcqs.hint: CT says 'not an adenoma'; PET says 'malignant and progressing.'

mcqs.q: Why is correct characterization of this adrenal lesion important for the patient's lung-cancer management?
opts: An adrenal metastasis upstages the disease and changes treatment (systemic therapy vs. potentially curative local therapy), so misclassification would alter prognosis and management
mcqs.exp: An adrenal metastasis indicates stage IV disease, shifting management from potentially curative local treatment toward systemic therapy. Accurate characterization (washout CT, PET, biopsy) is therefore critical for correct staging and treatment decisions.
mcqs.hint: Metastasis means stage IV — it changes the whole treatment plan.

title: Left Adrenal Metastasis from Breast Cancer – Hemorrhagic
caption: Left adrenal metastasis in a 58-year-old woman with breast cancer. Axial unenhanced CT (a), portal-phase CT (b), T2-weighted fast spin echo (c), b800 diffusion-weighted image (d), ADC map (e), and coronal 3D fat-suppressed T1-weighted gradient-recalled-echo portal-phase post-contrast image (f) show a left adrenal mass (arrow). The lesion shows intralesional hemorrhage (spontaneously hyperdense area on unenhanced CT), hypointensity on T2, and no enhancement on CT or MR; the peripheral solid component shows a restricted pattern of diffusion.

mcqs.q: A spontaneously HYPERDENSE area on unenhanced CT within this adrenal mass indicates:
mcqs.exp: Spontaneous high attenuation on unenhanced CT indicates intralesional hemorrhage (acute/subacute blood). Metastases — including from breast cancer — can hemorrhage, producing hyperdense areas and complicating characterization.
mcqs.hint: High density before contrast = blood within the lesion.

mcqs.q: The peripheral solid component shows RESTRICTED diffusion (bright on b800 DWI, low ADC). What does this suggest?
opts: Hypercellular viable tumor tissue at the periphery
mcqs.exp: Restricted diffusion (high DWI signal with low ADC) reflects dense cellularity, indicating viable solid tumor at the periphery. This helps identify the metastatic solid component amid hemorrhage and necrosis.
mcqs.hint: Bright DWI + low ADC = packed cells = viable tumor.

mcqs.q: The lesion shows NO enhancement on CT or MR yet is a metastasis. How is this reconciled?
opts: Much of the lesion is hemorrhagic/necrotic (non-enhancing), while the viable solid component (shown by restricted diffusion) is the true tumor — enhancement can be masked or minimal in a largely hemorrhagic metastasis
mcqs.exp: A predominantly hemorrhagic/necrotic metastasis may show little internal enhancement because much of it is non-viable clot; the viable tumor is identified by restricted diffusion (and any subtle peripheral enhancement). Diffusion imaging is therefore valuable when enhancement is equivocal.
mcqs.hint: Mostly clot (non-enhancing), but DWI reveals the living tumor.

mcqs.q: Hypointensity on T2 in a hemorrhagic lesion is explained by which factor?
opts: Blood-product/hemosiderin and deoxyhemoglobin causing T2 shortening (low signal)
mcqs.exp: Blood products such as deoxyhemoglobin and hemosiderin shorten T2 and produce low T2 signal. In this hemorrhagic metastasis, T2 hypointensity reflects blood products rather than the bright T2 typical of simple fluid or many solid tumors.
mcqs.hint: Blood-breakdown products darken T2.

mcqs.q: In a breast-cancer patient, what makes diffusion-weighted MRI especially useful for this hemorrhagic adrenal mass?
opts: When hemorrhage/necrosis mask enhancement, DWI can still detect the viable hypercellular tumor component, aiding characterization and confirming metastasis
mcqs.exp: In hemorrhagic or necrotic lesions where enhancement is equivocal or absent, DWI highlights the viable, hypercellular tumor by restricted diffusion — helping confirm a metastasis. It complements CT attenuation and washout, especially when contrast findings are inconclusive.
mcqs.hint: DWI finds the living tumor when enhancement is hidden by blood.
